eras.label: In the Beginning
eras.worldContext: Before history began, God created everything from nothing.

eras.label: The Age of the Patriarchs
eras.worldContext: The world's first great cities rose in Mesopotamia. Egypt ruled the ancient world. And God chose one family to change everything.

eras.label: The Exodus and Wilderness
eras.worldContext: Egypt was the superpower of the ancient world, with pharaohs who claimed to be gods. God was about to prove otherwise.

eras.label: The Promised Land
eras.worldContext: Israel entered Canaan, a land of walled cities and powerful kings. The question was whether they would trust God, or try to do it themselves.

eras.label: The Kingdom Era
eras.worldContext: Israel demanded a king. They got three: one who started well and ended badly, one after God's own heart, and one who built the Temple but lost the kingdom.

eras.label: Exile and Return
eras.worldContext: Babylon destroyed Jerusalem and took God's people captive. But exile is never the end of God's story.

eras.label: The Life of Jesus
eras.worldContext: Rome controlled the world. Religious leaders controlled the Temple. And into that world, God himself was born in a barn.

eras.label: Death, Resurrection and the Early Church
eras.worldContext: Twelve ordinary people watched Jesus rise from the dead and told them to go change the world. Somehow, they did.

stories.title: In the Beginning
stories.hook: Before space, time, or matter existed, God was already there. Then He spoke, and everything began.
story: Before time started, before anything existed at all, God was already there. And God created, the sky, the earth, everything. At first the earth was a dark, empty, formless place, with water covering everything and God's Spirit moving over it all like wind over the surface of the deep.
Then God spoke. "Light!" And light blazed into existence. God looked at the light and loved it. He separated it from the darkness and named them, Day and Night. That was day one.
God spoke again. "Sky!" And a great open space spread out between the waters, pushing them apart, water below, water above, and sky stretching between them. That was day two.

God spoke again. "Water, gather together, and let dry ground appear." And it happened, just like that. He called the dry ground Earth and the gathered waters Seas, and He looked at what He'd made and loved it. Then He said, "Earth, grow things, every kind of plant and tree, loaded with seeds and fruit." And the ground erupted with growing things. God looked at it and loved it. That was day three.

God spoke again. "Lights in the sky, to mark the seasons, the days, the years, to separate day from night." And there they were, the great blazing sun to rule the day, the silver moon to rule the night, and stars beyond counting scattered across the darkness. God looked at all of it and loved what He saw. That was day four.

Then the seas came alive. God spoke and the water filled up with creatures, everything that swims and darts and glides through the deep. Birds lifted off and wheeled through the sky. God looked at what He'd made and loved it. He blessed them: "Have children. Fill the seas. Fill the sky." That was day five.
Then land animals, every kind of creature that walks and crawls and runs across the earth. God looked at all of it and loved it.

Then God said something He hadn't said before. "Let us make human beings, someone like us, made in our image." And He did. He made them male and female, and He looked at them and loved them. He blessed them and said, "Have children and fill the earth. Take care of it. You're in charge of every fish, every bird, every living thing." Then God stepped back and looked at everything He had made, and it was very good. Not just good. Very good. That was day six.
Day seven, God rested. He had finished everything He set out to do, and He rested and called the day holy.

Then the story gets up close. God took dust from the ground and shaped it into a man, and He bent down and breathed into his nostrils, and the man came alive. God planted a garden in Eden, full of beautiful trees loaded with fruit, and He put the man there to tend it and take care of it.

Then God said, "It isn't good for the man to be alone. I'm going to make someone who is exactly right for him." God caused the man to fall into a deep sleep, and while he slept He took one of his ribs and shaped it into a woman, and He brought her to the man. The man woke up and saw her and said, "Finally, bone of my bone, flesh of my flesh." And the two of them were completely at home with each other, no hiding, no shame, nothing between them at all.

devotional.bigIdea: God made everything, including you, on purpose. You are the last and most important thing He made, created in His own image, looked at with love, and called very good.
questions: God said everything was good all week, but when He made people He said it was very good. Why do you think He used different words?
God rested on the seventh day, not because He was tired, but because the work was done. What do you think it means to rest the way God rested?
God breathed His own breath into the man. He didn't do that for any animal or any tree. What does that tell you about what makes people different from everything else God made?

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: John opens his Gospel the same way Genesis does, 'In the beginning', and says Jesus was there for all of it, the Word through whom everything was made.

connections.title: Impossible News
connections.why: The same Spirit who hovered over the waters at creation overshadowed Mary at the Incarnation. God was making something new, again.

connections.title: The Empty Tomb
connections.why: Paul calls anyone in Christ a 'new creation', the same language, the same God, making all things new again.

quiz.question: What were the very first words God spoke to create the world?
options: Let there be light
Let there be earth
Let there be life

quiz.question: What did God do on the seventh day?
options: Made the sun and moon
Rested and called the day holy
Created the animals

quiz.question: How did God make the man come alive?
options: He spoke his name
He touched him with fire
He breathed into his nostrils

quiz.question: What did God say about everything He had made at the end of the sixth day?
options: It was very good
It was finished
It was holy
It was good enough
quiz.explanation: After creating everything, including humans, God looked at all He had made and called it very good. Not just good, but very good.

quiz.question: What did God use to make the first woman?
options: Breath from His own mouth
Dust from the ground
Clay from the riverbank
A rib from the man
quiz.explanation: God caused the man to fall into a deep sleep and took one of his ribs, forming the woman, showing that she is of the same nature as the man.

stories.title: The First Bad Choice
stories.hook: They had everything. One rule. And a voice that made them wonder whether God was holding something back.
story: The garden was perfect. Adam and Eve had everything, beautiful trees, clear rivers, good food, and God Himself walking with them in the cool of the evening like a close friend. And God had given them just one rule: don't eat from the tree in the middle of the garden. "The day you eat from it," He told them, "you will certainly die."

Then a serpent showed up, the craftiest creature in the garden. He didn't come with a threat. He came with a question. "Did God really say you can't eat from any tree in the garden?" He twisted the words just slightly, making one rule sound like all the rules.

Eve corrected him: they could eat from every tree except that one. But the serpent pressed harder. "You won't die. God knows that when you eat it your eyes will be opened and you'll be like Him, knowing good and evil." He was offering her a reason to distrust the One who had given her everything.
She looked at the fruit. It looked good. It looked like it would make her wise. She took it and ate it, and gave some to Adam, who was standing right there the whole time, and he ate it too.

Everything changed the moment they swallowed it. Not in the way the serpent had promised, they didn't feel powerful or wise. They felt exposed. Ashamed. They grabbed leaves and made clothes because suddenly they couldn't stand the thought of being seen. And when they heard God walking in the garden that evening, instead of running toward Him the way they always had, they hid.

"Where are you?" God called. He knew exactly where they were. He was giving them a chance to come out. Adam answered from the shadows: "I heard you in the garden, and I was afraid because I was naked, so I hid." God asked the real question: "Did you eat from the tree I told you not to eat from?"

Adam blamed Eve. Eve blamed the serpent. Nobody admitted what they'd done. And the consequences were real, pain, hard work, broken relationships, and eventually death. The ground that had given everything freely would now push back with thorns and sweat.

But before God sent them out of the garden, He made something for them, garments of skin, real coverings, not the flimsy leaves they'd grabbed for themselves. An animal died. God provided the covering. And He made a promise to the serpent that was actually the first piece of good news the world had ever heard: one day, a descendant of the woman would crush the serpent's head. The rescue was already on its way.

devotional.bigIdea: Temptation almost never looks like evil. It usually looks like a reasonable question about whether God can really be trusted. The answer, from the garden to today, is always yes.
questions: The serpent didn't lie outright, he twisted the truth just slightly. Why do you think a half-truth can be more dangerous than a full lie?
Adam was standing right there and said nothing. What do you think was going through his mind? What would you have done?
Even after the worst moment in human history, God made them clothing with His own hands. What does that tell you about who God is when we mess up?

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Paul says sin and death entered the world through one man's choice in this garden. And life came back through one Man's choice at the cross.

connections.title: The Boy Who Came Home
connections.why: Just like the prodigal son, Adam and Eve 'came to themselves' too late, and discovered that coming back to the Father is always possible.

connections.title: In the Beginning
connections.why: God's promise to crush the serpent's head is the first announcement of the gospel, the whole Bible is the story of that promise being kept.

quiz.question: What was the one rule God gave Adam and Eve in the garden?
options: Do not talk to animals
Do not leave the garden
Do not eat from the tree in the middle of the garden

quiz.question: What did the serpent tell Eve would happen if she ate the fruit?
options: Her eyes would be opened and she would be like God
She would get sick and die
She would become very strong

quiz.question: What did God make for Adam and Eve before sending them out of the garden?
options: A boat to travel in
Garments of skin to clothe them
A new home

quiz.question: What did Adam and Eve do immediately after eating the fruit?
options: They hid from each other
They ran from the garden
They fell asleep
They sewed fig leaves together to cover themselves
quiz.explanation: Their eyes were opened and they realized they were naked, so they sewed fig leaves together. Shame entered the world.

quiz.question: What did God promise would happen between the serpent and the woman's offspring?
options: The serpent would be banished underground
They would never meet again
The offspring would crush the serpent's head
They would live in peace
quiz.explanation: God told the serpent that the woman's offspring would crush his head, the first hint in Scripture of Jesus defeating Satan.

stories.title: Two Brothers
stories.hook: The world's first brothers. The world's first act of worship. And the world's first murder, all in one chapter.
story: Adam and Eve had children, and their firstborn was Cain, a farmer who worked the ground. Their second son was Abel, a shepherd who kept flocks. Two brothers, two different kinds of work, and one God they both tried to worship.

When the time came to bring offerings to God, Cain brought some of his crops, just some, the text says, without much detail. Abel brought the firstborn of his flock, the best ones, the fat portions. The difference wasn't crops versus animals. It was the heart behind the gift. Abel gave his absolute best. Cain brought whatever he had lying around.
God accepted Abel's offering. He didn't accept Cain's. And Cain's face fell, not with regret, not with a desire to do better, but with anger. Pure, hot anger.

God came to Cain right then, before anything terrible had happened, and offered him a way out. "Why are you angry? If you do what is right, won't you be accepted? But if you don't, sin is crouching at the door, it wants you, but you have to master it." God was warning him. He was coaching him. The choice was still Cain's to make.

Cain didn't listen. He said to his brother, "Let's go out to the field." And when they got there, Cain attacked Abel and killed him. The first death in human history. The first murder in human history. Between two brothers.

God came again. "Where is your brother Abel?" Cain's answer has echoed ever since: "I don't know. Am I my brother's keeper?" He lied. He deflected. But the ground had already told God what had happened, Abel's blood crying out from the soil.

Cain was sent away, a wanderer, cut off from the land he'd worked his whole life. He cried out that the punishment was more than he could bear. And even then, even for a murderer, God placed a mark on Cain to protect him. Nobody would kill him. Even Cain was not abandoned.

This story asks a question that never gets old: am I my brother's keeper? The whole rest of the Bible answers it. Yes. We are responsible for each other. And unaddressed anger, left alone and fed, always ends up somewhere it was never supposed to go.

devotional.bigIdea: God warned Cain before the worst moment, and He warns us too. Sin crouching at the door hasn't changed. But neither has God's word: you can master it.
questions: God came to Cain personally and warned him before anything irreversible happened. Why do you think Cain still went ahead with it?
Abel gave his best. Cain gave whatever. What does it look like to give God your best in everyday life, not just at church?
Even after murdering his brother, Cain received God's protection. Does that surprise you? What does that tell you about God's mercy?

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Jesus points back to exactly this kind of anger in the Sermon on the Mount, murder starts in the heart, long before the hands get involved.

connections.title: The First Bad Choice
connections.why: Sin crouching at Cain's door is the direct result of what happened in the garden. The consequence of the fall played out in the very next generation.

connections.title: The Boy Who Came Home
connections.why: John uses Cain as the opposite of love, the example of what it looks like when jealousy and hatred are allowed to grow unchecked.

quiz.question: What was different about the offerings Cain and Abel brought to God?
options: Cain brought more than Abel did
Abel brought his very best while Cain just brought some
Abel brought crops and Cain brought animals

quiz.question: What did God warn Cain about before anything bad happened?
options: That the harvest would fail
That Abel would leave the family
That sin was crouching at the door waiting to control him

quiz.question: What did God do for Cain even after he murdered his brother?
options: Placed a mark on him to protect him
Gave him a new home
Forgave him immediately

quiz.question: Where did God send Cain after he murdered Abel?
options: To serve as a slave in a distant land
Into the wilderness with no food
To live as a wanderer east of Eden
Back to his father Adam
quiz.explanation: God sent Cain away to live as a restless wanderer east of Eden, cut off from the ground he had worked and from God's presence.

quiz.question: What question did Cain ask God that has echoed through history?
options: Why did you not accept my offering?
Am I my brother's keeper?
Where has Abel gone?
Will you forgive what I have done?
quiz.explanation: "Am I my brother's keeper?", Cain's deflection has become one of the most famous lines in all of Scripture, exposing how quickly sin hardens the heart.

stories.title: The Ark and the Rainbow
stories.hook: In a generation that had forgotten God entirely, one man still walked with Him. Then God asked him to build something that had never been built before.
story: The world had gone badly wrong. Not in small ways, all the way wrong. God looked down at what people had become and what He saw broke His heart. Every thought, every plan, every desire, twisted toward evil, all day, every day. The world He had made and called very good had become something He grieved over.

But there was one man who was different. His name was Noah. In a whole generation that had turned its back on God, Noah walked with Him. That's how the Bible puts it, he walked with God, the same way Enoch had before him, close and faithful, day after day. God saw it.

So God came to Noah and told him what was coming, a flood, unlike anything the world had ever seen, that would cover everything. And He gave Noah a job: build an ark. Not a boat. An ark, enormous, three decks tall, longer than a football field and a half, sealed inside and out with pitch. Load it with your family and two of every kind of creature that breathes air. Because everything else is going to be covered.

Noah did it. All of it, exactly as God said. He spent decades swinging an axe and hauling timber in the middle of dry land while his neighbors watched and wondered what on earth he was doing. No rain. No flood. No sign at all that any of this made sense. And Noah kept building.

When the ark was finished, the animals came, two by two, every kind, just as God had said. Noah and his wife, his sons and their wives, eight people in all, walked up the ramp. And then God Himself shut the door.

The rain started. Not just rain, the skies opened and the underground springs burst upward at the same time, and the water rose and rose until even the highest mountains were covered. Everything that breathed on dry land died. Only the ark floated on, exactly as God had designed it.

For five months the water covered the earth. Then God remembered Noah, and a wind began to blow, and slowly the water started going down. The ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat, in what is now eastern Turkey. Noah waited. He sent out a raven, then a dove. The dove came back the first time with nothing. The second time with a fresh olive leaf. The third time she didn't come back at all, and Noah knew the earth was ready.

They stepped out of the ark into a silent, washed world. And the first thing Noah did was build an altar and worship. God received the offering and made a promise He has kept every single day since: never again. Never again a flood to destroy all life. And He put a rainbow in the clouds as the sign of that promise, not a symbol people invented and gave religious meaning to, but a covenant God placed there Himself. He said: whenever the rainbow appears, I will see it and remember.
Every rainbow you have ever seen is God keeping His word. That is what kind of God He is.

devotional.bigIdea: Noah built for decades without seeing a single drop of rain, because he trusted the One who was asking. Every rainbow since is proof that God keeps every promise He makes.
questions: Noah built for years without any sign that he was right to do it. What do you think kept him going when nothing around him made sense?
God shut the door of the ark Himself, Noah didn't have to decide when it was time. Is there something in your life right now where you need to trust God with the timing?
Every rainbow is a promise God made thousands of years ago that He is still keeping today. What does that tell you about the promises He has made to you?

connections.title: The Empty Tomb
connections.why: Peter says the ark and the flood pointed forward to baptism, being carried safely through water into new life in Christ.

connections.title: A Promise as Big as the Stars
connections.why: The rainbow covenant with Noah is the first of God's great covenants. The same God who promised Noah will promise Abraham, Moses, David, and ultimately all of us through Jesus.

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Jesus Himself refers to the days of Noah, people carrying on as normal, completely unaware, right up until the moment everything changed.

quiz.question: How did Noah respond when God told him to build the ark?
options: He waited to see if it would rain before starting
He argued and asked for a sign first
He did everything exactly as God commanded

quiz.question: Who shut the door of the ark when it was time?
options: The animals pushed it closed
God Himself shut the door
Noah and his sons

quiz.question: What sign did God put in the sky as a promise to never flood the whole earth again?
options: A bright star
A pillar of fire
A rainbow

quiz.question: What did Noah do as soon as he came out of the ark?
options: He built an altar and offered burnt offerings to God
He planted a vineyard
He sent a message to his neighbors
He built a city
quiz.explanation: The first thing Noah did on dry land was build an altar and worship. God responded by promising never again to destroy every living creature.

quiz.question: How long were Noah and his family on the ark?
options: Six months
Three years
Forty days and forty nights
About a year
quiz.explanation: Though the rain fell for forty days, Noah and his family were on the ark for about a year before the earth was dry enough to leave.

stories.title: The Tower of Babel
stories.hook: One language. One people. One enormous building project. And a lesson that every generation since has had to learn again.
story: After the flood, Noah's descendants spread across the earth and the population grew. For a while, everyone still spoke the same language, one people, one tongue, one conversation stretching across the whole human family.

Then a large group settled on a flat plain in Shinar, the region we now call southern Iraq, one of the most fertile places in the ancient world. They figured out how to make bricks from clay and fire them hard, sealing them together with bitumen, the tar that naturally seeps from the ground there. They were good builders. And they had an idea.

"Come on," they said to each other. "Let's build a city with a tower that reaches all the way to heaven. We'll make a name for ourselves, and we won't get scattered all over the earth." Every word of that plan matters. Build for ourselves. A name for ourselves. And don't get scattered, which was the exact opposite of what God had told them to do.

God looked down at what they were building. The text has a quiet humor to it, the God who holds the universe in His hands had to come down just to see their tower. And what He saw wasn't just a construction project. It was a heart problem. People unified around their own glory, convinced they didn't need anyone above them, building toward heaven on their own terms.

So God confused their language. Overnight, workers who had spoken the same words all their lives suddenly couldn't understand each other. The construction stopped. Crews broke apart along the lines of who could still communicate. Families who understood each other gathered together and moved away, and the scattering they had been trying to prevent happened anyway, just on God's terms instead of their own.
The city was called Babel, which in Hebrew sounds like the word for confused. They had wanted to build a gate to God. God turned it into a monument to confusion.

But Babel isn't the end of the story. Jump forward thousands of years to the day of Pentecost in Jerusalem, and the Holy Spirit falls on a room full of followers of Jesus, and suddenly every person in the crowd hears the wonders of God in their own native language. What Babel broke apart, the Spirit of God put back together. Not through human ambition, but through the grace of God, gathering people from every nation into one family.

devotional.bigIdea: Babel was never really about a building, it was about the direction of the human heart. Ambition pointed at our own glory always ends in confusion. Ambition surrendered to God builds something that lasts.
questions: The people at Babel were talented and organized, their problem wasn't incompetence but direction. What is the difference between ambition that honors God and ambition that replaces Him?
They said 'let us make a name for ourselves.' What names are people trying to make for themselves today, and what does it cost them?
Babel scattered people. Pentecost put them back together. What does that tell you about God's patience and His plan?

connections.title: The Day Everything Changed
connections.why: Pentecost is the direct reversal of Babel, where God confused languages to scatter, the Spirit united them to gather every nation into one family.

connections.title: A Promise as Big as the Stars
connections.why: Just a few verses after Babel, God calls Abram, and through him begins the long work of blessing every nation that Babel tried to unify by pride.

connections.title: The First Bad Choice
connections.why: The serpent promised 'you will be like God.' At Babel, people tried to make that happen themselves. Same pride, bigger stage.

quiz.question: Why did the people want to build a tower reaching to heaven?
options: To make a name for themselves and not be scattered
To worship God from a high place
To see the whole earth from above

quiz.question: How did God stop the building of the tower?
options: He sent rain to wash it away
He knocked it down with wind
He confused their language so they could not understand each other

quiz.question: What event thousands of years later reversed what happened at Babel?
options: Moses receiving the Ten Commandments
The building of the Temple in Jerusalem
Pentecost when the Holy Spirit let everyone hear in their own language

quiz.question: What were the people of Babel trying to avoid by building the tower?
options: War with neighboring tribes
Being scattered across the earth
Another flood
Being forgotten by future generations
quiz.explanation: They said "let us make a name for ourselves, lest we be dispersed over the face of the whole earth." Ironically, God scattered them anyway.

quiz.question: What does the name "Babel" mean?
options: Confusion
High place
Tower of heaven
Great city
quiz.explanation: Babel means confusion, the city got its name from what God did there when He confused their language.

stories.title: A Promise as Big as the Stars
stories.hook: God told a 75-year-old man to leave everything he had ever known and go somewhere He would show him later. The man went.
story: Abram had a good life. He lived in Ur of the Chaldeans, one of the greatest cities in the ancient world, sitting on the banks of the Euphrates River in what is now southern Iraq. He had a wife named Sarai, a nephew named Lot, flocks and herds and servants and a home that worked. He was comfortable. He was settled. He was seventy-five years old.

Then God spoke to him. And what God said was both simple and terrifying: "Leave your country, your people, and your father's household. Go to the land I will show you." No destination. No map. No timeline. Just go, and I will show you when you get there.

Attached to that call was a promise so large Abram could barely hold it. God would make him into a great nation. He would bless him and make his name great. And through him, through this one man in one ancient city, all peoples on earth would be blessed. Paul would explain it centuries later: the gospel itself was announced in advance to Abraham that day. All nations will be blessed through you.

Abram went. He packed everything and left Ur, the only home he had ever known, without a forwarding address. He traveled north to Haran, and after his father Terah died there, continued south into the land of Canaan. God appeared to him and said: To your offspring I will give this land. Abram built an altar.

Years passed. Then a decade. Then another. God renewed the promise multiple times. Once He walked Abram outside and told him to count the stars. So shall your offspring be. Abram looked up at a sky blazing with light, and believed. The Bible records it plainly: Abram believed the Lord, and He credited it to him as righteousness. Not works. Not religious achievement. Belief. That was enough.

But Sarai could not have children, and she was long past the age of trying. When Abram was ninety-nine, God appeared again and changed his name. Abram, exalted father, became Abraham, meaning father of many nations. Sarai became Sarah. And God told them: within a year, Sarah would have a son. Sarah, listening from the tent, laughed out loud. She was ninety years old. God heard the laugh. Is anything too hard for the Lord? He asked. A year later, they held their son. They named him Isaac, he laughs.

Then came the test that has no equal. God told Abraham to take Isaac, the child of the promise, the miracle son he had waited twenty-five years for, to a mountain in the region of Moriah and sacrifice him there. Abraham rose early the next morning. He saddled his donkey, took his son, cut wood for the fire, and set out. Three days of walking toward something he could not explain. Isaac carried the wood up the hill and asked: Father, where is the lamb? Abraham answered: God himself will provide the lamb, my son.

At the summit, Abraham built the altar, bound his son, and raised the knife. And the angel of the Lord called from heaven: Abraham! Abraham! Stop. I know now that you fear God, because you have not withheld your son. Abraham looked up and saw a ram caught in a thicket. He sacrificed it in place of his son. He named the place The Lord Will Provide, and so it has been called ever since.

The ram caught in the thicket. Bound. Substituted. Dying in the place of the son who should have died. Every generation of parents who has read this story to their children has understood: this is pointing somewhere. A thousand years later, on a hill in the same region, another Son would carry wood up another hill. And this time, there would be no ram.

devotional.bigIdea: Abraham didn't know where he was going. He just knew who was asking. That is the whole definition of faith, not certainty about the destination, but trust in the Guide.
questions: Abraham left everything at seventy-five years old with no map and no destination. What would be hardest for you to leave behind if God asked you to go?
He waited twenty-five years for the promised son. What do you think God was doing in Abraham during those years that He couldn't have done in twenty-five days?
The ram in the thicket died in Isaac's place. Who is the ultimate substitute that story was pointing toward, and what does that mean for you?

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: God gave His own Son, just as He asked Abraham to give his. But this time, no angel stopped it.

connections.title: The Empty Tomb
connections.why: The writer of Hebrews says Abraham believed God could raise Isaac from the dead. That faith pointed straight to the resurrection.

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Paul says the promise to Abraham, all nations will be blessed through you, was the gospel announced in advance. Jesus fulfilled it.

quiz.question: How old was Abram when God told him to leave everything and go?
options: Forty years old
Sixty years old
Seventy-five years old
quiz.explanation: Abram was seventy-five years old, not young, when he trusted God and left everything he knew.

quiz.question: What did God tell Abraham when He took him outside at night?
options: Listen to the wind, that is my voice
Count the stars, so shall your offspring be
Look at the sea, so great will your family be
quiz.explanation: God took Abraham outside and said count the stars, so shall your offspring be. And Abraham believed Him.

quiz.question: What did Abraham find caught in the thicket at the last moment?
options: A ram
A dove
A lamb
quiz.explanation: A ram was caught in the thicket, and Abraham sacrificed it in place of his son Isaac. He named that place The Lord Will Provide.

quiz.question: What did God change Abram's name to, and what does it mean?
options: Isaac, he laughs
Israel, one who wrestles with God
Elohim, God is with us
Abraham, father of many nations
quiz.explanation: God changed his name from Abram to Abraham, meaning "father of many nations", even before Abraham had any children with Sarah.

quiz.question: What was the name of the mountain where Abraham was asked to sacrifice his son?
options: Mount Carmel
Mount Sinai
Mount Moriah
Mount Hermon
quiz.explanation: Mount Moriah, the same mountain range where, centuries later, the Temple would be built in Jerusalem, and where Jesus would be crucified.

stories.title: The Dreamer's Long Road
stories.hook: His brothers sold him into slavery. He spent years in prison for something he did not do. Then he saved the world, and the people who had betrayed him.
story: Jacob had twelve sons, and he made no secret of which one he loved most. Joseph was the favorite, and everyone knew it. The richly ornamented coat his father gave him was a daily reminder. His ten older brothers noticed. They hated Joseph for it, and the hatred grew worse when he started telling them his dreams.

He dreamed that their sheaves of grain bowed down to his. He told them. He dreamed that the sun and the moon and eleven stars were bowing before him. He told them again. Are you actually going to rule over us? they demanded. They hated him more for his dreams than for anything else.

One day, far from home near Dothan, the brothers saw their chance. They grabbed Joseph, stripped off his coat, and threw him into an empty cistern. Then they sat down to eat. A caravan of traders appeared on the road heading to Egypt, and Judah had an idea. Why kill him? Let's sell him. Twenty pieces of silver later, Joseph was on his way to Egypt. The brothers dipped the coat in goat's blood and brought it to their father. Jacob mourned for years.

In Egypt, Joseph was sold to a man named Potiphar, the captain of Pharaoh's guard. The Lord was with Joseph, that phrase runs through his story like a thread, and everything he touched prospered. Potiphar put him in charge of everything. Then Potiphar's wife falsely accused him, and Joseph ended up in prison. He had done everything right and still ended up in a cell.

In prison, the Lord was still with him. Two of Pharaoh's officials had disturbing dreams the same night. Joseph interpreted them perfectly: the cupbearer would be restored in three days, the baker would be executed. Both came true exactly as he said. He asked the cupbearer one thing: remember me when you get out. The cupbearer walked out into the sunlight, and forgot about Joseph for two full years.

Then Pharaoh had two dreams that nobody could interpret. Seven fat cows devoured by seven thin ones. Seven healthy heads of grain swallowed by seven scorched ones. The cupbearer finally remembered: there is a Hebrew man in prison. Joseph was brought out, cleaned up, and stood before the most powerful man in the ancient world. He interpreted the dreams without hesitation: seven years of abundance, followed by seven years of famine. Appoint a wise man to store food now. Pharaoh looked at his officials and said: can we find anyone in whom is the spirit of God like this man? He made Joseph second-in-command of all Egypt. Joseph went from prison to palace in a single day. He was thirty years old.

When the famine came and spread across the ancient Near East, Joseph's brothers traveled to Egypt to buy grain. They stood before the governor and bowed down with their faces to the ground. Joseph recognized them immediately. They had no idea who he was. The dream about the sheaves had come true in a way none of them could have imagined.

He tested them across multiple visits, not for revenge, but to see if they had changed. When he saw that Judah, who had proposed the sale, was now willing to sacrifice himself to protect the youngest brother Benjamin, Joseph could hold back no longer. He cleared the room of all his Egyptian staff and wept so loudly that the whole palace heard him. Then he said the words that have rung through every generation since: You intended to harm me. But God intended it for good.

Not: what happened to me was fine. Not: your betrayal didn't matter. But: God was present in the betrayal, working through it, toward something none of us could see. Joseph's life is the Bible's most extended answer to the question: where is God when terrible things happen? He is there. He has always been there. And He was already writing the next chapter.

devotional.bigIdea: You intended to harm me, but God intended it for good. Joseph's story doesn't promise that bad things won't happen. It promises that no bad thing is outside God's reach.
questions: Joseph spent years in prison for something he didn't do. What do you think kept him from becoming bitter?
God is described as being with Joseph in the pit, in Potiphar's house, and in the prison. What does it mean that God is with you in the worst places, not just the good ones?
Joseph said you intended harm, God intended good. What is the difference between that and just pretending a bad thing was actually fine?

connections.title: The Boy Who Came Home
connections.why: Joseph wept and kissed his brothers, the same extravagant forgiveness the father showed the prodigal son. Forgiveness runs through both stories.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Stephen in Acts calls Joseph a picture of Jesus, rejected by his brothers, sold for silver, exalted to save many lives. The pattern points forward.

connections.title: The Baby in the River
connections.why: God used Joseph's suffering to get His people into Egypt, and then used Moses to get them out. One story sets up the next.

quiz.question: How much silver did Joseph's brothers sell him for?
options: Ten pieces of silver
Twenty pieces of silver
Fifty pieces of silver
quiz.explanation: Joseph was sold to the Ishmaelite traders for twenty pieces of silver, a small price for a person's life.

quiz.question: How old was Joseph when he became second-in-command of Egypt?
options: Thirty years old
Twenty years old
Twenty-five years old
quiz.explanation: Joseph was thirty years old when he stood before Pharaoh and went from prison to palace in a single day.

quiz.question: What did Joseph say to his brothers when he revealed who he was?
options: I have been waiting to find you all these years
You will pay for what you did to me
You intended to harm me, but God intended it for good
quiz.explanation: Joseph said you intended harm, but God intended it for good. It is one of the greatest statements of faith and forgiveness in the whole Bible.

quiz.question: What gift did Jacob give Joseph that made his brothers jealous?
options: The best portion of land
A horse of his own
A coat of many colors
His own flock of sheep
quiz.explanation: Jacob gave Joseph a richly ornamented robe, a sign of favoritism that inflamed his brothers' jealousy and set the whole story in motion.

quiz.question: What did Joseph's two dreams as a boy suggest about his future?
options: That he would travel to a foreign land
That he would one day rule over his brothers
That God would speak to him as a prophet
That he would be a great farmer
quiz.explanation: Joseph dreamed that sheaves of grain and then the sun, moon, and stars bowed down to him, a picture of his future authority over his whole family.

stories.title: The Baby in the River
stories.hook: A mother hid her baby for three months while an empire tried to kill him. When she could hide him no longer, she put him in the river Pharaoh had chosen as his grave.
story: More than four hundred years had passed since Joseph saved Egypt from famine. The seventy people who had come down from Canaan had grown into a nation, too large to count easily, too numerous to ignore. And a new Pharaoh sat on the throne. One who had never heard of Joseph. One who looked at this enormous population of foreigners in his land and felt one thing: fear.

His solution was slavery. Systematic, brutal, intentional slavery, mortar and brick in the scorching Egyptian sun, building the great store cities of Pithom and Rameses. The harder Pharaoh pressed them, the more the Israelites multiplied. The more they multiplied, the more Egypt dreaded them. So Pharaoh escalated. He ordered the Hebrew midwives, two women named Shiphrah and Puah, to kill every Hebrew baby boy at birth. The midwives feared God more than they feared Pharaoh. They let the boys live. God was kind to them for it.
Then Pharaoh issued a public decree: every Hebrew boy born must be thrown into the Nile. The river the Egyptians worshipped as a god would become the grave of an entire generation.

Into this world, a woman from the tribe of Levi gave birth to a son. She took one look at him and hid him. Three months of impossible courage, muffled cries, watchful neighbors, a secret that one wrong sound could shatter. When she could hide him no longer, she made a plan that required a deeper kind of faith than hiding ever had.

She took a basket made of papyrus reeds, the same Hebrew word used for Noah's ark, and almost certainly on purpose, waterproofed it with tar and pitch, and placed her baby inside. Then she carried him to the Nile and set him among the reeds at the water's edge. The river where Hebrew boys were supposed to die would carry this one to safety. His sister Miriam watched from a distance.

Pharaoh's own daughter came down to bathe. She saw the basket. She opened it, and the baby cried. Her heart bent toward him immediately, even knowing he was a Hebrew baby, even knowing her father's decree. She kept him anyway.

Miriam appeared from the reeds at exactly the right moment. Shall I find a Hebrew woman to nurse the baby for you? Yes, said the princess. Miriam ran and got her mother. And the woman who had wept placing her baby in a basket was paid by Pharaoh's own household to raise her own son.
His name was Moses, a name that in Egyptian means son of, but that echoes the Hebrew word for drawn out. She drew him out of the water. He would spend his life drawing others out.

A decree meant to kill him placed him inside Pharaoh's palace. The river chosen as the instrument of death became the instrument of rescue. The very household trying to destroy the Hebrew people was funding the education of the man who would set them free. God was not absent from those four hundred years of silence. He was already writing the next chapter.

devotional.bigIdea: God was at work in Egypt long before Moses knew it and long before Israel could see it. What looks like the end of the story is often just the setup for what God is actually doing.
questions: Moses's mother placed him in the very river Pharaoh had chosen as his grave. Have you ever had to trust God with something you couldn't control?
God used Pharaoh's own daughter and Pharaoh's own palace to prepare the man who would bring Pharaoh down. What does that tell you about how God works?
The midwives Shiphrah and Puah are named in Scripture. Most people in Pharaoh's court are not. What do you think that means about who God notices?

connections.title: The Ark and the Rainbow
connections.why: The word for Moses's basket is the same Hebrew word used for Noah's ark. Both were vessels of rescue floating on water, the pattern is deliberate.

connections.title: The Burning Bush
connections.why: Acts tells us Moses was educated in all the wisdom of Egypt, the very training he received in Pharaoh's palace prepared him for everything God would ask him to do.

connections.title: Impossible News
connections.why: Jesus was also a baby whose life was threatened by a king's decree, whose family fled to protect Him. Moses's story foreshadows the flight to Egypt.

quiz.question: What were the names of the two midwives who refused to obey Pharaoh's order?
options: Rachel and Leah
Miriam and Zipporah
Shiphrah and Puah
quiz.explanation: Shiphrah and Puah feared God more than Pharaoh and let the Hebrew boys live. God was kind to them for their courage.

quiz.question: What was Moses's basket made from?
options: Clay baked in a kiln
Wood and animal skin
Papyrus reeds sealed with tar and pitch
quiz.explanation: Moses's mother made a basket from papyrus reeds and waterproofed it, the same Hebrew word used for Noah's ark.

quiz.question: Who ran to get Moses's own mother to nurse him?
options: Moses's mother herself
His sister Miriam
Pharaoh's daughter
quiz.explanation: Miriam was watching from a distance and stepped forward at exactly the right moment, getting their own mother paid to nurse Moses.

quiz.question: Why was the birth of Moses especially dangerous?
options: Pharaoh had ordered all newborn Hebrew boys to be thrown into the Nile
Pharaoh had enslaved all Hebrew men who were working age
There was a plague spreading through the Hebrew settlements
There was a famine and food was scarce
quiz.explanation: Pharaoh feared the growing Hebrew population and ordered every newborn Hebrew boy drowned in the Nile, making Moses's survival a miracle from his first breath.

quiz.question: Who adopted Moses and raised him in the palace?
options: Pharaoh's wife
A Hebrew slave who served the palace
Pharaoh's sister
Pharaoh's daughter
quiz.explanation: Pharaoh's daughter found the basket in the reeds, felt compassion for the crying baby, and raised him as her own son in the royal palace.

stories.title: The Burning Bush
stories.hook: A fugitive shepherd who had given up on his old life saw a bush that burned without burning up. Then God called him by name.
story: Moses had been in the desert for forty years. He had fled Egypt after killing an Egyptian guard who was beating a Hebrew slave, the right instinct, the wrong execution, and ended up in Midian, on the far side of the wilderness. He married Zipporah, the daughter of a priest named Jethro. He had children. He tended sheep. The boy raised in Pharaoh's palace spent forty years in the desert with livestock. He was eighty years old. Whatever he had once imagined his life might be, this was not it.

One ordinary day, leading the flock to the far side of the wilderness near a mountain called Horeb, Moses saw something that should not have been possible. A bush was on fire. That alone was not unusual, the dry scrub of the Sinai desert caught fire in summer. What was unusual was that the bush was not burning up. The flames were real, dancing and leaping, but the bush stayed green. Moses turned aside to look. Just turned aside. One small act of curiosity. And that is when God spoke.

Moses! Moses! He answered: Here I am. God told him to take off his sandals, the ground beneath his feet was holy, made holy by the presence of God. Then He introduced Himself: I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, the God of Jacob. Centuries had passed since those names walked the earth. The promises had not expired.

I have seen the misery of my people in Egypt, God said. I have heard them crying out. I know their suffering. And I have come down to rescue them. Every word deliberate: seen, heard, known, come down. Not distant. Not indifferent. Present, and moving. So now, go. I am sending you to Pharaoh to bring my people out of Egypt.

Moses had a list of reasons why this was a terrible idea. Who am I to go to Pharaoh? God's answer: I will be with you. I don't even know your name, what do I tell them? God's answer: I AM WHO I AM. Tell them I AM sent you. The name YHWH, the Lord, means the self-existent one, the always-present one, the one who simply is without beginning or end. Not I was. Not I will be. I AM. Always present tense.

What if they don't believe me? God gave him signs, a staff that became a serpent, a hand that became leprous and was restored. I am not a good speaker. God's answer: Who gave human beings their mouths? Is it not I, the Lord? I will help you speak and teach you what to say.

And finally, the honest one, stripped of all the reasonable-sounding objections: Please send someone else. At this point the Lord's anger burned against Moses. And still God didn't pull the call back. He gave Moses his brother Aaron as a companion and spokesman. The mission didn't change. Moses was still going.

Moses went back to Jethro, asked to return to Egypt to see if his relatives were still alive, and set out. He was eighty years old. He had a staff and a brother and a God who had appeared to him in a fire that didn't burn. He had been told that the One who made every human mouth would teach him what to say. That was all he had. It was enough. It has always been enough.

devotional.bigIdea: Every single objection Moses made was real. Every single one was answered the same way: I will be with you. That is still the only answer God gives, and it is still enough.
questions: Moses had five separate objections to God's call. Which one do you most identify with, and what do you think God's answer to your version of that objection would be?
God said I AM, not I was, not I will be, always present tense. What does it mean for your daily life that God is always here, always now?
Moses turned aside to look at the burning bush. What might he have missed if he had been too busy? What does that tell you about paying attention to the unexpected?

connections.title: The Baby in the River
connections.why: God says I have seen, I have heard, I know, answering four hundred years of silence. The same God who protected Moses as a baby was now sending him back.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Jesus told the Pharisees: before Abraham was, I AM. He took the name God spoke from the burning bush and applied it to Himself directly.

connections.title: The Day Everything Changed
connections.why: Stephen retells the burning bush story in Acts and says the angel of the Lord appeared in the flame, pointing to the same Spirit who would appear as fire at Pentecost.

quiz.question: Why was the burning bush so unusual?
options: The bush was burning but not burning up
It was on top of a mountain
The fire was speaking words
quiz.explanation: Bushes catching fire in the dry desert was common. What stopped Moses in his tracks was that this bush kept burning without being consumed.

quiz.question: What did God tell Moses his name was?
options: The God of Heaven
The Almighty
I AM WHO I AM
quiz.explanation: God said I AM WHO I AM, always present tense, self-existent, without beginning or end. It is the most profound name ever spoken.

quiz.question: Who did God give Moses as a companion when Moses kept asking God to send someone else?
options: His father-in-law Jethro
His nephew Caleb
His brother Aaron
quiz.explanation: God gave Moses his brother Aaron as a companion and spokesman, Moses would tell him what to say and Aaron would say it.

quiz.question: What did God tell Moses to do before coming closer to the burning bush?
options: Bring an offering from his flock
Remove his sandals because he was standing on holy ground
Bow down and cover his face
Fast for three days before approaching
quiz.explanation: God told Moses to remove his sandals, a sign of reverence before holy ground. God's presence makes ordinary places extraordinary.

quiz.question: What was Moses's first objection when God called him to lead Israel out of Egypt?
options: He said "Who am I that I should go to Pharaoh?"
He said he was too old
He said he was a murderer and could not lead
He said the people would not listen to him
quiz.explanation: "Who am I?", Moses's first response was deep humility. God's answer was not to list Moses's qualifications but to say "I will be with you."

stories.title: Through the Sea on Dry Ground
stories.hook: Two million people stood between the Egyptian army and the sea. There was nowhere to go. Then God moved.
story: They were free. After four hundred years, the Israelites walked out of Egypt, two million people, with their flocks and their children and their unleavened bread still warm from the fire. A pillar of cloud led them by day and a pillar of fire by night. They had been slaves. Now they were walking toward a promise.

Then Pharaoh changed his mind. The grief of losing his firstborn had lasted one night. By morning, the loss of his entire workforce felt unacceptable. He took six hundred of his best chariots and all the chariots of Egypt and pursued the Israelites into the wilderness. His army was one of the most powerful military forces in the ancient world.

The Israelites looked up and saw the Egyptians coming. Behind them, an army of chariots. Ahead of them, the Red Sea. There was nowhere to go. And they did what frightened people often do: they turned on the person who had led them there. Was there no room in Egypt to bury us? Did you bring us out here to die? We told you to leave us alone in Egypt!

Moses answered with words that have steadied people in impossible situations for three thousand years: Do not be afraid. Stand firm and you will see the deliverance the Lord will bring you today. The Egyptians you see today you will never see again. The Lord will fight for you, you need only to be still.

Then God told Moses to raise his staff over the sea. All night, a strong east wind blew. And the waters divided. A wall of water on the right side, a wall of water on the left side, and the ground between them went dry. Two million people walked through the sea on dry ground. The text says the waters were like a wall to them on their right and on their left. Not a river crossing. Not shallow water. Walls.

The Egyptian army followed them in. And God looked down at the Egyptian forces and threw them into confusion. Chariot wheels came off. Horses panicked. The soldiers understood too late what was happening, The Lord is fighting for Israel against Egypt! they said. They tried to turn around. They couldn't.

When Israel reached the far bank, Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and the water came back, covering the chariots, the horsemen, the entire army of Pharaoh. Not one of them survived. The Israelites saw the Egyptian army lying dead on the shore, and they feared the Lord and believed in Him and in His servant Moses.

Then Moses and the Israelites sang. One of the oldest songs in human history, breaking out on the far side of the sea: The Lord is my strength and my defense; He has become my salvation. Miriam took up a tambourine, and all the women followed her, dancing. They had been slaves. The army that had held them was gone. And the God who had been silent for four hundred years had just made Himself impossible to ignore.

They had done nothing to earn this rescue. They had panicked, blamed Moses, and wished they were back in slavery. And God parted the sea anyway. That is what grace looks like, not rescue because you deserve it, but rescue because He loves you and He made a promise.

devotional.bigIdea: When you are standing between the army and the sea with nowhere to go, that is often exactly where God does His most spectacular work. He doesn't need your ability. He just needs your stillness.
questions: The Israelites panicked and blamed Moses even though God had been performing miracles for months. Why do you think it is so easy to forget what God has done when a new crisis arrives?
Moses said be still and the Lord will fight for you. What does it actually mean to be still when you are scared?
The rescue came at the absolute last possible moment. Why do you think God so often waits until the situation looks completely impossible?

connections.title: The Ark and the Rainbow
connections.why: Paul says Israel's crossing of the sea was a kind of baptism, passing through water into a new life of freedom, just as baptism pictures death and resurrection.

connections.title: The Empty Tomb
connections.why: God says through Isaiah: forget the former things, I am doing a new thing. The crossing of the sea was pointing forward to an even greater rescue at the empty tomb.

connections.title: The Burning Bush
connections.why: After the crossing, the people believed in God and in Moses. The faith that was born at the sea is the same faith God had been calling Moses toward at the burning bush.

quiz.question: What did Moses tell the Israelites to do when they were trapped between the army and the sea?
options: Pick up weapons and fight
Pray loudly and march forward
Be still, the Lord will fight for you
quiz.explanation: Moses said stand firm, do not be afraid, be still, the Lord will fight for you. Sometimes the most powerful thing you can do is stop trying to fix it yourself.

quiz.question: What happened to the Egyptian army when they followed Israel into the sea?
options: The water came back and covered them completely
They were captured and made slaves
They turned back before the water closed
quiz.explanation: When Moses stretched out his hand, the water came back over the entire Egyptian army. Not one of them survived.

quiz.question: What did Miriam do after Israel crossed safely to the other side?
options: She knelt down and wept with gratitude
She built an altar to God
She took up a tambourine and led the women in dancing
quiz.explanation: Miriam took up a tambourine and led all the women in dancing and singing, one of the oldest celebrations of worship in recorded history.

quiz.question: What did God use to drive back the sea and make a path of dry ground?
options: An angel who pushed the water aside
A strong east wind that blew all night
The staff of Moses touching the water
A flash of lightning that split the waters
quiz.explanation: God drove back the sea with a strong east wind that blew all night, making the seabed dry ground. Natural means, supernatural timing and purpose.

quiz.question: What went between the Israelites and the pursuing Egyptian army the night before the crossing?
options: A wall of fire
A line of angels
The pillar of cloud moved behind Israel
A thick cloud of darkness
quiz.explanation: The pillar of cloud moved from the front to the back of Israel, giving light to Israel but darkness to Egypt, keeping the two camps apart all night.

stories.title: The Ten Commandments
stories.hook: The mountain shook with thunder and lightning and thick cloud. Then God spoke ten words that shaped civilization.
story: Three months after walking out of Egypt, the Israelites arrived at the base of a mountain in the middle of the Sinai Peninsula. Mount Sinai. The same mountain where Moses had seen the burning bush. God told Moses to prepare the people. In three days, He was coming down to speak to them Himself.

On the morning of the third day, they woke to something they had never seen before and would never forget. Thunder and lightning covered the mountain. A thick cloud settled on the peak. A trumpet blast, not made by human hands, rang out so loud that the entire camp trembled. Then the whole mountain began to shake and smoke, because God had descended on it in fire. Moses spoke, and God answered him in thunder.

The people were to stay back from the mountain. Anyone who touched it would die. The boundary was clear. God was coming down, and His presence was not something to treat casually. This was not a tame or distant God. This was the God who had split the sea and sent plagues and led them through the wilderness with a pillar of fire. And He was about to speak.

The first four commandments were about God Himself. You shall have no other gods before me. You shall not make idols. You shall not misuse the name of the Lord your God. Remember the Sabbath day and keep it holy, one day in seven, rest the way God rested. These four said: know who God is, keep Him first, treat His name with the reverence it deserves, and build rest into the rhythm of your life.

The last six were about people. Honor your father and your mother. You shall not murder. You shall not commit adultery. You shall not steal. You shall not give false testimony against your neighbor. You shall not covet what belongs to your neighbor. These six said: life together requires honoring the people around you, protecting what belongs to them, and telling the truth. The whole fabric of human community rests on these things.

The people were so overwhelmed by the fire and thunder that they drew back from the mountain and said to Moses: you speak to us, and we will listen. But do not let God speak to us, or we will die. Moses went into the thick darkness where God was. He came back with two stone tablets, the Law written by the finger of God.

God did not give these commandments to earn His people's love. He gave them after He had already rescued His people, because rescued people need to know how to live. The pattern is important. First the rescue. Then the law. Salvation came first. The commandments came after, not as the price of freedom but as a picture of what freedom actually looks like.

Centuries later, a religious expert asked Jesus which commandment was the greatest. Jesus collapsed all ten into two. Love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, and mind. And love your neighbor as yourself. All the Law and the Prophets, He said, hang on these two commandments. The ten become two, and the two rest on one word: love.

devotional.bigIdea: The Ten Commandments are not a list of rules to earn God's love. They are a picture of what life looks like when God's love has already taken root in your heart.
questions: Jesus said all ten commandments can be summarized as love God and love people. Do you think that makes it simpler or harder than following ten rules?
God gave the commandments after rescuing His people from Egypt, not before. Why do you think the rescue came first?
Which of the ten commandments do you think is hardest to keep, and why?

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Jesus said He did not come to abolish the Law but to fulfill it, and then showed that the Law was always about the heart, not just the behavior.

connections.title: Through the Sea on Dry Ground
connections.why: God opens the Ten Commandments with 'I am the Lord your God who brought you out of Egypt.' The rescue always comes before the requirement.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Paul says what the Law could not do, make us righteous from the inside, God did by sending His Son. Jesus fulfilled what the commandments pointed toward.

quiz.question: How did God deliver the Ten Commandments to Israel?
options: He spoke them out loud from the mountain in fire and thunder so the whole nation heard
He sent an angel to write them in the sky
He whispered them to Moses alone on the mountain
quiz.explanation: God spoke directly to the whole nation from the mountain, out of fire and thunder, before giving Moses the stone tablets.

quiz.question: What are the first four commandments about?
options: How to treat your neighbors and family
The relationship between God and His people
Rules for worship in the tabernacle
quiz.explanation: The first four commandments are all about God, no other gods, no idols, honor His name, keep the Sabbath. They establish who God is and how to relate to Him.

quiz.question: How did Jesus summarize all ten commandments?
options: Do not murder, steal, or lie
Keep the Sabbath and honor your parents
Love God with everything you have and love your neighbor as yourself
quiz.explanation: Jesus said all the Law hangs on two things: love God completely and love your neighbor as yourself. The ten collapse into two, and the two rest on love.

quiz.question: What were the Ten Commandments written on?
options: Two tablets of stone, written by the finger of God
Clay tablets baked in a fire
Wooden planks carried by the priests
Papyrus scrolls sealed in gold
quiz.explanation: God wrote the commandments on two stone tablets with His own finger, a permanent, authoritative record of His covenant with Israel.

quiz.question: Which commandment is the only one with a specific promise attached to it?
options: Do not murder
Remember the Sabbath day
Do not commit adultery
Honor your father and your mother
quiz.explanation: "Honor your father and mother" comes with a promise: "that your days may be long in the land." Paul later calls it the first commandment with a promise.

stories.title: Water From the Rock
stories.hook: The people were thirsty enough to stone Moses. He struck a rock with his staff and water poured out.
story: The Israelites had been in the wilderness long enough for the initial wonder of the Red Sea to fade. Now they were camped at a place called Rephidim, and there was no water. Not less water. No water. The thirst was real and the panic was rising.

They turned on Moses. Why did you bring us out of Egypt? To kill us and our children and livestock with thirst? Moses was in genuine danger. He cried out to God: what shall I do with these people? They are almost ready to stone me.

God told Moses to take the elders of Israel and his staff, the same staff that had struck the Nile and parted the sea, and walk ahead to a rock at Horeb. Strike the rock, God said. And water will come out of it so the people can drink.

Moses did what God said. In front of the elders of Israel, he struck the rock. And water poured out. Enough for a nation of people and their animals, pouring from a rock in the middle of the desert, because Moses struck it at God's command.

He named the place Massah and Meribah, meaning testing and quarreling, because the Israelites had tested God, saying is the Lord among us or not? The miracle answered the question. But the question itself was the problem. After everything God had done, they were still asking whether He was with them.

The rock in the wilderness appears again in the New Testament in a way that would have surprised everyone who first heard it. Paul writes that the rock that followed Israel in the wilderness was Christ. The water from the stone was a picture of something deeper, a source of living water that no desert could dry up, flowing from One who would be struck so that others could drink.

Jesus stood in the Temple courts on the last day of a festival and cried out: if anyone is thirsty, let him come to me and drink. Whoever believes in me, rivers of living water will flow from within them. The rock in the wilderness had been pointing to this moment for fifteen hundred years.

devotional.bigIdea: The Israelites forgot what God had done the moment things got hard again. God answered with water from a rock. He keeps providing for people who keep forgetting He provides.
questions: The Israelites forgot the Red Sea crossing the moment they were thirsty. Have you ever forgotten something God had done for you when a new problem came along?
Moses cried out to God instead of giving up or fighting back. What does it look like to take your frustration to God instead of taking it out on people?
Paul says the rock was Christ. What do you think it means that Jesus is the source of living water? What kind of thirst does He satisfy?

connections.title: The Burning Bush
connections.why: The same staff Moses used at the burning bush and the Red Sea was the one God told him to use to strike the rock. The signs were all connected to one story.

connections.title: Through the Sea on Dry Ground
connections.why: Paul says the rock that followed Israel was Christ, connecting the water in the wilderness to the Red Sea crossing as two moments of the same supernatural provision.

connections.title: The Woman at the Well
connections.why: Jesus offered the Samaritan woman living water that would never run dry, the fulfillment of what the rock in the wilderness only pointed toward.

quiz.question: Why were the Israelites so angry with Moses at Rephidim?
options: There was no water and they were afraid of dying of thirst
They were lost and could not find the way forward
They wanted to go back to Egypt and Moses refused
The food had run out and they were starving
quiz.explanation: There was no water at all. The thirst was real and the people were desperate enough to threaten Moses with stoning.

quiz.question: What did God tell Moses to use to bring water from the rock?
options: A prayer spoken in front of all the people
A bowl of water poured over the rock as an offering
His staff, the same one used at the Red Sea
His hands pressed against the stone
quiz.explanation: God told Moses to take his staff and strike the rock at Horeb. The same staff used at the Red Sea was the instrument God chose here too.

quiz.question: What did Moses name the place, and what did the names mean?
options: Kadesh and Barnea, meaning holy and wilderness of wandering
Massah and Meribah, meaning testing and quarreling
Rephidim and Shur, meaning resting place and wall
Sinai and Horeb, meaning holy ground and sacred mountain
quiz.explanation: Moses named it Massah and Meribah because the people had tested God and quarreled, asking is the Lord among us or not even after all He had done.

quiz.question: What did Paul say the rock in the wilderness represented?
options: The strength of Israel that God was building in them
The covenant God made with Abraham about the land
The Law of God given to Moses on Sinai
Christ, who was the source of spiritual water for Israel
quiz.explanation: Paul writes that the rock was Christ, meaning the water God provided in the wilderness was pointing all along to the living water Jesus would offer.

quiz.question: What did Jesus cry out in the Temple that connects to this story?
options: I am the bread of life, whoever comes to me will never be hungry
Come to me all who are weary and I will give you rest
If anyone is thirsty let him come to me and drink
I am the gate, whoever enters through me will be saved
quiz.explanation: On the last day of a water festival, Jesus stood up and offered living water to anyone who was thirsty, fulfilling what the rock in the wilderness had pointed toward.

stories.title: The Scarlet Thread
stories.hook: Two spies. A city they had to take. A woman who hid them, and a red rope that changed everything.
story: Forty years in the wilderness were finally over. Moses had died on the mountain after seeing the Promised Land from a distance, and Joshua had taken command. The Israelites were camped on the eastern bank of the Jordan River, looking west at Canaan, a land of walled cities and powerful kingdoms. The first obstacle between them and the promise was Jericho.

Joshua sent two spies across the river to look over the land, and especially Jericho. They entered the city and came to the house of a woman named Rahab. She lived in the city wall itself, her house built right into it. She was a Canaanite. A prostitute. Not the kind of person you would expect to feature in a story about faith.

But the king of Jericho found out the spies were in the city and sent soldiers to Rahab's house. She had already hidden them under the stalks of flax drying on her roof. She sent the soldiers in the wrong direction, told them the men had already left and if they hurried they could still catch them. The soldiers left. The spies were safe on her roof.

Then Rahab went up to the spies and said something remarkable. She had heard about the God of Israel. She had heard how He dried up the Red Sea when they came out of Egypt. She had heard what He did to the two kings of the Amorites. And she believed what she had heard. Our hearts melted in fear, she said. The Lord your God, He is God in heaven above and on earth below.

She had never met God. She had never been to a worship service. She had only heard the stories, and she believed them. And she made a choice that put her life in danger. She asked only one thing in return for saving the spies. Spare me and my family when you take the city.

The spies agreed. They gave her a sign. A scarlet cord tied in the window of her house. Anyone inside the house with the scarlet cord would be spared when Israel came. Rahab lowered them by a rope through the window and told them which way to run. They escaped. She tied the scarlet cord in the window.

When Jericho fell, after the Israelites had marched around the walls for seven days and the walls collapsed at a shout on the seventh day, Joshua kept the promise. Rahab and her entire family were brought out safely while the city was destroyed around them. The woman with the scarlet cord in her window walked out alive.

The story does not end there. Rahab appears in the genealogy of Jesus in Matthew chapter one, listed by name as an ancestor of the Messiah. A Canaanite woman who believed what she heard about God and acted on it became part of the family line through which God would save the world. The scarlet cord that saved her household pointed forward across fifteen centuries to another scarlet sacrifice that would make a way for all of us.

devotional.bigIdea: Rahab had the wrong background, the wrong nationality, and the wrong reputation. What she had was faith, and that was enough. God does not require a perfect past to write someone into His story.
questions: Rahab had only heard stories about God, she had never experienced Him herself. But she believed and acted on what she heard. What stories about God have shaped your faith?
The scarlet cord in Rahab's window saved everyone who stayed inside. What do you think that cord was pointing toward?
Rahab risked her life to protect the spies. Where do you think that kind of courage comes from?

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Rahab is listed in Hebrews 11, the hall of faith, as someone who acted on belief before she could see the outcome. The scarlet cord points to the blood of Christ.

connections.title: The Ark and the Rainbow
connections.why: Rahab says 'the Lord your God is God in heaven above and earth below', the same God who made a covenant with Noah. She recognized the covenant God from the stories she had heard.

connections.title: Impossible News
connections.why: Rahab is named in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus, a Canaanite woman in the family line of the Messiah. God consistently chooses unexpected people to carry the story forward.

quiz.question: Where did Rahab hide the two Israelite spies?
options: In a cave outside the city walls
In a storage room beneath her house
Under stalks of flax drying on her roof
quiz.explanation: Rahab hid the spies under the stalks of flax drying on her roof, then sent the king's soldiers in the wrong direction.

quiz.question: What sign did the spies give Rahab to mark her house for safety?
options: A scarlet cord tied in the window
An olive branch hung on the wall
A white stone placed above the door
quiz.explanation: The spies told Rahab to tie a scarlet cord in the window, anyone inside the house marked by that cord would be spared when Israel took the city.

quiz.question: Where does Rahab appear later in the Bible?
options: As the mother of the prophet Elijah
In the genealogy of Jesus in Matthew chapter one
As a judge who led Israel into battle
quiz.explanation: Rahab is named in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus, a Canaanite woman who believed became an ancestor of the Messiah.

quiz.question: What did Rahab tell the king's men about the spies when they came looking?
options: She said she had never seen them
She said they had gone to the market
She refused to speak and said nothing
She said the men had already left and told them to chase after them
quiz.explanation: Rahab misdirected the king's men, sending them on a chase while the spies were hidden on her roof. Her deception protected God's people at great personal risk.

quiz.question: What did Rahab say showed her that the God of Israel was real?
options: She had heard God's voice in the wind
She had seen angels near the Israelite camp
She had heard what God did at the Red Sea and how Israel defeated the kings
She had a vision in the night
quiz.explanation: Rahab said she had heard about the Red Sea crossing and the defeated kings, and that all Canaan was melting with fear. Faith came through hearing.

stories.title: The Walls Come Down
stories.hook: God's battle plan for Jericho was march, trumpet, and shout. The most fortified city in Canaan fell without a single sword being swung.
story: Jericho was shut up tight. The gates were bolted, the walls were thick, and nobody was going in or out. The whole city had heard what had happened at the Red Sea and what Israel had done to the two kings of the Amorites. Fear had locked the city down. The commander of the Lord's army appeared to Joshua outside the city with his sword drawn, and Joshua fell face down in worship. He was not fighting this battle alone.

God gave Joshua the battle plan. March around the city once a day for six days. Seven priests carrying rams' horn trumpets are to walk in front of the ark. On the seventh day, march around the city seven times with the priests blowing their trumpets. When they sound a long blast, have all the people give a loud shout, and the wall of the city will collapse.

No siege ramps. No battering rams. No arrows or spears. March, trumpet, shout. To any military mind looking at Jericho's walls, the plan was absurd. But Joshua told the people exactly what God had said and they obeyed.

For six days they marched around the city once each day. The priests blew the trumpets. The ark of the Lord followed. The people made no sound, no war cry, no word at all, just the sound of feet on the ground and trumpets in the air. Then they went back to camp. Six days of this. Six days of looking at walls that had not moved.

On the seventh day they got up at dawn and marched around the city seven times. The seventh time around, when the priests sounded the trumpet blast, Joshua commanded the people: shout! For the Lord has given you the city! The people shouted. The trumpets blared. And the wall collapsed.

Every part of it. Flat to the ground. The armies of Israel charged straight in, every man going directly ahead, and they took the city. Joshua kept his promise to Rahab. The two spies went to her house, the one with the scarlet cord in the window, and brought her and her entire family out safely before the city was destroyed.

Jericho fell not because Israel was stronger, not because their military strategy was superior, but because God had said: I have given you this city. The walls came down the moment the people believed it enough to shout about it before they could see it.

devotional.bigIdea: Israel marched in silence for six days looking at walls that did not move. On the seventh day they shouted before the walls fell, not after. God asks us to trust what He has said before we can see it.
questions: For six days the walls did not move. How do you think the people felt on day three or four? What kept them going?
The battle plan looked ridiculous from the outside. Has God ever asked you to do something that did not make sense? What happened?
The people shouted before the walls fell, not after. What is the difference between celebrating what God has promised and celebrating what you can already see?

connections.title: The Scarlet Thread
