connections.why: Rahab's scarlet cord was in her window when the walls came down. She was protected in the middle of the destruction because she had trusted the God of Israel before the battle began.

connections.title: Three Hundred Men and Torches
connections.why: Joshua told the people the Lord has given you the city before a wall had fallen, just as Gideon went into battle because God said I have given them into your hands.

connections.title: Through the Sea on Dry Ground
connections.why: Hebrews lists Jericho alongside the Red Sea crossing as an act of faith. The walls fell by faith. Both victories came the same way: trusting what God said before seeing what God would do.

quiz.question: Why was Jericho shut up tight when Israel arrived?
options: A plague had broken out inside the city
They were celebrating a festival and had closed the gates
They were afraid because of what they had heard about the God of Israel
They had seen Israel's army and were preparing their defenses
quiz.explanation: The whole city had heard about the Red Sea crossing and the defeat of the Amorite kings. Fear had sealed the city completely.

quiz.question: What was God's battle plan for taking Jericho?
options: March around the city, blow trumpets, and shout on the seventh day
Dig under the walls at night and collapse them from beneath
Shoot flaming arrows over the walls to set the city on fire
Send spies in at night to open the gates from the inside
quiz.explanation: March once a day for six days, seven times on the seventh day with trumpets blowing, then shout. No weapons, no siege equipment, just obedience.

quiz.question: What did the people do for the first six days?
options: They built siege ramps and assault towers around the city
They shouted war cries to intimidate the people inside
They prayed and fasted at the base of the walls
They marched in silence with only the sound of trumpets
quiz.explanation: Joshua told them not to give a war cry or raise their voices until the day he commanded them to shout. Six days of silence and trumpets.

quiz.question: What happened to the walls when the people shouted on the seventh day?
options: The gates swung open but the walls stayed standing
The walls collapsed completely flat to the ground
A crack appeared and soldiers broke through it
The top section crumbled and soldiers climbed over the rubble
quiz.explanation: The wall collapsed completely. The armies of Israel charged straight in, every man going directly ahead, because there was nothing left to stop them.

quiz.question: Who was protected when Jericho was destroyed?
options: The priests who had carried the ark around the city
All of the children inside Jericho
Rahab and her entire family who were in her house with the scarlet cord
The Israelite scouts who had first entered the city
quiz.explanation: Rahab and everyone inside her house with the scarlet cord in the window were brought out safely, just as the spies had promised.

stories.title: Three Hundred Men and Torches
stories.hook: God told a frightened farmer to fight an army of 135,000 men with 300 soldiers and some clay pots.
story: Israel had a pattern in those years that repeated like a slow, painful loop. They would forget God, chase after the idols of the surrounding nations, fall under foreign oppression, cry out to God in desperation, and God would raise up a deliverer. Then the cycle would begin again. It was the same story, repeated generation after generation, because the problem was never the enemy outside. It was always the heart inside.

This time the oppressor was Midian. For seven years, the Midianites had swept into Israel at harvest time like locusts, taking everything. Crops, livestock, all of it. The Israelites were hiding in caves and mountain strongholds just to survive. They cried out to God.

God's answer came to a man named Gideon, who at that moment was threshing wheat in a winepress, hidden underground, trying not to be seen. An angel of the Lord appeared and said to him: the Lord is with you, mighty warrior. Gideon looked around at the winepress. Mighty warrior. He said: if the Lord is with us, why has all this happened to us? The angel did not answer the question. He gave Gideon a commission instead. Go in the strength you have. Am I not sending you?

Gideon asked for a sign, and God gave him one. Fire came out from the rock and consumed the offering Gideon had laid there. He tested God again with a fleece, wet while the ground stayed dry, then dry while the ground was wet. God answered both tests patiently. Then Gideon gathered an army of 32,000 men to face a Midianite force of 135,000.

God said: too many. If you win with this many, Israel will think they did it themselves. Send home anyone who is afraid. 22,000 left. Still too many, God said. Take them to the water. Watch how they drink. Those who knelt with their face to the water were sent home. Those who lapped the water with their hand to their mouth, 300 men, stayed. Three hundred against 135,000.

The battle plan was stranger still. Divide into three groups, surround the camp at night, carry torches hidden inside clay jars, and at Gideon's signal blow trumpets, smash the jars, raise the torches, and shout. No swords. No charge. Just light and noise in the darkness.

In the middle of the night, the torches blazed, three hundred trumpets blared, and 300 voices cried out: a sword for the Lord and for Gideon! The Midianite camp erupted in chaos. In their confusion and terror, they turned their swords on each other. They ran. Israel pursued. The victory was total.

Gideon went from hiding in a winepress to routing 135,000 men. The difference was not confidence. The difference was not superior strategy. The difference was that God was with him, and God had deliberately stripped away every human advantage so that nobody could miss it. Not with overwhelming resources, but with willing, obedient people and impossible odds, so that when the victory came, everyone knew who won it.

devotional.bigIdea: God reduced Gideon's army from 32,000 to 300 on purpose. He often reduces your resources before the battle so the victory is obviously His. Small forces and impossible odds are exactly how He works.
questions: Gideon told God exactly how he felt, confused, afraid, and abandoned. Is it okay to be that honest with God? What do you think happens when you are?
God kept reducing the army until only 300 were left. Why do you think God works that way instead of just giving you everything you need?
Where in your life right now do you feel like Gideon, hiding in the winepress, feeling like the wrong person for what is ahead?

connections.title: The Burning Bush
connections.why: God called Gideon the same way He called Moses, Go in the strength you have, am I not sending you? Both men felt completely unqualified. Both went anyway.

connections.title: One Stone
connections.why: David and Gideon both faced impossible military odds and both understood the same thing: the battle belongs to the Lord. The victory is never about the size of your army.

connections.title: The Day Everything Changed
connections.why: Paul says God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong. Gideon's 300 is exactly that pattern, God consistently chooses the small and the unlikely.

quiz.question: What was Gideon doing when the angel of the Lord appeared to him?
options: Praying at the tabernacle in Shiloh
Threshing wheat in a winepress, hiding from the enemy
Leading his army against the Midianites
quiz.explanation: Gideon was hiding in a winepress, threshing wheat underground so the Midianites wouldn't see him. Not exactly where you'd expect to find a mighty warrior.

quiz.question: How many men did God reduce Gideon's army to before the battle?
options: Three thousand
Thirty
Three hundred
quiz.explanation: God reduced the army from 32,000 all the way down to 300, so that when Israel won, nobody could claim they did it themselves.

quiz.question: What was the battle plan God gave Gideon?
options: Surround the camp at night with torches, trumpets, and a shout, no swords
Send the 300 men in disguise to cause confusion inside the camp
Charge the camp at dawn with swords drawn
quiz.explanation: Torches hidden in clay jars, trumpets, and a shout in the night, that was the whole plan. No swords drawn. No charge. Just light and noise and God.

quiz.question: What did Gideon lay out overnight to test whether God was really calling him?
options: A lamp that he asked God to keep burning all night
A clay pot filled with water
A fleece of wool, asking God to make it wet while the ground stayed dry
A sacrifice on the altar
quiz.explanation: Gideon tested God twice with a fleece, first wet fleece on dry ground, then dry fleece on wet ground. God patiently answered both tests of a fearful man.

quiz.question: Why did God reduce Gideon's army so dramatically before the battle?
options: Because God wanted to test Gideon's obedience
So Israel would not boast that their own strength had saved them
Because the larger army had too many cowards in its ranks
Because a smaller army is always more effective in battle
quiz.explanation: God said "The people with you are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hand, lest Israel boast over me, saying, My own hand has saved me."

stories.title: Wherever You Go
stories.hook: Her husband died. Her mother-in-law's husband died. Then Ruth said the most loyal words in the Bible.
story: There was a famine in Israel. A man named Elimelech took his wife Naomi and their two sons to live in Moab, a neighboring country east of the Jordan, to wait out the famine. In Moab, the sons married Moabite women. One was named Orpah. The other was named Ruth. And then, over the years that followed, all three men died. Elimelech first. Then both sons. Three widows. No husbands. No children. In a world where a woman without a man had almost no way to survive.

Naomi heard that the famine in Israel was over and decided to go home to Bethlehem. She told her daughters-in-law to go back to their own families, back to their own people, their own gods, their own chance at a new life. She had nothing to offer them. It was the generous thing to say. Orpah kissed her mother-in-law goodbye and went back. Ruth held on.

Naomi said: your sister-in-law has gone back to her people and her gods. Go back with her. Ruth would not go. She said words that have been repeated at weddings and funerals and moments of deep loyalty for three thousand years. Do not urge me to leave you or turn back from you. Where you go I will go, and where you stay I will stay. Your people will be my people and your God my God. Where you die I will die, and there I will be buried. Not because Naomi was offering her anything. Because Ruth loved her, and love does not calculate the cost.

They arrived in Bethlehem at the beginning of the barley harvest, two widows with nothing. Ruth went to the fields to glean, to pick up the grain the harvesters left behind, the ancient provision in the Law for the poor. She happened to come to a field belonging to a man named Boaz, a relative of Naomi's dead husband.

Boaz noticed her. He asked his workers who she was, heard her story, and immediately gave instructions to leave extra grain for her on purpose. Let her drink from the workers' water jars, make sure nobody bothers her. He told her: I have heard everything you have done for your mother-in-law since your husband died, how you left your father and mother and your homeland and came to a people you did not know. May the Lord repay you for what you have done.

Ruth asked why he was being so kind to a foreigner. He said: you have come to take refuge under the wings of the Lord God of Israel. She had exchanged her own gods for His, her own people for His people, and He had noticed.

Boaz was what the Law called a kinsman-redeemer, a close relative with the right and the responsibility to redeem a family member who had fallen into poverty or lost their land. He chose to use that right. He married Ruth. From that marriage came a son named Obed, who had a son named Jesse, who had a son named David.

A foreign widow who chose loyalty over comfort, in a field where she happened to glean, with a man who was exactly the right person in exactly the right position. The God who redeemed Ruth through Boaz was already planning a greater redemption through a greater Son, one who would be called the redeemer of all humanity.

devotional.bigIdea: Ruth gave up everything familiar because she loved Naomi and chose Naomi's God. Loyalty to the people God has placed in your life, especially when it costs something, is one of the most beautiful things you can offer.
questions: Ruth had every practical reason to go back to her own people. What do you think made her stay with Naomi instead?
Boaz said Ruth had come to take refuge under the wings of the Lord God of Israel. What does it mean to take refuge in God?
Ruth ends up in the family line of both David and Jesus. What does that tell you about the long-term results of faithful, loyal choices?

connections.title: The Boy Who Came Home
connections.why: Ruth's commitment to Naomi, where you go I will go, mirrors the unconditional loyalty of the father in the prodigal son. Love that does not calculate the cost.

connections.title: Impossible News
connections.why: Ruth is named in Matthew's genealogy of Jesus alongside Rahab. Two foreign women, both outsiders, both in the family line of the Messiah. God's grace reaches further than we expect.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Boaz acted as kinsman-redeemer, paying the price to redeem what was lost. Jesus is the ultimate kinsman-redeemer, paying the price to redeem what sin had taken from all of us.

quiz.question: What did Ruth say to Naomi when Naomi told her to go back to her own people?
options: She agreed to leave but promised to come back
She said she would stay only until Naomi reached Bethlehem safely
She said where you go I will go, your people will be my people and your God my God
quiz.explanation: Ruth's words have echoed through three thousand years: where you go I will go. She chose loyalty over safety, and Naomi's God over her own people's gods.

quiz.question: What was Boaz's relationship to Naomi's family that made him especially significant?
options: He was an old friend of Elimelech from childhood
He was a kinsman-redeemer, a close relative with the right to redeem the family
He was the leading elder at the city gate in Bethlehem
quiz.explanation: Boaz was a kinsman-redeemer, in the Law, a close relative had the right and responsibility to redeem a family member who had lost everything. He chose to use that right for Ruth.

quiz.question: Who was the famous grandson of Ruth and Boaz?
options: Solomon, the king who built the Temple
Samuel, the prophet who anointed the first kings
David, the greatest king Israel ever had
quiz.explanation: Ruth and Boaz had a son named Obed, who had a son named Jesse, who had a son named David, and David's line eventually led to Jesus.

quiz.question: What did Ruth do every day to provide food for herself and Naomi?
options: She gleaned grain in the fields behind the harvesters
She fished in the river near the city
She made and sold bread from their small supply of grain
She worked as a servant in Bethlehem's market
quiz.explanation: Ruth gleaned in the fields, picking up grain left behind by harvesters. God's law commanded farmers to leave grain for the poor and the foreigner.

quiz.question: What did Boaz instruct his workers to do for Ruth?
options: Give her a portion of the harvest pay at the end of each day
Deliberately pull out stalks from the bundles and leave them for her
Let her eat at the workers' table and rest in their shelter
All of the above
quiz.explanation: Boaz told his men to let her eat with them, protect her from harm, and intentionally drop grain from the bundles for her to collect. His kindness was extravagant.

stories.title: Speak, Lord
stories.hook: A boy kept hearing his name in the night. He did not know yet that it was God.
story: Samuel was a boy who lived in the tabernacle at Shiloh, serving the priest Eli. His mother Hannah had prayed for a child with such desperate intensity that Eli had thought she was drunk. God heard her prayer and gave her Samuel, and she brought him back to the tabernacle to serve God there, as she had promised. He was young. He slept near the ark of God. He helped Eli, who was old and whose eyes had grown so weak he could barely see.

The word of the Lord was rare in those days. The text notes this with a quiet sadness. Visions were not common. The spiritual life of Israel had grown thin. The priests themselves, Eli's sons Hophni and Phinehas, were corrupt and wicked, dishonoring the offerings and taking advantage of the people who came to worship. Eli knew and did not stop them. The light of God's voice had dimmed.

One night, Samuel lay down to sleep in the temple near the ark of God. And he heard his name: Samuel! He got up and ran to Eli. Here I am, you called me. Eli said: I did not call you. Go back and lie down. Samuel went back. The voice came again: Samuel! He ran to Eli again. Here I am, you called me. Eli said: I did not call you, my son. Go lie down.

A third time the voice came. A third time Samuel ran to Eli. And this time Eli understood what was happening. The old priest told the boy: go and lie down, and if He calls you again, say, Speak, Lord, for your servant is listening. Samuel went and lay down in his place.
The Lord came and stood and called as at other times: Samuel! Samuel! And Samuel said: Speak, for your servant is listening. Those five words became the posture of his entire life.

What God said was hard. He told Samuel that He was going to judge the family of Eli because Eli's sons had blasphemed God and Eli had not restrained them. Samuel lay there until morning, afraid to tell Eli what he had heard. But Eli called him and said: do not hide from me what God said. May God deal with you severely if you hide anything. And Samuel told him everything. Every word.

Eli's response reveals a man who had failed at the most important thing and who knew it. He is the Lord, Eli said. Let Him do what is good in His eyes. Samuel grew up, and the Lord was with him. And all of Israel knew that Samuel was established as a prophet of the Lord. The voice of God had returned to Israel, speaking through a boy who had simply said: I am listening.

devotional.bigIdea: God is still speaking. The question has never been whether He speaks, it is whether we are listening. Samuel's entire prayer was four words: your servant is listening.
questions: Samuel kept running to Eli because he didn't recognize God's voice yet. How do you learn to recognize a voice you've never heard before?
Eli had been a priest his whole life but seems to have stopped hearing God. What do you think happens to our ability to hear God when we stop paying attention?
What would it look like for you to pray Samuel's prayer, Speak, Lord, your servant is listening, and actually mean it today?

connections.title: The Burning Bush
connections.why: Moses turned aside to look at the burning bush, one small act of attention that changed everything. Samuel said speak, Lord, I am listening. Both stories turn on paying attention to God.

connections.title: The Day Everything Changed
connections.why: At Pentecost, Peter quotes Joel: your sons and daughters will prophesy. Samuel was the beginning of that prophetic stream, God speaking through available people of every age.

connections.title: Left Behind in Jerusalem
connections.why: Samuel grew in favor with God and people, the exact same phrase used for Jesus growing up in Nazareth. Both were young, both were available, both were in the place of worship.

quiz.question: Why did Samuel keep running to Eli when he heard his name called?
options: He had never heard God's voice before and didn't recognize it
He was testing whether Eli was awake
He thought Eli was in danger and needed help
quiz.explanation: Samuel had never heard God speak before, he didn't recognize the voice. It took three times before Eli realized what was happening and told Samuel how to respond.

quiz.question: What four words did Samuel say when God called him the fourth time?
options: What would you have me do
Here I am, Lord God
Speak, for your servant is listening
quiz.explanation: Speak, for your servant is listening, four words that became the posture of one of Israel's greatest prophets. Complete availability. Quiet expectation.

quiz.question: Why did God choose to speak to Samuel rather than to Eli the high priest?
options: Because Eli was too old to serve God faithfully
Because Samuel was more righteous than Eli
Because Eli had failed to restrain his wicked sons and the word of the Lord had grown rare
quiz.explanation: The word of the Lord was rare because the spiritual life of Israel had grown thin, including Eli's own failure to lead his family. God spoke to the boy who was still listening.

quiz.question: Why had the word of the Lord become rare in Israel at the time of Samuel's birth?
options: Because Israel had been conquered by a foreign nation
Because Eli's sons were wicked priests who despised the offerings of God
Because God was waiting for a new prophet to be born
Because the people had stopped building altars to God
quiz.explanation: Eli's sons Hophni and Phinehas were corrupt priests who treated God's offerings with contempt, and Eli did not stop them. Spiritual darkness had settled over Israel.

quiz.question: What message did God give Samuel to deliver to Eli that same night?
options: That God would judge Eli's family because he honored his sons above God
That Israel would be restored if Eli repented
That Samuel would replace Eli as priest immediately
That a foreign nation would attack Israel within the year
quiz.explanation: God told Samuel to tell Eli that judgment was coming on his family, because Eli knew his sons were sinning and did not rebuke them. It was Samuel's first hard assignment.

stories.title: One Stone
stories.hook: Nine feet tall. Armored head to toe. He had been taunting the army of God for forty days. A teenager showed up with five rocks.
story: For forty days, a Philistine soldier had walked out of the enemy camp every morning and evening and shouted the same challenge across the valley. His name was Goliath. He was from Gath. He stood over nine feet tall, head to foot in bronze armor, carrying a spear with an iron point that weighed fifteen pounds. His shield-bearer walked ahead of him. Every morning and every evening, the armies of Israel saw him coming and ran.

A boy named David arrived at the Israelite camp carrying food from his father for his three older brothers who were serving in the army. He heard Goliath's challenge. He heard the fear in the camp. He asked what would be done for the man who killed this Philistine. His oldest brother turned on him immediately. Why are you here? Who is watching your sheep? I know your pride. You came just to watch the battle.

David ignored him and kept asking. The words reached King Saul, and David was brought before him. Saul looked at this boy and said: you cannot fight him. You are just a boy and he has been a warrior his whole life. David answered with the kind of calm that can only come from a long track record with God. He told Saul about the lion and the bear he had killed while protecting his father's sheep. The Lord who delivered me from the lion and the bear, he said, will deliver me from this Philistine.

Saul offered David his armor. Heavy bronze helmet, coat of mail, sword. David put it on, took a few steps, and took it all off. He was not used to it. He went to the stream and picked up five smooth stones. He put them in his shepherd's bag, took his sling in his hand, and walked toward the giant.

Goliath looked David over and was insulted. Am I a dog, that you come at me with sticks? He cursed David by his gods and told him he would feed his flesh to the birds and the wild animals. David's response has been repeated across three thousand years of impossible situations. You come at me with sword and spear and javelin, he said. I come against you in the name of the Lord of hosts, the God of the armies of Israel, whom you have defied. The battle is the Lord's, and He will deliver you into our hands.

David reached into his bag, took out one stone, loaded his sling, and let it fly. The stone struck Goliath on the forehead. The giant fell face down on the ground. David had no sword, so he ran to where Goliath had fallen, took the giant's own sword, and killed him with it. When the Philistines saw their champion was dead, they turned and ran. Israel pursued them and won a complete victory.

One stone. One shepherd boy who had learned to trust God with the lion and the bear before he ever faced a giant. The size of the enemy was never the issue. It was the direction David was looking. Goliath looked at a boy with a sling. David looked at a God who had never lost a battle.

devotional.bigIdea: David didn't defeat Goliath because he was fearless. He defeated him because he had already learned to trust God in smaller battles, with the lion, with the bear. Giants are always defeated in training first.
questions: David said the Lord who delivered me from the lion and the bear will deliver me from this Philistine. What smaller battles has God already helped you through that might be preparing you for something bigger?
Everyone in Israel saw the same giant. David saw a different battle. What made his perspective different from everyone else's?

David refused Saul's armor and went with what he knew, a sling, five stones, and his God. What does it look like to fight your battles with what God has already given you instead of what someone else thinks you need?

connections.title: Three Hundred Men and Torches
connections.why: David said the battle is the Lord's, the exact same truth Gideon learned with 300 men against 135,000. The victory always belongs to God.

connections.title: The Burning Bush
connections.why: God told Samuel when choosing David that man looks at the outward appearance but the Lord looks at the heart. David was chosen for the same reason Moses was called, not for what others saw, but for what God saw.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Paul asks who can be against us if God is for us. David lived that question out loud in the valley of Elah, and Jesus answered it finally and completely at the cross.

quiz.question: How long had Goliath been challenging the Israelite army before David arrived?
options: One hundred days
Seven days
Forty days
quiz.explanation: Goliath had been walking out twice a day for forty days, and for forty days, the entire army of Israel had been running from him.

quiz.question: What did David take with him to face Goliath?
options: King Saul's armor and sword
Five smooth stones from the stream and his sling
A spear borrowed from one of the soldiers
quiz.explanation: David refused Saul's armor, he wasn't used to it. He went with what he knew: five smooth stones, his shepherd's sling, and his God.

quiz.question: What did David say when Goliath threatened him?
options: You come with sword and spear, I come in the name of the Lord, and the battle is His
I am not afraid of you because I am stronger than you look
God will protect me and your weapons cannot harm me
quiz.explanation: David said you come at me with sword and spear, I come in the name of the Lord of hosts. The battle is the Lord's. That was his whole strategy.

quiz.question: What experience had David already had that gave him confidence facing Goliath?
options: He had trained with Israel's best soldiers since he was young
He had seen God defeat the Philistines in a previous battle
He had received a vision from God promising him victory
He had killed both a lion and a bear that attacked his flock
quiz.explanation: David told Saul he had killed lions and bears with his bare hands to protect his sheep. If God could do that, He could handle one uncircumcised Philistine.

quiz.question: How many stones did David take from the stream before facing Goliath?
options: One, he trusted God completely from the start
Three, one for Goliath and two just in case
Five smooth stones
Seven, a number of completeness
quiz.explanation: David chose five smooth stones. He only needed one, but he was prepared. Faith and preparation are not opposites.

stories.title: The Cave and the King
stories.hook: The king who wanted David dead walked into a cave and David was already hiding inside it. He had the perfect chance and refused to take it.
story: Saul had been chasing David for years. David had done nothing wrong. He had served the king faithfully, won battles, played music to soothe Saul's troubled spirit, and married his daughter. But Saul was consumed by jealousy and fear, and he had turned the full force of the royal army against one man in the wilderness. David and his men had been living in caves and empty places, always moving, always hunted.

One day Saul took three thousand of his best fighting men into the wilderness of En Gedi to hunt for David. He came to some sheepfolds along the way and went into a cave to rest. He did not know that David and his men were hiding deep in that same cave.

David's men whispered to him: this is the day the Lord spoke of when He said He would give your enemy into your hands. Do what you want with him. David crept forward in the darkness. He cut off the corner of Saul's robe. Saul did not notice. He left the cave. David went no further.

Immediately David felt the weight of what he had almost done. He said to his men: the Lord forbid that I should do this thing to my lord, the Lord's anointed, to put out my hand against him, seeing he is the Lord's anointed. He restrained his men and would not let them attack Saul.

When Saul had gone some distance from the cave, David came out and called after him. My lord the king! When Saul looked back, David bowed face to the ground. He held up the piece of robe. See, my father, see the corner of your robe in my hand. I cut it off, but I did not kill you. There is no wrong or treason in my hands. The Lord judge between me and you. May He avenge me against you, but my hand will not touch you.

Saul wept. He said: you are more righteous than I, for you have repaid me good though I have repaid you evil. He admitted it plainly, standing in the open with three thousand men behind him and the man he had been hunting in front of him. And then he called off the hunt and went home.

David would wait years more before becoming king. He would not take the throne by force or by revenge. He would wait for God's timing, even when God's timing felt impossible to understand. The man who would not kill the king who was trying to kill him had learned something in the cave that no throne can teach: that power taken is not the same as power given.

devotional.bigIdea: David had the perfect opportunity to end his suffering and take the throne in one moment. He refused. Not because he was afraid, but because he trusted God to do what only God should do. Waiting for God is not weakness. It is the hardest kind of faith.
questions: David's men told him this was the moment God had prepared. David disagreed. How do you know the difference between an opportunity God has given and a temptation dressed up as an opportunity?
David respected Saul's position even while Saul was trying to kill him. What does it look like to respect someone in authority who is treating you unfairly?
David waited years for God to make him king instead of taking the throne himself. Is there something in your life right now where you are being asked to wait instead of act? What makes that hard?

connections.title: One Stone
connections.why: David killed Goliath because the battle was the Lord's. He refused to kill Saul for the same reason. Both decisions came from the same understanding of who is actually in charge of outcomes.

connections.title: The Lord Is My Shepherd
connections.why: David wrote Psalm 57 hiding from Saul in a cave. Have mercy on me O God, in you my soul takes refuge. The cave that sheltered him was also where his most honest prayers were written.

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Jesus said love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you. David practiced this before Jesus ever said it, blessing the man who had been hunting him and refusing revenge.

quiz.question: Why was Saul hunting David in the wilderness?
options: David had fled after being accused of treason by the priests
Saul was consumed by jealousy and fear after David's victories
David had stolen money from the royal treasury
Saul believed David was planning to overthrow the kingdom
quiz.explanation: David had done nothing wrong. Saul was consumed by jealousy at David's success and fear that God had chosen David to replace him as king.

quiz.question: What did David do when he had the chance to kill Saul in the cave?
options: He tied Saul up and held him as a hostage for peace
He woke Saul up and confronted him directly
He killed him quietly so his men would not see
He cut off only a corner of Saul's robe and let him leave unharmed
quiz.explanation: David crept forward and cut off a corner of Saul's robe but would not harm him. He immediately felt troubled even about that much.

quiz.question: What reason did David give for not killing Saul?
options: Saul was the Lord's anointed and David would not lift his hand against him
He wanted to wait until he had more men before confronting the king
He was afraid of what Saul's army would do in revenge
He thought killing Saul would make him look guilty to the people
quiz.explanation: David said the Lord forbid that I should do this to my lord, the Lord's anointed. Saul's position was given by God, and only God should remove it.

quiz.question: How did Saul respond when David showed him the piece of robe?
options: He was furious and ordered his men to charge at David immediately
He said nothing and walked away without acknowledging what David had done
He wept and admitted that David was more righteous than he was
He offered to make peace but secretly planned to continue the hunt
quiz.explanation: Saul wept and said you are more righteous than I, for you have repaid me good though I have repaid you evil. He admitted the truth plainly.

quiz.question: What did David's refusal to kill Saul show about the kind of king he would become?
options: That he was still loyal to Saul despite everything
That he trusted God to give him the throne rather than taking it by force
That he was waiting for a bigger army before making a move
That he was too gentle to be an effective ruler
quiz.explanation: David would not take what God had promised by methods God had not authorized. Power taken is not the same as power given. He was learning to wait for God.

stories.title: The Lord Is My Shepherd
stories.hook: The most famous poem in human history. Written by a king who had been a shepherd and never forgot what that meant.
story: David wrote a lot of songs. A hundred and fifty of them made it into the Bible, and he wrote many from the hardest places of his life. Hiding in caves. Running from enemies. Grieving losses that broke him. He was a shepherd before he was a king, and he never forgot what that meant. He spent years in the hills outside Bethlehem learning the rhythms of the flock. How sheep need to be led to water, not just pointed at it. How they panic easily. How they will not drink from rushing streams. How they need a shepherd who goes ahead of them into every place they fear.
And then he wrote this song. Six short verses. The most widely known piece of writing in human history.

The Lord is my shepherd. I shall not want. Not I should not want, or I try not to want. I shall not want. Present tense, absolute, resting on a Person. He makes me lie down in green pastures. Sheep will not lie down unless they feel completely safe, no predators nearby, no tension in the flock, no hunger, nothing threatening. The shepherd has to create those conditions. He leads me beside still waters, quiet streams and not rushing ones, because sheep will not drink from fast-moving water. He restores my soul.

He guides me in paths of righteousness, not because I am good at finding them, but for His name's sake. The path reflects on the shepherd, not just the sheep. Even though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, not around it but through it, I will fear no evil. For you are with me. Not watching from a distance. With me. Your rod and your staff, they comfort me. The rod was for fighting off predators. The staff was for guiding and rescuing sheep that had fallen into ravines. Both were signs of the shepherd's active presence.

You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies. This is no longer the sheep in the pasture. This is a guest at a feast, honored while enemies look on, unable to touch what is under the host's protection. You anoint my head with oil. My cup overflows. Not trickles, not barely enough. Overflows.

Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life. The Hebrew word translated follow is the same word often used for pursue, like a hunter chasing prey or a soldier pursuing a retreating enemy. Goodness and mercy are not walking quietly behind David. They are in pursuit. They will catch him.

And I shall dwell in the house of the Lord forever. The whole psalm has been moving toward this. Not just for today's pasture, not just for this valley, not just for this enemy-surrounded table. Forever. The shepherd is leading somewhere. The journey has a destination. And it is home.

devotional.bigIdea: The Lord is your shepherd, not your advisor, not your emergency contact. Your shepherd. He goes before you, stays with you, fights for you, and is leading you somewhere. You are not wandering. You are being led.
questions: David says I shall not want, not I try not to want, but I shall not. What do you think it takes to actually believe that about God?
The valley of the shadow of death is walked through, not around. Has God ever led you through something hard instead of around it? What did you find on the other side?
Goodness and mercy pursue us like a hunter pursues prey. What does it feel like to know that God's goodness is not passive, it is chasing you down?

connections.title: One Stone
connections.why: David wrote about walking through the valley of the shadow of death from experience. He had already walked through that valley in the valley of Elah, with a giant in front of him and an army behind him.

connections.title: The Empty Tomb
connections.why: Jesus said I am the good shepherd, the good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep. Psalm 23 is David's description. John 10 is Jesus's claim. They are the same Shepherd.

connections.title: The Boy Who Came Home
connections.why: The father in the prodigal son story runs to his returning son, the same way a shepherd leaves the ninety-nine to find the one. Goodness and mercy in pursuit.

quiz.question: Why does a shepherd need to lead sheep to still waters rather than fast-moving streams?
options: Because fast-moving streams are dangerous and sheep could fall in
Because sheep panic easily and won't drink from rushing fast-moving water
Because still water is cleaner and healthier for sheep
quiz.explanation: Sheep won't drink from fast-moving water, they need still, quiet streams. The shepherd knows this and leads them accordingly. God meets us where we actually are, not where we should be.

quiz.question: What does the Hebrew word translated as 'follow' in 'goodness and mercy shall follow me' actually mean?
options: Pursue like a hunter chasing prey
Wait patiently for someone to arrive
Walk quietly behind someone
quiz.explanation: The Hebrew word means to pursue, like a hunter, like a soldier. Goodness and mercy aren't strolling behind David. They are in active pursuit of him, and they will catch him.

quiz.question: In the psalm, what does the shepherd's rod represent?
options: A walking stick to help the shepherd keep balance on hills
A measuring stick to count how many sheep are in the flock
A weapon for fighting off predators that threaten the flock
quiz.explanation: The rod was a weapon, the shepherd used it to fight off lions, bears, and any predator threatening the flock. The rod and the staff together represent the shepherd's active protection and guidance.

quiz.question: What does the phrase "he restores my soul" suggest the shepherd does for the sheep?
options: Gives them extra food when they are hungry
Brings back sheep that have wandered away and gotten lost
Gives weary sheep a place to rest and recover
Heals sheep that have been injured by predators
quiz.explanation: Restoring the soul means bringing the sheep back to wholeness, rest, recovery, healing from exhaustion. The shepherd tends to the whole sheep, not just the physical needs.

quiz.question: What does the psalmist mean by "I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever"?
options: He was making a vow never to leave Jerusalem
He was expressing confidence that God's presence would be his permanent home
He was promising to attend the Temple every day
He planned to become a priest and live in the Temple
quiz.explanation: The psalm ends not in fear but in confidence, the shepherd's presence is home. It points forward to eternity with God, not just a building.

stories.title: The Wisest Request
stories.hook: God told Solomon he could ask for anything. Solomon asked for wisdom. God gave him wisdom and everything else besides.
story: Solomon was the son of David, the third king of Israel, and he was young. He had inherited the throne of the most powerful kingdom in the region, and the weight of it was real. He loved God and walked in the ways of his father David, but he was standing at the beginning of something enormous and he knew it.

God appeared to Solomon in a dream at night. Ask for whatever you want me to give you. It is the kind of offer that reveals exactly what someone cares about. Solomon could have asked for wealth. He could have asked for long life or the death of his enemies. He was surrounded by powerful nations that would have loved to destroy Israel, and he could have asked for military strength to crush them all.

Instead Solomon prayed honestly. He said: you have made your servant king in place of my father David, but I am only a little child and do not know how to carry out my duties. Your servant is here among the people you have chosen, a great people, too numerous to count. So give your servant a discerning heart to govern your people and to distinguish between right and wrong. For who is able to govern this great people of yours?

The Lord was pleased that Solomon had asked for this. God said: since you have asked for discernment in administering justice and have not asked for long life or wealth for yourself, or the death of your enemies, but for discernment to administer justice, I will do what you have asked. I will give you a wise and discerning heart, so that there will never have been anyone like you, nor will there ever be.

And then God gave him what he had not asked for. Wealth and honor, so that in your lifetime you will have no equal among kings. And if you walk in obedience to me and keep my commands, I will also give you a long life. He had asked for nothing but wisdom. He received wisdom and everything else.

The wisdom was immediately tested. Two women came before him with a terrible dispute. Both had given birth to sons. One baby had died in the night. Both women now claimed the living child. Solomon called for a sword. Cut the living child in two and give half to one and half to the other. The real mother cried out immediately. Give her the child, she said. Please do not kill him. The other woman said cut it. Solomon gave the child to the mother who had begged for its life.

Solomon asked for the right thing first. He knew he was small for the job. He knew that governing people justly required more than intelligence or experience. He needed a kind of knowing that only God could give. And the asking itself showed he already had the beginning of wisdom, because wisdom begins with knowing what you do not have.

devotional.bigIdea: Solomon could have asked for anything. He asked for the one thing that would help him serve other people well. God was so pleased by that request that He gave Solomon everything else on top of it. What we ask for reveals what we treasure.
questions: If God said to you tonight ask for whatever you want, what would you ask for? What does your first instinct reveal about what you value most?
Solomon said he was only a little child and did not know how to carry out his duties. Why is knowing what you do not know the beginning of wisdom?
God gave Solomon wealth and honor even though he did not ask for them. Jesus said seek first the kingdom of God and all these things will be added to you. How do these two stories fit together?

connections.title: Speak, Lord
connections.why: Solomon loved God and walked in the ways of his father David, and God appeared to him and spoke directly. Both Samuel and Solomon received words from God while they were young, before their greatest work began.

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Jesus said seek first the kingdom of God and all these things will be added to you. Solomon did exactly this, asking for wisdom to serve God's people rather than personal gain, and received everything else besides.

connections.title: The Lord Is My Shepherd
connections.why: The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom, wrote Solomon. Psalm 23, written by his father David, is what that wisdom looks like fully lived out: a life resting completely in God's hands.

quiz.question: What did God offer Solomon when He appeared to him in a dream?
options: Victory over all of Israel's enemies in battle
The plans for building the Temple in Jerusalem
A long life and prosperity for his entire reign
Whatever Solomon wanted to ask for
quiz.explanation: God appeared in a dream and said ask for whatever you want me to give you. It was an open offer that revealed exactly what Solomon cared about most.

quiz.question: What did Solomon ask for, and why?
options: Military strength because Israel was surrounded by powerful enemies
A discerning heart because he knew he was young and the task was too great for him
Wealth so he could build the Temple and make Israel great
Long life so he would have time to govern the nation well
quiz.explanation: Solomon asked for a discerning heart to govern God's people justly. He said he was like a little child who did not know how to carry out his duties. His humility was the request.

quiz.question: Why was God pleased with Solomon's request?
options: Because Solomon had prayed in exactly the right way
Because it showed Solomon was smarter than the kings before him
Because Solomon had not asked for personal gain but for the ability to serve others justly
Because wisdom was the most powerful thing God could give a king
quiz.explanation: God was pleased because Solomon had not asked for wealth, long life, or the death of his enemies, but for discernment to serve God's people well.

quiz.question: What did God give Solomon in addition to wisdom?
options: Wealth and honor beyond any king of his lifetime
Perfect health and freedom from all disease
A vast army that no neighboring nation could defeat
The ability to speak every language in the world
quiz.explanation: God gave Solomon what he had not asked for: wealth and honor so that in his lifetime there would be no equal among kings. Asking for the right thing brought everything else.

quiz.question: How did Solomon prove his wisdom in the case of the two mothers?
options: He prayed overnight and God revealed the answer in another dream
He questioned each woman for three days until one confessed
He asked the elders of Israel to vote on which woman was telling the truth
He ordered the child cut in two, knowing the real mother would rather give up her child than see it harmed
quiz.explanation: Solomon called for a sword and said divide the child. The real mother immediately begged for the child's life. Her love revealed the truth.

stories.title: Fire from Heaven
stories.hook: One prophet. 850 false prophets. A soaking wet altar. And then fire fell from the sky.
story: Israel had a king named Ahab, and the Bible says he did more evil in the eyes of the Lord than any king before him. He married a woman named Jezebel, who came from a nation that worshipped the god Baal. Under their rule, Baal worship spread across Israel and the prophets of God were hunted and killed. Into this situation, a man from Gilead named Elijah appeared before Ahab with a single message: there will be no rain in Israel except at my word.

Then God told Elijah to hide. For three and a half years there was no rain. The drought was devastating. God sent ravens to bring Elijah food beside a brook east of the Jordan. When the brook dried up, God sent him to a widow in Zarephath, a Gentile town, where her jar of flour and jug of oil never ran out for the rest of the drought. Elijah was fed and hidden while Israel suffered under the consequences of Ahab's choices.

In the third year, God told Elijah it was time. He appeared before Ahab and issued a challenge. Gather all Israel and all 450 prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel. Let them prepare a bull on an altar without lighting it. I will do the same. The god who answers by fire is God. Ahab agreed.

The prophets of Baal went first. They called on Baal from morning until noon. Nothing. They danced around the altar. Nothing. Elijah watched and started mocking them. Maybe your god is busy. Maybe he is traveling. Maybe he is sleeping and needs to be woken up. They shouted louder. They slashed themselves with swords and spears until blood flowed. By late afternoon there was still no response.

Then Elijah stepped forward. He rebuilt the altar using twelve stones, one for each tribe of Israel. He dug a trench around it. He arranged the wood and the bull. And then, to make it harder, to make it more obviously impossible, to make absolutely certain nobody could doubt what was about to happen, he ordered four large jars of water poured over everything. Three times. Twelve jars of water on the altar, the sacrifice, the wood. Water filled the trench.

Elijah prayed one simple prayer. Lord, God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, let it be known today that you are God in Israel and that I am your servant and have done all these things at your command. Answer me, Lord, answer me, so these people will know that you are God.

Fire fell from heaven. It burned up the sacrifice, the wood, the stones, and the soil. It licked up all the water in the trench. Every drop. When the people saw it, they fell face down on the ground and cried: the Lord, He is God. The Lord, He is God.

Then Elijah said to Ahab: go, eat and drink, for there is the sound of heavy rain. On top of the mountain Elijah prayed seven times. The seventh time his servant saw a cloud the size of a man's hand rising from the sea. By the time Ahab reached Jezreel, the sky was black with clouds and wind. The rain came down in torrents. Three and a half years of drought, ended by a prayer and a cloud the size of a hand.

devotional.bigIdea: Elijah didn't just ask God to match Baal, he soaked the altar in water to make the miracle more obviously impossible. He understood that God is most glorified when the situation is most impossible.
questions: Elijah poured twelve jars of water over the altar before asking God to send fire. What does that tell you about how Elijah understood God's power?
The prophets of Baal shouted louder when nothing happened. Elijah prayed one simple sentence. What is the difference between those two approaches to God?
The rain came from a cloud the size of a man's hand. Have you ever seen something tiny that turned into something enormous? What does that tell you about small beginnings?

connections.title: Three Hundred Men and Torches
connections.why: Elijah prayed I have done all these things at your command, the same posture as Gideon. Both acted under direct instruction, both saw God do what was impossible, and both understood the victory was never theirs to claim.

connections.title: The Day Everything Changed
connections.why: James uses Elijah as the model of effective prayer, a man just like us who prayed and the rain stopped, then prayed again and it came. The same Holy Spirit who empowered Elijah was poured out at Pentecost.

connections.title: Through the Sea on Dry Ground
connections.why: When the fire fell, the people cried The Lord, He is God, just as Israel cried out after the Red Sea crossing. God keeps interrupting history with moments that are impossible to explain away.

quiz.question: How did Elijah make the contest on Mount Carmel even harder before asking God to send fire?
options: He blindfolded himself so he could not help in any way
He stood far away from the altar and called out to God from a distance
He had twelve large jars of water poured over the altar, the sacrifice, and the wood
quiz.explanation: Elijah had twelve jars of water poured over everything, three times. Water filled the trench around the altar. He made it more obviously impossible so nobody could doubt what God did.

quiz.question: How many prophets of Baal were on Mount Carmel that day?
options: One thousand
Forty-five
Four hundred and fifty
quiz.explanation: There were 450 prophets of Baal against one prophet of God. Elijah stood alone against the entire false religious establishment of Israel, and God answered by fire.

quiz.question: What did Elijah tell Ahab to do before the rain even appeared?
options: Repent publicly before all the people of Israel
Go and eat and drink because he could already hear the sound of heavy rain
Rebuild the altars to God throughout the land
quiz.explanation: Elijah told Ahab to go eat and drink, the sound of heavy rain was already coming, even though there wasn't a cloud in the sky yet. That is faith: acting on what God said before you can see it.

quiz.question: How did God feed Elijah in the wilderness when he was exhausted and wanted to die?
options: An angel touched him twice and gave him food and water for the journey ahead
A widow found him and brought him food from her last supply
A stream appeared in the desert with fresh water
Ravens brought him bread and meat as they had before
quiz.explanation: An angel touched the sleeping Elijah and gave him cake and water, twice. Then God said the journey was too great for him without food. God is gentle with burned-out servants.

quiz.question: How did God speak to Elijah at Mount Horeb after the wind, earthquake, and fire?
options: Through a vision in the night
In a loud voice from the sky that all could hear
Through another angel who spoke face to face
In a low whisper, a still small voice
quiz.explanation: God was not in the wind, earthquake, or fire, but in the still small voice afterward. God often speaks most clearly in the quiet after the storm.

stories.title: The Man Who Lost Everything
stories.hook: Job was the most righteous man on earth. Then he lost everything. And God never told him why.
story: There was a man in the land of Uz named Job. He was blameless and upright, a man who feared God and turned away from evil. He had seven sons and three daughters, thousands of animals, and a large household. He was the greatest man among all the people of the East. And God was proud of him.

In the heavenly court, the adversary came before God and raised a challenge. Does Job fear God for nothing? Have you not put a hedge around him and everything he has? You have blessed the work of his hands. But stretch out your hand and strike everything he has, and he will surely curse you to your face. God said: everything he has is in your power. Only do not lay a hand on the man himself.

In a single day, Job lost everything. Raiders took his oxen and donkeys. Lightning struck and killed his sheep. More raiders took his camels. And then a messenger came with the worst news. A mighty wind struck the house where his children were eating and it collapsed. All ten were dead.

Job stood up and tore his robe and shaved his head in grief. Then he fell to the ground in worship. He said: naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked I will depart. The Lord gave and the Lord has taken away. May the name of the Lord be praised. In all of this, Job did not sin or charge God with wrongdoing.

Then Job lost his health. His body was covered with painful sores from the soles of his feet to the crown of his head. His wife said: are you still maintaining your integrity? Curse God and die. Job answered: shall we accept good from God and not trouble? His three friends came and sat with him in silence for seven days, because they saw how great his suffering was.

Then Job spoke. He did not pretend to be fine. He cursed the day he was born. He demanded an answer from God. He argued his case, insisting on his innocence, refusing to accept his friends' easy explanations that suffering must be punishment for sin. His friends were wrong, and God would say so later. Job was more honest than they were, even in his pain.

Then God spoke from a whirlwind. He did not answer Job's questions. He asked His own. Where were you when I laid the earth's foundation? Have you ever given orders to the morning? Can you bind the chains of the Pleiades? The questions went on for four chapters. Not an explanation. A revelation. Not the answer Job had asked for, but something better: a vision of the God who holds everything together.

Job said: my ears had heard of you but now my eyes have seen you. And God restored everything Job had lost, twice over. But the restoration was not the point. The point was the whirlwind, and the God who spoke from it, and the man who was broken and still would not let go.

devotional.bigIdea: Job never got an explanation for what happened to him. He got something better: an encounter with God. Sometimes God does not answer our questions about suffering. He shows up in the middle of it instead. That is not nothing. That is everything.
questions: Job's friends said he must have sinned for such terrible things to happen. God said they were wrong. Why do you think people want to explain suffering as punishment? What is wrong with that explanation?
Job was angry, honest, and loud with God about his pain. He was also still worshipping. What does it tell you about God that honest, angry prayer is not the same as faithlessness?
God never told Job why it happened. He showed Job who He was instead. Would you rather have an explanation or an encounter? What is the difference?

connections.title: The Lord Is My Shepherd
connections.why: Job said I know that my Redeemer lives. In the middle of his worst suffering, he held onto the same certainty David expressed in Psalm 23: that the shepherd knows where He is leading even when the path is dark.

connections.title: Come Out
connections.why: Jesus said Lazarus's illness was for God's glory, not explained ahead of time. Job's suffering was similarly purposeful without being explained to Job. Both stories ask us to trust a God who sees more than we see.

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Jesus said blessed are those who mourn, for they will be comforted. Job mourned honestly and without pretending. He is the oldest portrait in Scripture of the mourner Jesus was talking about.

quiz.question: What kind of man was Job before his suffering began?
options: A wealthy king who had conquered many nations
A prophet who had spoken against the king of Israel
A priest who served in the Temple in Jerusalem
A blameless and upright man who feared God described as the greatest in the East
quiz.explanation: Job was blameless and upright, feared God, and turned away from evil. God Himself described Job as the greatest man among all the people of the East.

quiz.question: In a single day, what did Job lose?
options: His sight, his hearing, and his ability to walk
His wife, his children, and all his animals
His wealth and all ten of his children
His health, his wealth, and his position in the community
quiz.explanation: Raiders took his animals, lightning killed his sheep, and a wind collapsed the house where all ten of his children were eating. Everything was gone in one day.

quiz.question: What did Job say after losing everything instead of cursing God?
options: The Lord gave and the Lord has taken away. May the name of the Lord be praised
I will continue to serve you even though I do not understand this
I know that you have a reason for this even if I cannot see it yet
Why have you abandoned me after I have served you faithfully?
quiz.explanation: Job tore his robe, shaved his head, fell to the ground in worship, and said: the Lord gave and the Lord has taken away. May the name of the Lord be praised.

quiz.question: What was wrong with what Job's three friends said to him?
options: They told Job to stop praying and accept that God was not listening
They gave easy explanations that his suffering must be punishment for sin, but they were wrong
They said God had abandoned Job and would not hear his prayers
They said Job should offer sacrifices to make God stop the suffering
quiz.explanation: Job's friends insisted his suffering must be punishment. God later rebuked them and said they had not spoken the truth about Him the way Job had.

quiz.question: When God answered Job from the whirlwind, what did He give Job?
options: A complete explanation of why each thing had happened to him
A promise that the suffering would end soon if Job kept trusting
A list of the sins Job had committed that had caused his suffering
A vision of His own greatness and wisdom rather than answers to Job's questions
quiz.explanation: God answered Job's questions with questions of His own, showing Job the vastness of creation and His power. Job never got an explanation. He got an encounter with God.

stories.title: The Servant Who Suffers
stories.hook: Seven hundred years before the cross, a prophet described it in detail. Who has believed what we heard? Who could have imagined this?
story: Isaiah was a prophet in Jerusalem seven hundred years before Jesus was born. He wrote more about the coming Messiah than any other prophet. And in chapters 52 and 53 he wrote something that has stopped people cold for three thousand years: a portrait of a suffering servant so detailed, so precise, so unlike anything anyone expected of the Messiah, that it sounds like it was written the morning after the crucifixion.

He wrote: see, my servant will act wisely. He will be raised and lifted up and highly exalted. And then immediately: just as there were many who were appalled at him, his appearance was so disfigured beyond that of any human being and his form marred beyond human likeness. The Messiah, everyone knew, would be a king. Isaiah said he would be unrecognizable.

He grew up before him like a tender shoot, and like a root out of dry ground. He had no beauty or majesty to attract us to him, nothing in his appearance that we should desire him. He was despised and rejected by mankind, a man of suffering, and familiar with pain. Like one from whom people hide their faces he was despised, and we held him in low esteem.

Surely he took up our pain and bore our suffering, yet we considered him punished by God, stricken by him, and afflicted. But he was pierced for our transgressions, he was crushed for our iniquities. The punishment that brought us peace was on him, and by his wounds we are healed.

We all, like sheep, have gone astray, each of us has turned to our own way. And the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all. He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth. He was led like a lamb to the slaughter. And as a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth.

He was assigned a grave with the wicked, and with the rich in his death, though he had done no violence, nor was any deceit in his mouth. Yet it was the Lord's will to crush him and cause him to suffer. After he has suffered, he will see the light of life and be satisfied. By his knowledge my righteous servant will justify many, and he will bear their iniquities.

Isaiah wrote these words and died. He never saw Jesus. He never saw the crucifixion. He never saw the empty tomb. He wrote seven hundred years too early to see any of it. And yet he described the crown of thorns, the silence before Pilate, the burial in a rich man's tomb, and the resurrection, each detail precise, each one landing in history exactly where he said it would. The scroll was read in synagogues every week for seven centuries. The Messiah everyone expected was a conqueror. Isaiah had been describing a lamb.

devotional.bigIdea: Isaiah described the cross seven hundred years before it happened. He was not guessing. The suffering servant was always the plan. The cross was not a tragedy that God turned into victory. It was the victory. It was always the victory.
questions: Isaiah said the servant was despised and rejected and that we held him in low esteem. Why do you think people who were looking for the Messiah missed Jesus when He came?
He was pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities. The punishment that brought us peace was on him. What does it mean to you personally that Jesus took what you deserved?
Isaiah wrote this without seeing it happen, trusting that God meant what He said. Is there a promise from God that you are holding onto right now without being able to see how it will come true?

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Every detail Isaiah wrote landed precisely at Golgotha. He was pierced for our transgressions. He was assigned a grave with the wicked and with the rich in his death. Isaiah was describing what he could not yet see.

connections.title: The Ark and the Rainbow
connections.why: We all like sheep have gone astray. The flood came because of human sin. Isaiah named the same problem and pointed to the same solution: one who would bear what we brought on ourselves.

connections.title: A Promise as Big as the Stars
connections.why: God told Abraham all nations would be blessed through him. Isaiah shows how: through a servant who would justify many and bear their iniquities. The promise to Abraham finds its fulfillment in Isaiah 53.

quiz.question: When did Isaiah write this description of the suffering servant?
options: Seven hundred years before Jesus was born
During the week Jesus was crucified in Jerusalem
About fifty years before Jesus was born
During the time of King David, four hundred years before Jesus
quiz.explanation: Isaiah prophesied around 700 BC, approximately seven hundred years before the crucifixion. He described events he could not have witnessed in precise detail.

quiz.question: What did Isaiah say about the servant's appearance?
options: He would wear royal robes that showed his authority from God
He had no beauty or majesty to attract people to him and was despised and rejected
He would glow with a heavenly light that marked him as divine
He would be tall and strong, inspiring fear in his enemies
quiz.explanation: Isaiah said the servant had no beauty or majesty, was despised and rejected, a man of suffering and familiar with pain. Nobody was expecting a Messiah who looked like that.

quiz.question: According to Isaiah, why did the servant suffer?
options: Because God was punishing him for the sins of the nation of Israel
Because he had chosen a life of poverty and service to the poor
Because his enemies were stronger than he was and overwhelmed him
Because he was pierced for our transgressions and crushed for our iniquities
quiz.explanation: He was pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities. The punishment that brought us peace was on him. He suffered in the place of others.

quiz.question: What did Isaiah say the servant would do when he was led to his suffering?
options: He would curse those who were harming him
He would explain to the crowd why he was suffering
He would not open his mouth, like a lamb led to the slaughter
He would call out to God to rescue him with a miracle
quiz.explanation: He was led like a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before its shearers is silent, he did not open his mouth. Jesus said nothing before Pilate that fulfilled this exactly.

quiz.question: What did Isaiah say would happen after the servant's suffering?
options: He would see the light of life and be satisfied, and justify many
The nation of Israel would finally be restored to its former greatness
His followers would be scattered and his memory would fade
A new prophet would arise to continue what the servant had started
quiz.explanation: After he has suffered, he will see the light of life and be satisfied. By his knowledge my righteous servant will justify many. Isaiah described the resurrection seven hundred years before it happened.

stories.title: The Furnace
stories.hook: The king heated the furnace seven times hotter than normal and threw three men in. He saw four men walking around inside it.
story: King Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon built a statue of gold ninety feet high. He assembled all the officials of his empire and issued a command. When the musicians play, everyone must bow down and worship the statue. Anyone who does not bow will immediately be thrown into a blazing furnace.

Three Jewish men in Nebuchadnezzar's service refused. Their names were Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego. They were friends of Daniel, the same young men who had refused to eat the king's food years earlier. When the music played and everyone else bowed, they stood.

Some Babylonian officials went immediately to the king. They told him the three men had defied his command. They had not bowed. Nebuchadnezzar was furious. He brought the three men before him and gave them one more chance. If you bow down when you hear the music, good. If you do not, you will be thrown into the blazing furnace. And then what god will be able to rescue you from my hand?

Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego did not waver. Their answer is one of the most remarkable statements in Scripture. King Nebuchadnezzar, we do not need to defend ourselves before you in this matter. If we are thrown into the blazing furnace, the God we serve is able to deliver us from it, and He will deliver us from your hand. But even if He does not, we want you to know that we will not serve your gods or worship the image of gold you have set up.

Even if He does not. They were not claiming a guarantee. They were not bargaining with God. They were stating plainly what they knew to be true: God can save us. And if He chooses not to, we still will not bow. That is not the words of people who believe God only when it is comfortable.

Nebuchadnezzar ordered the furnace heated seven times hotter than usual. The heat was so intense that the soldiers who threw the three men in were killed by the flames. But then the king leaped to his feet in amazement. He said: was it not three men we tied up and threw into the fire? Look, I see four men walking around in the fire, unbound and unharmed, and the fourth looks like a son of the gods.

He called them out. They walked out of the furnace. Not a hair on their heads was singed. Their robes were not scorched. There was no smell of fire on them at all. And Nebuchadnezzar praised the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, who had sent His angel and rescued His servants who trusted in Him.

devotional.bigIdea: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego said God can save us, but even if He does not, we still will not bow. That is faith at its deepest. Not faith that always gets the miracle, but faith that holds on whether the miracle comes or not.
questions: The three men said even if He does not save us, we will not bow. What do you think made them able to say that? Where does that kind of faith come from?
Nebuchadnezzar had all the power in the room. But the three men were not afraid of him. What does it look like in real life to fear God more than you fear people?
A fourth person appeared in the fire with them. Who do you think that was? What does it mean that God was with them in the fire rather than keeping them out of it?

connections.title: The Lion's Den
connections.why: Daniel's three friends refused to bow to the statue just as Daniel refused to stop praying. Both stories are about the same kind of faith: doing what is right regardless of the consequence.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: God promised through Isaiah: when you walk through the fire you will not be burned. At Golgotha, Jesus walked through the ultimate fire so that death itself would lose its power over those who belong to God.

connections.title: Singing at Midnight
connections.why: Nebuchadnezzar said the men trusted in God and defied the king's command. Paul and Silas did the same thing in a Philippian prison, worshipping when they should have been afraid, and God showed up there too.

quiz.question: What did King Nebuchadnezzar command everyone to do when the music played?
options: Bow down and worship the golden statue he had built
Bring a gift to the palace as a tribute to the empire
Stand at attention and salute the flag of Babylon
Recite an oath of loyalty to the king three times
quiz.explanation: Nebuchadnezzar built a golden statue ninety feet high and commanded everyone to bow down and worship it when the music played. The penalty for refusal was the furnace.

quiz.question: What did the three men say when Nebuchadnezzar gave them one more chance to bow?
options: They asked for more time to pray and seek an answer from God
They said they would bow if the king released the other Jewish exiles
They said nothing and simply stood in silence waiting for whatever came next
They said God is able to save us, but even if He does not, we will not bow
quiz.explanation: Their answer was remarkable: God can deliver us, and He will. But even if He does not, we will not serve your gods. No bargaining. No guarantees required.

quiz.question: What happened to the soldiers who threw the three men into the furnace?
options: They refused to obey the order and were arrested
They were killed by the intense heat of the fire
They fell unconscious from the smoke
They were blinded by the heat of the flames
quiz.explanation: The furnace had been heated seven times hotter than usual, and the heat killed the soldiers who threw the men in. But the three men themselves were unharmed inside.

quiz.question: What did Nebuchadnezzar see when he looked into the furnace?
options: Two men standing and one man kneeling in prayer
The men had vanished and the furnace was empty
Three men walking around with a fourth who looked like a son of the gods
The three men lying still on the floor of the furnace
quiz.explanation: The king leaped to his feet. He had thrown in three men tied up. He saw four men walking around unbound, and the fourth looked like a son of the gods.

quiz.question: What evidence was there that the men had been in the fire when they came out?
options: There was no evidence at all, not a hair singed, no smell of smoke
They moved slowly and their skin was red from the heat
Their robes were partially burned and their hair was singed
Their clothing was untouched but their sandals had burned away
quiz.explanation: Not a hair on their heads was singed. Their robes were not scorched. There was no smell of fire on them at all. The fire had touched nothing.

stories.title: The Lion's Den
stories.hook: They passed a law specifically designed to make Daniel's faith illegal. He prayed anyway.
story: Jerusalem had fallen. The Babylonians had destroyed the Temple, burned the city, and marched the people into exile. Among those taken was a young man named Daniel, selected for his intelligence and good appearance to be educated in the court of King Nebuchadnezzar. Daniel and three friends were given new Babylonian names and trained in all the learning of Babylon. They were being rebuilt into something else.

They refused to be entirely remade. Daniel asked permission not to eat the king's food, food that had been offered to idols and violated the dietary laws God had given Israel. The official in charge was afraid. If Daniel looked worse than the other young men, it could cost him his life. Daniel proposed a ten-day test. Give us vegetables and water and compare us to the others at the end. After ten days, Daniel and his friends looked healthier than any of the young men who had been eating the royal food. The official took away the king's food permanently.

Decades passed. Babylon fell to the Persians. A new king sat on the throne named Darius the Mede. Daniel had served faithfully through multiple kingdoms and risen to one of the highest positions in the empire. Darius planned to put him over the entire kingdom. The other administrators and satraps looked for something to use against him. They found nothing. He was not corrupt. He could not be bribed. There was no negligence in him. So they went after the only vulnerability they could find: his God.

They persuaded Darius to sign a decree. Thirty days, anyone who prays to any god or man except the king will be thrown into the den of lions. Darius signed it. A law of the Medes and Persians could not be changed, even by the king himself.

Daniel heard about the decree. He went home. And he did exactly what he had always done. Three times a day, he knelt at his upstairs window facing Jerusalem and prayed, giving thanks to God. He did not hide it. He did not change his routine. He just prayed. The administrators found him, reported him to Darius, and the king, deeply distressed, trying all day to find a way around his own law, had to give the order. May your God, whom you serve continually, rescue you.

Daniel was thrown into the lions' den. A stone was placed over the opening and sealed. Darius spent the night fasting, without entertainment, unable to sleep. At the first light of dawn he ran to the den and called out: Daniel, servant of the living God, has your God been able to rescue you from the lions? And Daniel answered from inside the den. My God sent His angel, and he shut the mouths of the lions. They have not hurt me, because I was found innocent in His sight.

Daniel was lifted out of the den. No wound was found on him, because he had trusted in his God. The administrators who had accused him were thrown in and the lions overpowered them instantly. Darius issued a decree across his entire empire. The God of Daniel is the living God and He endures forever. His kingdom will never be destroyed.

Daniel prayed the same prayer in the same place at the same time every single day for his entire life, whether under Nebuchadnezzar, Belshazzar, or Darius. He did not adjust his faithfulness based on the political climate. The lions could not touch a man that consistent, that surrendered, that unafraid.

devotional.bigIdea: Daniel's enemies couldn't find anything to use against him except his faithfulness to God. That is the best kind of life, so clean, so consistent, and so surrendered that the only vulnerability anyone can find is how much you love God.
questions: Daniel prayed the same prayer in the same place at the same time every day for his entire life. What does that kind of consistency with God look like for you?
