The administrators couldn't find corruption or negligence in Daniel, so they went after his faith. Is there anything in your life right now that someone opposed to God could use against you?
Darius said may your God rescue you, and then spent the whole night awake, hoping. What do you think he understood about Daniel's God by the end of that night?

connections.title: The Scarlet Thread
connections.why: Daniel was saved because he trusted in his God, the same faith that saved Rahab. Both were foreigners in enemy territory, both trusted God's rescue over human power, and both were saved completely.

connections.title: Speak, Lord
connections.why: Daniel prayed three times a day his whole life, the same posture as Samuel's speak Lord your servant is listening. Both men were defined by a lifetime of consistent availability to God.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Hebrews lists Daniel among those who by faith shut the mouths of lions. The rescue from the den was a shadow of the greater rescue, the stone rolled away from an even darker tomb.

quiz.question: Why did Daniel's enemies decide to target his faith instead of his work?
options: Because they could find no corruption or negligence in him, his work was perfect
Because Daniel had publicly offended them by refusing to eat with them
Because they were jealous of how much the king trusted him
quiz.explanation: The administrators looked everywhere for something to use against Daniel. They found nothing, he was not corrupt, not negligent, not bribable. The only vulnerability they could find was his God.

quiz.question: What did Daniel do when he heard about the decree forbidding prayer?
options: He prayed secretly in a hidden room so nobody could see him
He stopped praying temporarily until the thirty days were over
He went home and prayed exactly as he always had, three times a day at his open window
quiz.explanation: Daniel went home and did exactly what he had always done, three times a day, at his open window, facing Jerusalem. He didn't hide it. He didn't change his routine. He just prayed.

quiz.question: What did Darius say to Daniel before he was thrown into the den of lions?
options: I will change this law as soon as the thirty days are finished
I am sorry, I had no choice but to follow the law
May your God whom you serve continually rescue you
quiz.explanation: Darius said may your God whom you serve continually rescue you, then spent the whole night awake, fasting, hoping. He already knew something about Daniel's God before the morning came.

quiz.question: What did Darius do all night while Daniel was in the lions' den?
options: He drafted a new law to protect the Jews in his empire
He fasted, refused entertainment, and could not sleep
He prayed to the God of Israel for the first time
He held a feast to pretend nothing had happened
quiz.explanation: Darius fasted and could not sleep, the pagan king was more troubled by Daniel's situation than he was. God uses unlikely people to bear witness.

quiz.question: What did Daniel do three times every day that was his consistent practice?
options: Prayed toward Jerusalem with his windows open
Offered a sacrifice on a small altar in his room
Fasted and went without food
Read from the Hebrew Scriptures
quiz.explanation: Daniel prayed three times a day facing Jerusalem, the same practice he had kept his whole life. When the decree came, he did not change a thing.

stories.title: For Such a Time as This
stories.hook: A Jewish girl became queen of Persia and had to decide whether to risk her life to save her people.
story: The Jewish people were scattered across the Persian empire, living in exile far from the land God had promised them. In the capital city of Susa, a young Jewish woman named Esther lived with her older cousin Mordecai, who had raised her after her parents died. She was beautiful. She was brave. And for now, she kept her nationality a secret, following Mordecai's instructions.

King Ahasuerus, known in history as Xerxes, held a search across the empire for a new queen. Esther was brought to the palace. She won the favor of everyone who saw her. After twelve months of preparation, she was brought before the king. He loved her more than all the other women, set a royal crown on her head, and made her queen. Esther was now inside the most powerful palace in the world, and almost nobody knew she was Jewish.

Then a man named Haman rose to the highest position in the empire, second only to the king. Everyone bowed to Haman. Everyone except Mordecai, who would not bow to any man the way God's people bowed to God. This infuriated Haman. When he found out Mordecai was Jewish, he decided that punishing one man was not enough. He would destroy all of them, every Jewish person in the entire Persian empire.

Haman went to the king, told him there was a people in the empire who did not obey the king's laws, and received permission to issue a decree of extermination. Letters were sent to every province. On a single day, thirteen months away, all Jewish people, young and old, women and children, were to be killed. The city of Susa was bewildered. Mordecai tore his clothes and wept in the street.

He sent word to Esther. You must go to the king and beg for your people. Esther sent word back. Everyone knows that going to the king without being summoned means death unless he extends his golden scepter. I have not been summoned in thirty days. Mordecai's reply became one of the most famous sentences ever written. Do not think that because you are in the king's house you alone of all the Jews will escape. If you remain silent at this time, relief and deliverance for the Jews will arise from another place, but you and your father's family will perish. And who knows whether you have not come to the kingdom for such a time as this?

Esther asked Mordecai to gather all the Jews in Susa and fast for three days on her behalf. She and her maids would do the same. Then she said: I will go to the king, even though it is against the law. And if I perish, I perish.

She went. The king extended his golden scepter. She was safe. Over two banquets, with careful timing, Esther revealed what Haman had planned. The king's anger burned. Haman was executed on the very gallows he had prepared for Mordecai. A new decree was issued allowing the Jewish people to defend themselves. Mordecai was honored throughout the empire. And every year since, in a festival called Purim, the Jewish people have remembered the queen who walked into the throne room when she did not have to.

devotional.bigIdea: Esther didn't know if she would survive going to the king. She went anyway. If I perish, I perish, five words that changed the history of a people. Courage is not the absence of fear. It is going forward when you don't know the outcome.
questions: Mordecai told Esther: who knows whether you have not come to the kingdom for such a time as this? Do you believe God places people in specific places at specific times for specific purposes? What might that mean for your own life?
Esther said if I perish, I perish, and then she went. What do you think gave her the courage to go when she knew the risk?
Esther asked the whole Jewish community to fast for her for three days before she acted. What does it tell you about courage that she prepared that way instead of just going immediately?

connections.title: Wherever You Go
connections.why: Both Ruth and Esther were women who risked everything for the people they loved. Ruth said where you go I will go. Esther said if I perish, I perish. Both women's courage changed history.

connections.title: The Man Who Hunted Christians
connections.why: Mordecai said deliverance will come from another place, he trusted that God's plan was bigger than any one person. Paul discovered the same thing: God's purposes cannot be stopped by human opposition.

connections.title: Impossible News
connections.why: Mary said let it be to me according to your word, the same surrender as Esther's if I perish I perish. Both women stepped into something terrifying because they trusted the God who had placed them there.

quiz.question: Why was it dangerous for Esther to go to the king without being summoned?
options: The king was known to be cruel and unpredictable with visitors
Haman had issued an order that no one could see the king without his permission
Anyone who approached the king unsummoned was put to death unless the king extended his golden scepter
quiz.explanation: Persian law was absolute, approaching the king without being summoned meant death unless he chose to extend the golden scepter. Esther had not been summoned in thirty days. She went anyway.

quiz.question: What did Esther ask Mordecai and the Jewish people to do before she went to the king?
options: Fast for three days on her behalf, while she and her maids did the same
Pray at the Temple in Jerusalem for her safety
Prepare a plan to escape the empire if her mission failed
quiz.explanation: Esther asked for three days of fasting from all the Jewish people in Susa and from herself. She prepared through prayer and fasting before she acted. Courage and prayer went together.

quiz.question: What happened to Haman at the end of the story?
options: He was stripped of his position and forced to serve Mordecai
He was exiled to a distant province of the empire
He was executed on the very gallows he had built to hang Mordecai
quiz.explanation: Haman was executed on the gallows he had built for Mordecai. The trap he set for the Jewish people fell on him instead. This is a pattern throughout Scripture, those who set traps for God's people often fall into them.

quiz.question: What did Mordecai tell Esther when she hesitated to go to the king?
options: That the Jewish people would fight their own battles if she refused
That deliverance would come another way if she stayed silent, but she would not escape
That the king would certainly spare her life because she was queen
That God would protect her because she was righteous
quiz.explanation: Mordecai said if she stayed silent, deliverance would come another way, but she and her family would not escape. Then came the famous line: "Who knows whether you have not come to the kingdom for such a time as this?"

quiz.question: What was the name of the law Esther and Mordecai persuaded the king to make?
options: A proclamation honoring Mordecai throughout the empire
A decree canceling Haman's original order
A new decree allowing Jews to fight back and defend themselves
A law making Judaism the official religion of Persia
quiz.explanation: Since Persian law could not be reversed, the king issued a second decree allowing the Jews to arm themselves and fight back, and on the day of the attack, they won.

stories.title: The Man Who Ran From God
stories.hook: God told a prophet to go east. He boarded a ship going west. You cannot outrun the God who made the ocean.
story: God spoke to a prophet named Jonah with a clear assignment. Go to the great city of Nineveh and preach against it, because its wickedness has come up before me. Nineveh was the capital of Assyria, the most brutal empire in the ancient world, Israel's most feared enemy. Jonah heard the assignment clearly and went in exactly the opposite direction.

He went to Joppa and found a ship going to Tarshish, as far west as ships went. He paid the fare and went aboard, going away from the Lord. He went below deck and fell asleep. This is worth noticing: Jonah was running from God so hard that he fell asleep. The peace of a bad decision can feel indistinguishable from the peace of a right one, for a little while.

God sent a great wind on the sea. The storm was so violent the ship threatened to break apart. The sailors cried out to their own gods and threw cargo overboard to lighten the ship. And the prophet of the Lord slept below deck until the captain shook him awake. Get up and call on your god! Maybe he will notice us so we will not perish.

They cast lots to find out who was responsible for the disaster. The lot fell on Jonah. He admitted everything. I am a Hebrew and I worship the Lord, the God of heaven, who made the sea and the dry land. The sailors were terrified. Jonah told them to throw him overboard. The sea will calm down. This storm is because of me.

The sailors tried to row back to land. They could not. The storm grew worse. Finally they threw Jonah overboard. The sea became calm immediately. The sailors stood in awe and offered sacrifices to the Lord.

The Lord provided a great fish to swallow Jonah. Inside the fish, in the dark, Jonah prayed. He said: in my distress I called to the Lord, and He answered me. From the depths of the grave I called for help and you listened to my cry. Salvation comes from the Lord. After three days and three nights, the fish vomited Jonah onto dry land.

God spoke to Jonah a second time. Go to Nineveh. This time Jonah went. He walked into the city and proclaimed: forty more days and Nineveh will be overthrown. The Ninevites believed God. They declared a fast from the greatest to the least. The king sat in the dust and called the whole city to repent. When God saw that they turned from their evil ways, He relented from the destruction He had threatened.

Jonah was furious. This is exactly what I was afraid you would do, he told God. You are gracious and compassionate, slow to anger and abounding in love. So take my life, because it is better for me to die than to live. He sat outside the city sulking, hoping God would destroy it after all. God gave him a plant for shade, then took it away, and then said: you cared about this plant that you did not plant or tend. Should I not care about Nineveh, that great city? The book ends there, with the question hanging in the air.

devotional.bigIdea: Jonah ran from an assignment he did not want and ended up in the belly of a fish. God brought him back and gave him the same assignment again. God does not give up on the people He has called, even when those people are actively trying to escape.
questions: Jonah ran from God because he did not want Nineveh to be forgiven. Have you ever not wanted God to be gracious to someone? What was underneath that feeling?
Jonah prayed his most honest prayer from inside a fish in the dark. Why do you think people often pray most honestly when they are in the worst situations?
The book ends without telling us whether Jonah changed his mind about Nineveh. Why do you think the story ends with a question rather than a resolution?

connections.title: The Boy Who Came Home
connections.why: Jonah was angry that God was gracious and compassionate. The older brother in the prodigal son story was angry that his father threw a party. Both stories expose the same thing: our resistance to grace we did not expect.

connections.title: The Empty Tomb
connections.why: Jesus said just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the fish, so the Son of Man will be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth. Jonah's story pointed to the resurrection.

connections.title: The Man Who Hunted Christians
connections.why: Both Jonah and Paul were running in the wrong direction when God stopped them. Both were given a second chance and sent to people they would not naturally have chosen.

quiz.question: Why did Jonah run away from God's assignment to go to Nineveh?
options: He was afraid the Ninevites would kill him before he could preach
He did not believe God had actually spoken to him
He did not want Nineveh to repent and be forgiven because they were Israel's enemy
He had a previous commitment in Tarshish he could not break
quiz.explanation: Jonah admits at the end of the book that he ran because he knew God was compassionate and might forgive Nineveh. He did not want Israel's brutal enemy to receive God's mercy.

quiz.question: What happened to the storm when the sailors threw Jonah overboard?
options: It became even worse before finally stopping
It stopped immediately and the sea became calm
It gradually calmed over the next few hours
It continued for another day and then ended naturally
quiz.explanation: The moment they threw Jonah overboard the sea became calm. The sailors were so amazed they offered sacrifices to the Lord and made vows.

quiz.question: What did Jonah do inside the fish?
options: He tried to find a way to escape from inside the fish
He slept for most of the three days just as he had slept on the ship
He was unconscious for the entire three days
He prayed honestly to God acknowledging that salvation comes from the Lord
quiz.explanation: Jonah prayed from inside the fish. He acknowledged where he was and that God had heard him even from there. Salvation comes from the Lord.

quiz.question: What was Jonah's reaction when Nineveh actually repented?
options: He was furious and told God he would rather die than see Nineveh forgiven
He was relieved that his dangerous mission was over
He left quickly before the city could change its mind again
He was amazed and grateful that God had used him so powerfully
quiz.explanation: Jonah was angry. He told God this was exactly what he had feared, that God would show compassion. He sat outside the city hoping God would destroy it anyway.

quiz.question: Jesus used Jonah's story as a picture of what future event?
options: His forty days of fasting in the wilderness
His triumphant entry into Jerusalem
His death and resurrection after three days
His baptism in the Jordan River
quiz.explanation: Jesus said just as Jonah was three days in the belly of the fish, the Son of Man would be three days in the heart of the earth. Jonah's story pointed forward to the resurrection.

stories.title: The Wall Goes Up
stories.hook: Jerusalem's walls had been rubble for 140 years. A cupbearer to the Persian king asked for permission to go rebuild them. The wall went up in 52 days.
story: Nehemiah was a Jewish man living in exile, serving as cupbearer to Artaxerxes, king of Persia. It was one of the most trusted positions in the royal court. When visitors came from Jerusalem, Nehemiah asked about the city and the people who had survived. The news was devastating. The wall of Jerusalem is broken down and its gates have been burned with fire.

Nehemiah sat down and wept. He mourned for days. And then he prayed, a long honest prayer confessing Israel's sins, reminding God of His promises, and asking for success. He did not ask to leave his post. He asked for the chance to serve. Then he went back to his job and waited.

Four months later, the king noticed that Nehemiah's face was sad. He asked what was wrong. Nehemiah was terrified. A cupbearer was supposed to be invisible, never bringing his own sorrows into the royal presence. But he prayed a quick prayer in his heart and then answered honestly. The city where my ancestors are buried lies in ruins. The king asked: what do you want?

Nehemiah asked for everything he needed. Permission to go. Letters to the governors of the regions he would pass through. A letter to the keeper of the royal forest requesting timber for the gates. The king granted it all. Nehemiah arrived in Jerusalem with royal authority and a plan.

He did not announce himself immediately. He waited three days, then rode around the city walls at night, alone, examining the damage. Then he called the leaders together and told them what he intended to do and how God had been at work to make it possible. They said: let us start rebuilding. And they put their hands to the good work.

The opposition came immediately. Two men named Sanballat and Tobiah mocked the project openly. What are these feeble Jews doing? Even a fox would break down their stone wall. Nehemiah's response was to pray and keep building. When the wall reached half its height, the opposition threatened violence. Nehemiah armed the builders. Half worked while half held spears, and Nehemiah told them: do not be afraid. Remember the Lord, who is great and awesome.

The wall was completed in fifty-two days. When the enemies and all the surrounding nations heard about it, they were afraid and lost their self-confidence, because they realized that this work had been done with the help of God. A city that had been rubble for a hundred and forty years had its walls standing again in less than two months.

devotional.bigIdea: Nehemiah prayed, and then he planned. He wept, and then he worked. He asked God for help, and then he built the wall. Real faith does not just ask God to do something. It asks God for help and then gets up and does the work alongside Him.
questions: Nehemiah prayed before he answered the king, right in the middle of the conversation. What does that tell you about what prayer can look like in everyday life?

The enemies mocked the project and threatened violence, but Nehemiah kept building. What is the difference between a threat that should stop you and one that you should just pray about and keep going?
The wall went up in 52 days after sitting in rubble for 140 years. Is there something broken in your life or your community that feels impossible to rebuild? What would the first step be?

connections.title: For Such a Time as This
connections.why: Both Esther and Nehemiah were Jewish people in positions of influence in foreign courts who used that position to serve their people. Both prayed before speaking to the king.

connections.title: The Burning Bush
connections.why: Nehemiah's prayer begins: Lord, God of heaven, the great and awesome God. Both Moses and Nehemiah were ordinary people given extraordinary assignments by the same God.

connections.title: The Walls Come Down
connections.why: Joshua's generation tore down walls by faith in God. Nehemiah's generation built walls back up by faith in the same God. Both stories are about what is possible when people remember who they are working with.

quiz.question: What was Nehemiah's job before he went to rebuild Jerusalem?
options: A military commander in the Persian army
A cupbearer to the king of Persia
A merchant who traded between Jerusalem and Persia
A scribe who copied the laws of Persia
quiz.explanation: Nehemiah was cupbearer to King Artaxerxes, one of the most trusted positions in the royal court. He tasted the king's wine to protect him from poison.

quiz.question: What did Nehemiah do when he heard about the state of Jerusalem's walls?
options: He sat down, wept, mourned for days, and prayed
He sent a letter to the leaders in Jerusalem with instructions
He gathered other Jewish exiles and began planning a secret return
He immediately asked the king for permission to go to Jerusalem
quiz.explanation: Nehemiah sat down and wept. He mourned for days, and then he prayed a long honest prayer before doing anything else.

quiz.question: What did Nehemiah do at night when he first arrived in Jerusalem?
options: He prayed at the ruins of the Temple before doing anything else
He called all the leaders together to announce his plan immediately
He rode around the city walls alone in the dark to examine the damage
He met secretly with the priests to plan the rebuilding
quiz.explanation: Nehemiah waited three days, then rode out at night alone to inspect the walls. He told no one what God had put in his heart to do until he had assessed the situation himself.

quiz.question: How did Nehemiah respond when the enemies mocked the project and threatened violence?
options: He went out to meet the enemies and negotiate a peace agreement
He stopped building and waited for the threats to pass
He asked the king of Persia to send soldiers to protect the workers
He prayed and stationed armed guards while the builders kept working
quiz.explanation: Nehemiah prayed and armed the builders. Half worked while half held spears. He told them: do not be afraid. Remember the Lord, who is great and awesome.

quiz.question: How long did it take to rebuild the walls of Jerusalem?
options: One year despite constant opposition from surrounding nations
Fifty-two days which amazed all the surrounding nations
Six months working day and night with the whole city helping
Three years because of the scale of the destruction
quiz.explanation: The wall was completed in fifty-two days. When Israel's enemies heard about it, they were afraid and lost their self-confidence because they realized God had made it possible.

stories.title: Impossible News
stories.hook: A teenage girl in a forgotten village got a visit from an angel. What she said next would change everything.
story: Nazareth was a small town in Galilee, not on anyone's map of important places. In that town lived a young woman named Mary, pledged to be married to a man named Joseph, a descendant of King David. She was probably a teenager. She was nobody in particular by the world's reckoning. God had been watching her her whole life.

An angel named Gabriel appeared to her. Not in a dream, but in the room. Mary was frightened. Gabriel said: do not be afraid, Mary. You have found favor with God. You will conceive and give birth to a son, and you are to call him Jesus. He will be great and will be called the Son of the Most High. The Lord God will give him the throne of his father David, and his kingdom will never end.

Mary asked one practical question. How will this be, since I am a virgin? Not an objection, not a refusal. Just a question from someone trying to understand. Gabriel answered: the Holy Spirit will come on you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you. So the holy one to be born will be called the Son of God. And Elizabeth your relative, who everyone said was too old to have children, is already six months pregnant. For nothing is impossible with God.

Mary's answer has echoed across two thousand years. I am the Lord's servant. May it be to me according to your word. She was saying yes to something she did not fully understand, to a road she could not see to the end of, to a cost she could not yet calculate. She said yes anyway.

She went immediately to visit Elizabeth in the hill country of Judea. When Mary walked in the door and called out a greeting, the baby inside Elizabeth leaped. Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit and cried out: blessed are you among women, and blessed is the child you will bear! Why am I so favored, that the mother of my Lord should come to me? The baby in my womb leaped for joy the moment your greeting reached my ears.

And Mary sang. A song that turned the world upside down in four verses. God has brought down rulers from their thrones and lifted up the humble. He has filled the hungry with good things and sent the rich away empty. He has helped his servant Israel, remembering his mercy. The song of a teenage girl from a nothing town in Galilee, announcing that the whole order of the world was about to change.

Nine months later, in Bethlehem because of a Roman census, with no room in any inn, in a stable used for animals, Mary gave birth to her firstborn son. She wrapped him in cloths and placed him in a manger. The angels had called him the Son of the Most High. For tonight, he was wrapped in cloth and lying in a feeding trough. That is how God entered the world. Not with power, not with ceremony, not from the top down. As a baby in a barn, born to a teenage girl who had said yes.

devotional.bigIdea: Mary said yes to something she didn't fully understand, on a road she couldn't see to the end of, at a cost she couldn't yet calculate. That is what faith looks like, not certainty, but surrender.
questions: Gabriel told Mary nothing is impossible with God. Is there something in your life right now that feels impossible? What would it mean to actually believe that?
Mary said I am the Lord's servant, may it be to me according to your word. She said yes before she knew what it would cost. What would it mean for you to say that same prayer today?
Mary's song says God lifts up the humble and fills the hungry. What does that tell you about what God values, and what He is working toward in the world?

connections.title: For Such a Time as This
connections.why: Mary said let it be to me according to your word, the same surrender as Esther's if I perish I perish. Both women said yes to God before they knew the cost.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Simeon told Mary when Jesus was eight days old that a sword would pierce her own soul. The cost of her yes was already on its way. She carried it all the way to the cross.

connections.title: The Empty Tomb
connections.why: Gabriel said his kingdom will never end, and the resurrection is the moment that promise became undeniable. The baby in the manger rose from the dead.

quiz.question: What was Mary's one question when Gabriel told her she would have a son?
options: How will this be since I am a virgin?
What will happen to Joseph when he finds out?
Why has God chosen someone as unimportant as me?
quiz.explanation: Mary asked one practical question, how will this be since I am a virgin? Not a refusal, not a demand for proof. Just a question from someone trying to understand. Gabriel answered her honestly.

quiz.question: What happened when Mary greeted Elizabeth?
options: Mary told Elizabeth what the angel had said and Elizabeth wept with joy
The baby inside Elizabeth leaped and Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit
Elizabeth immediately knew Mary was pregnant because of a vision
quiz.explanation: The moment Mary's greeting reached Elizabeth, the baby inside her leaped. Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit and knew immediately, blessed are you among women, and blessed is the child you will bear.

quiz.question: Where was Jesus born and why?
options: In Jerusalem, because Mary wanted the baby to be born near the Temple
In Nazareth, because Mary could not travel in her condition
In Bethlehem, because a Roman census required Joseph to register in his hometown
quiz.explanation: A Roman census required everyone to register in their hometown. Joseph was from the line of David, so he went to Bethlehem, David's city. That is how a prophecy written 700 years earlier was fulfilled.

quiz.question: What did Gabriel tell Mary about her relative Elizabeth?
options: That Elizabeth who was called barren was six months pregnant, nothing is impossible with God
That Elizabeth was praying for Mary and had seen a vision of the coming child
That Elizabeth was the one God had originally chosen to be the mother of the Messiah
That Elizabeth had died and Mary should go comfort her family
quiz.explanation: Gabriel pointed to Elizabeth as proof, if God could open a barren old woman's womb, He could certainly do what He was doing with Mary.

quiz.question: What was the name Gabriel gave the child, and what does it mean?
options: Messiah, the anointed one
Immanuel, God is near
Emmanuel, God with us
Jesus, the Lord saves
quiz.explanation: The name Jesus comes from the Hebrew Yeshua, meaning "the Lord saves", His name announced His mission before He drew a single breath.

stories.title: Left Behind in Jerusalem
stories.hook: Jesus was twelve years old when His parents lost Him for three terrifying days, and found Him exactly where He said He would be.
story: Every year, Jewish families who could make the journey traveled to Jerusalem for the Passover feast. It was a week-long pilgrimage with dusty roads, large caravans of family and neighbors, and children running between the groups. Jesus's family made this journey every year. When he was twelve years old, they went as usual.

After the feast was over, the family began the journey home to Nazareth. The caravan was large enough that a twelve-year-old boy could reasonably be assumed to be with relatives or friends in another part of the group. Mary and Joseph walked a full day before they realized Jesus was not with them. They turned back immediately. Three days of searching. Three days of growing fear, in Jerusalem, looking for one boy.

They found him in the Temple courts. Not lost. Not frightened. Sitting in the middle of the teachers, listening to them and asking them questions. Everyone who heard him was amazed at his understanding and his answers. He had been there the entire time, engaged in the most serious religious conversation the Temple had ever hosted, at twelve years old.

Mary said what any mother would say. Child, why have you treated us like this? Your father and I have been anxiously searching for you! His answer is the first recorded sentence Jesus ever spoke in Scripture. Why were you searching for me? Didn't you know I had to be in my Father's house? He was not being dismissive. He was genuinely surprised they had not understood. Of course this is where He would be.

They did not understand what he meant. Mary stored all these things in her heart, the same phrase Luke used when the shepherds came on the night of His birth. She kept collecting moments she could not yet interpret, holding them the way you hold something precious and fragile, waiting for the day they would make sense.

Then Jesus went with them back to Nazareth. And the text says He was obedient to them. The Son of God, who had just held his own in debate with the greatest teachers in Israel, went home and submitted to the authority of a carpenter and his wife. Not because he had to. Because that is what God looks like when He takes on human flesh. He did not exempt himself from the ordinary. He submitted to parents, to community, to the slow rhythm of growing up.

Eighteen years of silence after this moment. Eighteen years of sawdust and ordinary days in Nazareth before His public ministry began. The God who made the universe spent more than ninety percent of His earthly life in obscurity, learning a trade, honoring His parents, growing up. That is worth sitting with for a long time.

devotional.bigIdea: Jesus spent eighteen years of obscurity after this moment, learning a trade, honoring His parents, growing up. God is not in a hurry. He does not skip the ordinary. The slow seasons are not wasted seasons.
questions: Jesus was surprised that Mary and Joseph didn't know where to find him. What does that tell you about how naturally Jesus oriented himself toward His Father?

Mary kept storing things in her heart that she didn't yet understand. Is there something in your life right now that you're holding without understanding? What does Mary's example teach you about how to carry it?
Jesus went back to Nazareth and was obedient to His parents for eighteen more years. What does it mean that the Son of God submitted himself to ordinary life and ordinary authority?

connections.title: Impossible News
connections.why: Mary stored all these things in her heart, the same phrase used when the shepherds came at the birth. She kept collecting moments she couldn't yet interpret, waiting for them to make sense.

connections.title: Speak, Lord
connections.why: Jesus increased in wisdom and stature and in favor with God and man, the exact same phrase used of Samuel growing up in the tabernacle. Both boys grew up in God's house.

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Jesus began his public ministry at about thirty years old. This Temple visit at twelve is his only recorded appearance in those eighteen years between, eighteen years of ordinary life before everything changed.

quiz.question: How long did Mary and Joseph search for Jesus before they found him?
options: Seven days, they searched the whole city of Jerusalem
One day, they found him that same evening
Three days, they had walked a full day before realizing he was missing
quiz.explanation: They walked a full day before realizing Jesus wasn't with them, then turned back immediately. Three days of searching before they found him in the Temple courts, not lost, not frightened, in deep conversation with the teachers.

quiz.question: What was Jesus doing when Mary and Joseph finally found him?
options: Sitting with the teachers, listening and asking questions that amazed everyone
Praying alone in a quiet corner of the Temple
Helping the priests prepare for the daily sacrifices
quiz.explanation: Jesus was sitting in the middle of the teachers, listening and asking questions. Everyone who heard him was amazed at his understanding and his answers. He was twelve years old.

quiz.question: What did Jesus do after this incident in the Temple?
options: He went to the Jordan River to be baptized by John
He went with his family back to Nazareth and was obedient to them
He stayed in Jerusalem to continue learning from the teachers
quiz.explanation: Jesus went back to Nazareth and was obedient to his parents. The Son of God submitted to human authority, a carpenter and his wife, for eighteen more ordinary years. He did not skip the slow seasons.

quiz.question: How old was Jesus when this event happened?
options: Eight years old
Fifteen years old
Ten years old
Twelve years old
quiz.explanation: Jesus was twelve, the age when a Jewish boy began preparing for his bar mitzvah, his formal entry into the life of the community. This was the threshold of young manhood.

quiz.question: What did Jesus mean when He said "I must be in my Father's house"?
options: That He needed to finish learning from the teachers before He could leave
That God had given him a specific assignment to complete in Jerusalem
That His first loyalty and deepest identity was His relationship with God His Father
That He preferred the Temple to the journey
quiz.explanation: Jesus was twelve years old and already clear about who He was and who His Father was. Even Mary and Joseph did not fully understand, but Jesus knew.

stories.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
stories.location: Mount of Beatitudes, Sea of Galilee
stories.hook: Jesus sat on a hillside overlooking the Sea of Galilee and delivered the most important speech in human history. Everything the world called blessed, He called backwards.
story: Large crowds had been following Jesus from Galilee, from the Decapolis, from Jerusalem and Judea and even from across the Jordan. They came because of the healings. They had heard what He could do. When Jesus saw the crowds, He went up on a mountainside, sat down, and began to teach. What He said turned everything upside down.

He started with eight statements, each beginning with the word blessed, meaning deeply happy, fully alive, flourishing in the truest sense. And each one was a paradox. Blessed are the poor in spirit, those who know they have nothing to bring God, no spiritual currency, no leverage. Theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are those who mourn. Blessed are the meek. Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness. Blessed are the merciful, the pure in heart, the peacemakers. Blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness.

The world had always said: blessed are the powerful, the wealthy, the successful, the ones who win. Jesus said no. The ones who are blessed are the ones who know their need, who grieve over what is broken, who are gentle, who want what God wants, who make peace instead of war, who are willing to suffer for what is right. The kingdom of God runs on entirely different currency than the kingdom of the world.

He kept going. You have heard it said do not murder, but I say to you, do not even be angry at your brother without cause. You have heard it said do not commit adultery, but I say to you, do not look at someone with lust. You have heard it said love your neighbor and hate your enemy, but I say to you, love your enemies. Pray for those who persecute you. The Law had set a floor. Jesus was calling people to a ceiling, not just the behavior but the heart behind it.

He talked about prayer. Do not perform it for an audience, he said. Go into your room, close the door, and pray to your Father who sees in secret. He gave them a model prayer. Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come, your will be done on earth as it is in heaven. Give us today our daily bread. Forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors. Lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil. A prayer that holds everything a person needs in seven short lines.

He talked about worry. He pointed to the birds. They do not sow or reap or store in barns, and your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not much more valuable than they? He pointed to the wildflowers. They do not labor or spin, yet Solomon in all his glory was not dressed like one of these. If that is how God clothes the grass of the field, how much more will He clothe you? Do not worry about tomorrow. Tomorrow has enough trouble of its own.

He closed with a choice. Two gates, two roads, two builders, two houses. One house was built on rock. When the storms came, it stood. One house was built on sand. When the storms came, it collapsed completely. Everyone who hears these words of mine, Jesus said, and puts them into practice is like the man who built on the rock. The storms are not optional. What you build on is.

When Jesus had finished speaking, the crowds were astonished at his teaching, because He taught as one who had authority, and not as their teachers of the law. He was not quoting sources. He was not appealing to tradition. He said: you have heard it said, but I say to you. He spoke as though the words were His to give. Because they were.

devotional.bigIdea: Jesus didn't come to lower the bar, He raised it from behavior to the heart. He wasn't interested in people who just managed their actions. He was after the transformation of everything underneath.
questions: Which of the Beatitudes surprises you most, and why? Which one do you most want to be true of you?
Jesus said the storms are coming for everyone, the question is what you're building on. What does it look like in real life to build on the rock instead of the sand?
Jesus told people to love their enemies and pray for those who persecute them. Has someone ever treated you the way Jesus describes? What would it actually take to respond the way He says?

connections.title: The Ten Commandments
connections.why: Jesus said I did not come to abolish the Law but to fulfill it. The Sermon on the Mount is Jesus completing what Moses started on Sinai, taking the Law from stone tablets to human hearts.

connections.title: The Boy Who Came Home
connections.why: Jesus teaches the Lord's Prayer, Our Father. The prodigal son's journey home is the lived-out version of that prayer: coming to a Father who runs toward us, not away.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Jesus said love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you. From the cross, He prayed: Father, forgive them. He didn't just teach it. He did it.

quiz.question: What word did Jesus use to begin each of the eight Beatitudes?
options: Holy
Blessed
Worthy
quiz.explanation: Each Beatitude begins with blessed, meaning deeply happy, fully alive, flourishing in the truest sense. And each one is a paradox: the poor in spirit, the mourning, the meek, these are the ones Jesus calls blessed.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say about worry when he pointed to the birds and the wildflowers?
options: He said worry shows a lack of faith and must be confessed as sin
He said worry is natural but prayer is the solution to overcome it
He said God feeds the birds and clothes the flowers, how much more will He care for you
quiz.explanation: Jesus pointed to birds and flowers as evidence of the Father's care, they don't worry, and God provides for them. Are you not much more valuable than they? Don't worry about tomorrow. Tomorrow has enough trouble of its own.

quiz.question: What is the difference between the two builders at the end of the Sermon on the Mount?
options: One built on rock and one built on sand, same storm hit both, only the rock house stood
One built a large house and one built a small house
One used good materials and one used poor materials
quiz.explanation: Both builders faced the same storm. The difference was what they built on. Jesus said anyone who hears his words and puts them into practice is like the man who built on rock. The storms are not optional, what you build on is.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say about the law, did He come to destroy it?
options: He said the old law no longer applied now that He had come
He said only the Ten Commandments still mattered
He said He had not come to abolish the law but to fulfill it
He said the law was a good starting point but love was better
quiz.explanation: Jesus said He had not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets but to fulfill them, and that not one letter would pass away until all was accomplished.

quiz.question: What did Jesus teach about loving your enemies that was completely radical?
options: That loving enemies was a noble ideal even if impossible in practice
That you should love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you
That enemies should be forgiven once they repented
That you should pray for your enemies from a safe distance
quiz.explanation: Jesus said love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you, so that you may be sons of your Father in heaven who sends rain on the just and unjust alike.

stories.title: The Woman at the Well
stories.hook: Jesus was tired and sat down at a well. What happened next changed an entire town.
story: Jews did not go through Samaria. It was the long way around, but it was the way most people chose, because Jews and Samaritans despised each other and had for centuries. Jesus chose to go through Samaria. The Gospel says He had to. Not because it was the shorter route, but because there was someone there He needed to meet.

He arrived at a well outside the town of Sychar around noon. His disciples had gone into the town to buy food. Jesus sat down at the well, tired from the journey. A Samaritan woman came to draw water. She came at noon, not in the morning when the other women came together. The middle of the day, alone, in the heat. There are reasons a woman comes to a well at noon instead of with everyone else.

Jesus asked her for a drink. She was startled. Jews do not ask Samaritans for anything, and men do not speak to women alone in public. She said: how is it that you, a Jew, ask for a drink from me, a Samaritan woman? Jesus answered: if you knew the gift of God, and who it is that is saying to you give me a drink, you would have asked him, and he would have given you living water.

She thought He was talking about actual water. She pointed out that He had nothing to draw with and the well was deep. Where do you get this living water? Are you greater than our father Jacob, who gave us this well? Jesus said: everyone who drinks of this water will be thirsty again. But whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again. The water that I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life.

She said: sir, give me this water so that I will not be thirsty or have to keep coming here to draw. And then Jesus said something that stopped everything. Go call your husband and come back here. She said: I have no husband. Jesus said: you are right. You have had five husbands, and the man you are with now is not your husband.

She said: sir, I perceive that you are a prophet. And she tried to redirect the conversation to a theological debate about where people should worship. Jesus would not be deflected. He told her that God is spirit, and those who worship Him must worship in spirit and in truth. She said: I know that Messiah is coming. Jesus said: I who speak to you am he.

She left her water jar and ran back into the town. She said to the people: come see a man who told me everything I ever did. Can this be the Christ? The people came out of the town and went to see for themselves. Many Samaritans from that town believed in him because of the woman's testimony. He told me everything I ever did. They asked him to stay, and he stayed two days, and many more believed because of his own word. They said to the woman: we believe now not just because of what you said, but because we have heard for ourselves, and we know that this is indeed the Savior of the world.

devotional.bigIdea: Jesus crossed every social boundary to have one conversation with one woman at a well at noon. She was the wrong nationality, the wrong gender, and had the wrong history. He came through Samaria because she was there. Nobody is too far outside the circle for Jesus to find them.
questions: The woman came to the well alone at noon, probably to avoid the other women. Jesus treated her with complete dignity and had a serious conversation with her. Have you ever felt like an outsider? What would it mean to be seen the way Jesus saw her?

Jesus told her everything she had ever done. She did not run away. She ran toward people and said come see. Why do you think being fully known by Jesus made her want to tell everyone rather than hide?

The townspeople said we believe not just because of what you said but because we have heard for ourselves. What is the difference between believing what someone tells you about Jesus and experiencing Him yourself?

connections.title: The Boy Who Came Home
connections.why: The father ran to the prodigal son across every social expectation. Jesus crossed into Samaria across every social boundary to find one woman. Both stories show a God who pursues the people everyone else has written off.

connections.title: Water From the Rock
connections.why: Moses struck a rock and water poured out in the wilderness. Jesus offered a woman at a well living water that would never run dry. The rock in the desert was pointing to this conversation for fifteen hundred years.

connections.title: The Day Everything Changed
connections.why: One woman's testimony brought an entire town out to hear Jesus. On Pentecost, Peter's testimony brought three thousand people to faith. Both stories show how one encounter with Jesus spreads outward in ways nobody planned.

quiz.question: Why did Jesus go through Samaria instead of taking the normal route around it?
options: He had to, because there was someone there He needed to meet
It was the shorter and faster route to Galilee
He wanted to show His disciples that Samaritans were not to be feared
His disciples had family in Samaria they wanted to visit
quiz.explanation: The Gospel of John says He had to go through Samaria. Not because of geography but because of purpose. There was a woman at a well who needed to be found.

quiz.question: Why was the woman surprised that Jesus spoke to her?
options: She had been told that strangers at the well were dangerous
She knew the well was considered holy and visitors were not allowed
Jews did not speak to Samaritans and men did not speak to women alone in public
She recognized him as a famous rabbi from Jerusalem
quiz.explanation: Two boundaries were crossed at once: Jews did not associate with Samaritans, and men did not speak to women alone in public. Jesus crossed both without hesitation.

quiz.question: What did Jesus offer the woman that she initially misunderstood?
options: A blessing over the well so that it would never run dry
Living water that would become a spring inside her welling up to eternal life
Healing for whatever illness had kept her from the other women in town
A way to avoid coming to the well every day by storing water at home
quiz.explanation: She thought He was talking about actual water from the ground. He was offering something that would satisfy a thirst no physical water can reach.

quiz.question: What did Jesus reveal about the woman that showed He knew everything about her?
options: He knew her name and the names of all five of her children
He knew she had been listening to the teachings of a false prophet
He knew she had been stealing water from the well without paying the town fee
He knew that she had had five husbands and the man she was with now was not her husband
quiz.explanation: He told her she had had five husbands and the man she was with now was not her husband. She had not told him any of this. She said: I perceive that you are a prophet.

quiz.question: What did the townspeople say after spending two days with Jesus?
options: We believe because of the miracles you performed in our town
We believe because this woman told us about you and we trust her word
We believe now not just because of what you said but because we have heard for ourselves
We believe because the scriptures say the Messiah would come to Samaria first
quiz.explanation: They told the woman: we believe now not because of what you said but because we have heard for ourselves. Secondhand faith became firsthand encounter.

stories.title: Five Loaves and Two Fish
stories.hook: Five thousand people were hungry and the disciples had five loaves and two fish. Jesus said that is enough.
story: A large crowd had been following Jesus because of the signs He was performing on the sick. He went up on a mountainside and sat down with His disciples. When He looked up and saw the great crowd coming toward Him, He said to Philip: where shall we buy bread for these people to eat? Philip did the math immediately. Eight months wages would not buy enough bread for each one to have a bite. And they were far from any town.

Andrew, Simon Peter's brother, said: there is a boy here who has five barley loaves and two small fish, but what are they among so many people? The boy had given what he had. He did not know what Jesus would do with it. He just handed it over.

Jesus said: have the people sit down. There was plenty of grass in that place, and they sat down, about five thousand men, not counting women and children. Jesus took the loaves, and when He had given thanks, He distributed them to those who were seated. He did the same with the fish. As much as they wanted.

When everyone had eaten their fill, Jesus said to His disciples: gather the pieces that are left over. Let nothing be wasted. They gathered them up and filled twelve baskets with the pieces left over from the five barley loaves. Twelve baskets of leftovers. More remained after everyone was satisfied than what existed before the miracle began.

When the people saw the sign Jesus had performed, they said: surely this is the Prophet who is to come into the world. Jesus, knowing that they intended to come and make Him king by force, withdrew again to a mountain by Himself. He had not come to be the kind of king they were imagining. The miracle pointed to something, but they were reaching for the bread instead of the One who broke it.

The next day, the crowd found Him on the other side of the lake and asked how He had gotten there. Jesus said: you are looking for me not because you saw signs but because you ate the loaves and had your fill. Do not work for food that spoils, but for food that endures to eternal life. He told them: I am the bread of life. Whoever comes to me will never go hungry, and whoever believes in me will never be thirsty.

One boy handed over five loaves and two fish. Jesus gave thanks and distributed them to five thousand people who ate until they were full, with twelve baskets left over. The math was impossible. It always is, when God is the one doing it.

devotional.bigIdea: A boy gave Jesus five loaves and two fish. It was not enough. Jesus gave thanks and distributed it and it became more than enough. What you have is never the point. What Jesus does with what you give is.
questions: Andrew said what are five loaves and two fish among so many people? Have you ever felt like what you had to offer was too small to matter? What does this story say about that?
Jesus gave thanks before the miracle happened, before there was any evidence it would work. What does it mean to give thanks before you can see the result?
The crowd wanted to make Jesus king because He had fed them. Jesus withdrew because they were missing the point. What is the difference between wanting what Jesus gives and wanting Jesus himself?

connections.title: Water From the Rock
connections.why: The crowd called Jesus the Prophet who is to come, the one Moses said God would raise up like him. Moses brought water from a rock in the wilderness. Jesus fed thousands on a hillside. Both point to God as the source of all provision.

connections.title: The Ark and the Rainbow
connections.why: Jesus gave thanks before distributing the bread, exactly as the head of a Jewish household gave thanks at every meal. Gratitude to God for provision runs from Noah's altar after the flood to this hillside outside Galilee.

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Jesus withdrew when the crowd tried to make Him king by force. He had already said His kingdom was not like the kingdoms of the world. They wanted a bread king. He came to be the bread.

quiz.question: Why did Jesus ask Philip where they could buy bread for the crowd?
options: Because Philip lived nearby and would know the local shops
Because Jesus was genuinely unsure how they would feed everyone
To test him, because Jesus already knew what He was going to do
Because Philip was the disciple in charge of the group's money
quiz.explanation: John tells us Jesus asked this to test Philip, because He already knew what He was going to do. The question was an invitation to see how Philip would respond.

quiz.question: What did the boy contribute to the miracle?
options: He gave five barley loaves and two small fish, which was everything he had with him
He was the first to believe that Jesus could feed everyone and encouraged the others
He ran to the nearest town and brought back extra bread for Jesus to multiply
He organized the crowd into groups so the food could be distributed fairly
quiz.explanation: A boy had five barley loaves and two small fish and handed them over. Andrew pointed out they were nowhere near enough. But the boy gave what he had.

quiz.question: How much food was left over after five thousand men plus women and children had eaten their fill?
options: None, because Jesus had multiplied exactly the right amount
A small amount equal to about what they started with
Two baskets, one for each of the fish that had been multiplied
Twelve baskets full of leftover pieces
quiz.explanation: They gathered twelve baskets of leftovers from the five barley loaves. More remained after everyone was satisfied than existed before the miracle began.

quiz.question: What did the crowd want to do after they saw the miracle?
options: They wanted Jesus to teach them how to perform the same miracle themselves
They wanted to make Jesus king by force
They wanted to gather all the leftover bread and store it for the winter
They wanted to follow Jesus back to Jerusalem to tell the Temple leaders
quiz.explanation: Knowing they intended to come and make Him king by force, Jesus withdrew to a mountain by Himself. They were reaching for a bread king. He had not come for that.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say He was, the day after the miracle, when the crowd found Him again?
options: I am the bread of life, whoever comes to me will never go hungry
I am the resurrection and the life, whoever believes in me will never die
I am the light of the world, whoever follows me will not walk in darkness
I am the good shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep
quiz.explanation: Jesus said I am the bread of life. He was pointing beyond the physical miracle to what it signified: He Himself is the true food, the one that satisfies forever.

stories.title: Stepping Out
stories.hook: The disciples saw someone walking toward them on the water in the middle of the night. Then Peter got out of the boat.
story: Right after feeding the five thousand, Jesus made the disciples get into a boat and go ahead of Him to the other side of the lake while He dismissed the crowd. Then He went up on the mountain alone to pray. By the time evening came, He was there alone, and the boat was already far from the land, beaten by the waves because the wind was against it.

In the fourth watch of the night, between three and six in the morning, Jesus came toward them, walking on the sea. When the disciples saw Him walking on the water, they were terrified. They said: it is a ghost! And they cried out in fear. But immediately Jesus spoke to them: take heart, it is I. Do not be afraid.

Peter answered Him: Lord, if it is you, command me to come to you on the water. Jesus said: come. So Peter got out of the boat and walked on the water and came toward Jesus. But when he saw the wind, he was afraid. And beginning to sink, he cried out: Lord, save me!

Jesus immediately reached out His hand and took hold of him. He said: O you of little faith, why did you doubt? And when they got into the boat, the wind ceased. And those in the boat worshipped Him, saying: truly you are the Son of God.

The story is often told as a story about Peter's failure. But Peter was the only one who got out of the boat. Eleven disciples stayed inside, dry and safe and watching from a distance. Peter walked on water. He also sank, but he walked first. And when he sank, he called the right name. And Jesus was immediately there.

He did not rebuke Peter for getting out. He said why did you doubt. The doubt was what caused the sinking, not the stepping out. The invitation had been real. The water had held him. It was only when he shifted his gaze from Jesus to the wind and the waves that he began to go under.

The disciples who had been too afraid to eat their bread with Jesus that night at the Passover table were the same ones who watched Peter walk on water and then worshipped. It takes seeing something impossible to move from following Jesus to believing in who He actually is.

devotional.bigIdea: Peter stepped out of the boat and walked on water. He also sank. Both things are true, and both things matter. The invitation was real. The water held him. It was only when he took his eyes off Jesus and looked at the storm that he started going under. Eyes up is everything.
questions: Peter was the only one who got out of the boat. The other eleven stayed safe inside. Which would you have been? What would it take to get out?
Jesus said why did you doubt, not why did you get out. What is the difference between a bold step of faith that goes badly and a failure of faith?
Peter cried out Lord save me and Jesus immediately reached out His hand. What does the word immediately tell you about how Jesus responds when we cry out to Him?

connections.title: Five Loaves and Two Fish
connections.why: This story happens immediately after the feeding of the five thousand. Jesus sent the disciples ahead in the boat right after the miracle. Two impossible things in one evening, and only one of them involved bread.

connections.title: Three Hundred Men and Torches
connections.why: Gideon was hiding in a winepress when God called him mighty warrior. Peter sank when he looked at the storm instead of Jesus. Both men discovered that faith is not about how you feel. It is about where you look.

connections.title: Through the Sea on Dry Ground
connections.why: God opened the Red Sea so Israel could walk through on dry ground. Jesus walked on the surface of the Sea of Galilee. Both are the same God showing that water does not limit Him, and it does not have to limit those who trust Him.

quiz.question: Where was Jesus when the disciples were struggling in the boat during the storm?
options: He had gone back to Capernaum to prepare a place for them to stay
He was alone on the mountain praying
He had fallen asleep in the back of a different boat following them
He was with the crowd that had stayed behind on the shore
quiz.explanation: After dismissing the crowd Jesus went up on the mountain alone to pray. He was praying while His disciples were struggling with the wind and waves.

quiz.question: What did the disciples think when they saw Jesus walking on the water?
options: They thought it was a trick of the light from the waves
They thought it was another boat coming to help them in the storm
They immediately recognized Him and rowed toward Him
They thought it was a ghost and cried out in fear
quiz.explanation: They were terrified and said it is a ghost and cried out in fear. Jesus immediately spoke to them: take heart, it is I, do not be afraid.

quiz.question: What happened when Peter stepped out of the boat and walked toward Jesus?
options: He walked on the water but when he saw the wind he was afraid and began to sink
He walked all the way to Jesus without any trouble because his faith was strong
He immediately sank the moment his feet touched the water
He took three steps then jumped back into the boat before he could sink
quiz.explanation: Peter walked on the water. He actually walked on it. It was only when he saw the wind and was afraid that he began to sink. Eyes on Jesus: walking. Eyes on the storm: sinking.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say to Peter when He reached out and caught him?
options: I told you the sea would hold you if only you believed
Do not be ashamed, even the smallest faith is enough
O you of little faith, why did you doubt?
Well done for stepping out while the others stayed in the boat
quiz.explanation: Jesus said O you of little faith, why did you doubt? He did not rebuke Peter for getting out of the boat. The getting out was right. The doubt was what caused the sinking.

quiz.question: What did the disciples do when Jesus and Peter got back into the boat and the wind stopped?
options: They asked Jesus why He had let the storm get so bad before coming to them
They celebrated Peter's courage and asked him to teach them to walk on water too
They were too exhausted from rowing to respond and fell asleep immediately
They worshipped Jesus saying truly you are the Son of God
quiz.explanation: They worshipped Him and said truly you are the Son of God. Seeing the impossible moved them from following Jesus to understanding who He actually was.

stories.title: The One Who Stopped
stories.location: The Road from Jerusalem to Jericho
stories.hook: A man was beaten and left for dead on the road. Two religious leaders walked past. A Samaritan stopped.
story: An expert in the Law stood up to test Jesus. Teacher, he said, what must I do to inherit eternal life? Jesus turned the question back on him: what is written in the Law? How do you read it? The man answered correctly: love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, strength, and mind. And love your neighbor as yourself. Jesus said: you have answered correctly. Do this and you will live.
But the man wanted to justify himself, so he asked a follow-up question: and who is my neighbor? Jesus told him a story.

A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, a road that dropped 3,300 feet through wild, rocky desert terrain known for ambushes and robbery. He was attacked by robbers, stripped of his clothes, beaten, and left half dead on the road.

A priest happened to be going down the same road. When he saw the man, he passed by on the other side. A Levite came to the place and looked at him, and he also passed by on the other side. Both men served in the Temple. Both knew the Law. Both saw a human being lying in the road, bleeding and dying. Both walked around him and kept going.

Then a Samaritan came to where the man was. And when he saw him, he had compassion. He went to him and bandaged his wounds, pouring on oil and wine. Then he put the man on his own donkey, brought him to an inn, and took care of him. The next day he took out two denarii, two days wages, and gave them to the innkeeper. Look after him, he said, and when I come back I will reimburse you for any extra expense you may have.
Jesus asked: which of these three do you think was a neighbor to the man who fell into the hands of robbers? The expert in the Law said: the one who had mercy on him. Jesus said: go and do likewise.

The man who asked who is my neighbor was trying to draw a boundary around the word, to find out who he was required to love. Jesus refused to draw that boundary. He did not answer the question who is my neighbor. He answered a different question: what does it look like to be a neighbor? And He told a story where the hero was the person the audience despised most.

devotional.bigIdea: The priest and the Levite knew the Law better than the Samaritan did. They walked past anyway. The Samaritan stopped. Knowing what is right and doing what is right are two different things. Jesus said go and do likewise, not go and understand likewise.
questions: The expert in the Law asked who is my neighbor because he wanted to know who he was required to love. Why do you think people try to put limits on who they have to care about?
The priest and the Levite were religious leaders who knew the Law. They still walked past. What does that tell you about the gap between knowing something and doing it?
Jesus said go and do likewise. Not think about it or feel bad about it or plan to do it someday. Go and do. What is one thing you could do this week that looks like the Samaritan stopping?

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: The expert quoted the two greatest commandments correctly, the same ones Jesus gave in the Sermon on the Mount. The whole sermon was about the difference between knowing the Law and living it. This parable is the same lesson with a story.

connections.title: The Woman at the Well
connections.why: In both stories, a Samaritan is the hero. Jesus kept honoring the people His audience despised most. The woman at the well brought a whole town to faith. The Samaritan on the road showed what neighbor love looks like.

connections.title: The Boy Who Came Home
connections.why: The father ran to the prodigal son. The Samaritan ran to a stranger bleeding on the road. Both did more than was required. Both paid a cost nobody asked them to pay. That is the shape of love in Jesus's stories.

quiz.question: Why did the expert in the Law ask who is my neighbor?
options: Because he thought Jesus had a different definition of neighbor than the Law did
Because he wanted to know which of his enemies he was required to forgive
Because he genuinely did not know the answer and wanted to learn
Because he wanted to justify himself by drawing a limit around who he had to love
quiz.explanation: Luke says he asked to justify himself. He wanted to find the boundary, the point where he could say I have loved everyone I am required to love. Jesus refused to give him that boundary.

quiz.question: What did the priest and the Levite do when they saw the man on the road?
options: They stopped to check if he was alive but could not carry him alone
They passed by on the other side without stopping to help
They went back to Jerusalem to get help and send it to the man
They prayed over the man from a distance before continuing their journey
quiz.explanation: Both the priest and the Levite saw the man and passed by on the other side. Both served in the Temple. Both knew the Law. Both walked around a dying man.

quiz.question: What did the Samaritan do when he saw the man on the road?
options: He helped the man to the side of the road and then went to find the nearest town for help
He stopped and asked the man what had happened before deciding whether to help
He had compassion, bandaged his wounds, put him on his donkey, took him to an inn, and paid for his care
He gave the man money for an inn but continued on his own journey
quiz.explanation: He saw the man and had compassion. He bandaged wounds, poured on oil and wine, put the man on his own donkey, took him to an inn, cared for him overnight, and paid for future care too. He went far beyond what anyone could have required of him.

quiz.question: How did Jesus reframe the original question who is my neighbor?
options: He answered a different question: which of the three men behaved like a neighbor?
He said everyone is your neighbor so the question does not need an answer
He told the man to go ask the priests since they were the experts in the Law
He defined neighbor as anyone who lives within a day's walk of your home
quiz.explanation: The man asked who is my neighbor. Jesus asked which one was a neighbor to the man who was robbed. He shifted from a question about who deserves my love to a question about who I am choosing to be.

quiz.question: What did Jesus tell the expert in the Law to do at the end of the story?
options: Study the Law more carefully so he would know how to love his neighbor correctly
Go and do likewise
Teach this story in the synagogue so others would learn who their neighbors were
Ask the Samaritans in his town to forgive him for how they had been treated
quiz.explanation: Jesus said go and do likewise. Not think about it, not plan for it, not understand it better. Go and do. The point was never knowledge. The point was action.

stories.title: The Man in the Tree
stories.hook: The most hated man in Jericho climbed a tree to see Jesus. Jesus looked up and said come down, I am coming to your house today.
story: Jesus was passing through Jericho. A man named Zacchaeus was the chief tax collector there, and he was wealthy. Tax collectors in first-century Judea worked for the Roman occupation, collecting taxes from their own people, and most of them collected more than required and kept the extra. Zacchaeus was the chief tax collector. He was hated, and he had made himself rich in a way everyone in Jericho understood.

He wanted to see who Jesus was. But the crowd was blocking his view, and he was short. So he ran ahead and climbed a sycamore-fig tree to see Jesus as He passed under it. A wealthy, powerful, grown man, running ahead of the crowd and climbing a tree like a child. Something about Jesus made him willing to look ridiculous.

When Jesus reached the spot, He looked up and said: Zacchaeus, come down immediately. I must stay at your house today. Zacchaeus came down at once and welcomed Him gladly. The crowd murmured. He has gone to be the guest of a sinner. They knew exactly what Zacchaeus was and what it meant for Jesus to walk into his house.

Zacchaeus stood up and said to the Lord: look, Lord, here and now I give half of my possessions to the poor. And if I have cheated anybody out of anything, I will pay back four times the amount. The Law required two to five times repayment for theft. Zacchaeus volunteered four times. He was not asked. He was not pressured. The presence of Jesus changed everything before a single sermon was preached.
Jesus said: today salvation has come to this house, because this man too is a son of Abraham. For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost.

The crowd had already written Zacchaeus off. He was a collaborator, a thief in a tax collector's robe, a man who had sold out his own people for Roman money. He was the last person anyone in Jericho would have chosen. Jesus chose him first. He looked up into the tree and called him by name.

Zacchaeus did not change and then meet Jesus. He met Jesus and then changed. The order matters. Jesus did not wait for him to be better. He invited Himself to lunch with the man everyone else was walking past. That is always how it works.

devotional.bigIdea: Zacchaeus climbed a tree to catch a glimpse of Jesus. Jesus stopped, looked up, and called him by name. The crowd had written Zacchaeus off. Jesus had not. Nobody is too far gone, too well-known for the wrong reasons, or too lost in the wrong choices for Jesus to stop and call their name.
questions: Zacchaeus ran ahead and climbed a tree. He was a wealthy, important man and he did not care how he looked. What made him willing to be that desperate to see Jesus?
Jesus invited Himself to Zacchaeus's house before Zacchaeus had changed anything about his life. What does that tell you about how Jesus treats people who are in the middle of bad choices?
Zacchaeus gave away half his wealth and repaid four times what he had stolen, and nobody asked him to. What does that tell you about what happens to a person when they genuinely encounter Jesus?

connections.title: The Boy Who Came Home
connections.why: Jesus said the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost. The father in the prodigal son story ran toward his son when he was still a long way off. Both stories show God moving toward the lost rather than waiting for them to clean up first.

connections.title: The Woman at the Well
connections.why: The crowd murmured that Jesus had gone to be the guest of a sinner, the same kind of response Jesus got for talking to the Samaritan woman. Jesus kept choosing the people everyone else had already rejected.

connections.title: The Man Who Hunted Christians
connections.why: Jesus called Zacchaeus by name out of a tree. Jesus called Saul by name on the road to Damascus. Both men were living in ways that put them outside the community of faith. Jesus found both of them and called them anyway.

quiz.question: Why was Zacchaeus so disliked by the people of Jericho?
options: He was the chief tax collector who worked for the Roman occupiers and had made himself wealthy by overcharging his own people
He had publicly argued against the religious leaders in the synagogue and been expelled
He had refused to help rebuild the city wall and kept his money while others worked
He was a foreign official sent by Rome to collect taxes and had no connection to the people
quiz.explanation: Zacchaeus was the chief tax collector, working for Rome, collecting from his own people, and keeping the extra. He was a collaborator and a thief, and everyone in Jericho knew it.

quiz.question: Why did Zacchaeus climb a sycamore-fig tree?
options: He was hiding from people who wanted to confront him about his tax collecting
He had climbed trees since childhood and it was a habit when he was excited
He thought Jesus might be more likely to notice him if he was up high
He was short and the crowd was blocking his view of Jesus
quiz.explanation: He was a short man and the crowd was too thick. So this wealthy, powerful chief tax collector ran ahead and climbed a tree like a child. Something about Jesus made him willing to look ridiculous.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say when He looked up and saw Zacchaeus in the tree?
options: Come down so the crowd can see that I am willing to speak even with sinners
Come down immediately, I must stay at your house today
Come down and tell these people what you have done with their money
Come down and follow me, and I will make you a collector of people instead of taxes
quiz.explanation: Jesus said come down immediately, I must stay at your house today. He invited Himself to lunch with the most hated man in the crowd. The crowd was stunned.

quiz.question: What did Zacchaeus announce he would do after Jesus came to his house?
options: He would stop collecting taxes and find another way to earn a living
He would use his position to reduce the taxes collected from the poorest families in Jericho
He would give half his possessions to the poor and repay four times anyone he had cheated
He would donate his entire fortune to the Temple and live simply from then on
quiz.explanation: Zacchaeus stood up and said he would give half to the poor and repay four times what he had taken dishonestly. Nobody asked him to. The presence of Jesus changed him before any sermon was preached.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say at the end of the story about why He had come?
options: The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost
I have come that they may have life and have it to the full
I have come to call not the righteous but sinners to repentance
Whoever has ears to hear, let them hear what the Spirit is saying
quiz.explanation: Jesus said the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost. Not to wait for the lost to find their way back. To seek them out. Zacchaeus up a tree was exactly who Jesus had come looking for.

stories.title: The King Rides In
