stories.hook: Jerusalem was expecting a king on a warhorse. Jesus came on a donkey. The crowd went wild anyway.
story: Jesus was approaching Jerusalem. He sent two disciples ahead to a village where they would find a donkey tied up with her colt. Untie them and bring them to me, He said. If anyone says anything to you, tell them the Lord needs them. The disciples found exactly what He had described and brought them back. They put their cloaks on the donkeys and Jesus sat on the colt.

This was not accidental. Five hundred years earlier, the prophet Zechariah had written: say to Daughter Zion, see, your king comes to you, gentle and riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey. The Messiah, Israel's conquering king, was supposed to arrive on a warhorse. Jesus chose a donkey, the animal of peace, the animal of a servant. He was announcing something, but not what the crowd expected to hear.

A very large crowd spread their cloaks on the road, a sign of welcome reserved for royalty. Others cut branches from the trees and spread them on the road. The crowds that went ahead and those that followed were shouting: Hosanna to the Son of David! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord! Hosanna in the highest heaven!

When Jesus entered Jerusalem, the whole city was stirred and asked: who is this? The crowds said: this is Jesus, the prophet from Nazareth in Galilee. They were not wrong, but they were not fully right either. They saw a prophet. They were welcoming a king. They were expecting a military liberator. He was something else entirely.

The same crowd that shouted Hosanna on Sunday would be shouting crucify Him by Friday. The welcome was real, but it was built on wrong expectations. They wanted a king who would drive out Rome. Jesus had come to do something Rome could never have done and could not stop: deal with the sin that was the root of every empire's power over human hearts.

He went into the Temple and drove out all who were selling and buying there. He overturned the tables of the money-changers and the seats of those selling doves. He said: it is written, my house will be called a house of prayer, but you are making it a den of robbers. The blind and the lame came to Him in the Temple and He healed them. The children were shouting in the Temple courts: Hosanna to the Son of David.

The chief priests and teachers of the law saw the wonderful things He did and heard the children shouting and were indignant. Jesus quoted Psalm 8 to them. From the lips of children and infants you, Lord, have called forth your praise. The children in the Temple understood something the religious leaders refused to see.

devotional.bigIdea: The crowd welcomed a king and got one. Just not the kind they were expecting. Jesus rode a donkey into Jerusalem because He came in peace, not to conquer Rome but to deal with something deeper. The kingdom He was building was not what anyone had planned for. It never is.
questions: The crowd expected a military king on a warhorse. Jesus came on a donkey. Why do you think God so often shows up in ways that are different from what people expect?
The same crowd that shouted Hosanna on Sunday shouted crucify Him on Friday. What does that tell you about the difference between enthusiasm for Jesus and actual faith in Him?

The children in the Temple were shouting praises while the religious leaders were indignant. Jesus said the children understood something the leaders missed. What do you think children see that adults sometimes can't?

connections.title: Impossible News
connections.why: Zechariah wrote your king comes to you riding on a donkey five hundred years before Jesus was born, just as Isaiah described the suffering servant seven hundred years before the cross. The entry into Jerusalem was a prophecy fulfilled in plain sight.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Hosanna means save us now. The crowd shouted it as a welcome. Five days later they were demanding His death. The journey from the triumphal entry to Golgotha is the whole story of what kind of king Jesus came to be.

connections.title: The Servant Who Suffers
connections.why: Isaiah said the servant would have no beauty or majesty, gentle and humble. Zechariah said the king would be gentle riding on a donkey. Both prophecies pointed to the same arrival. The King rode in exactly as promised.

quiz.question: What did Jesus ride into Jerusalem on, and why did it matter?
options: He walked on foot, refusing any symbol of worldly power
A warhorse, to show He had the power of a military king
A donkey, fulfilling Zechariah's prophecy of a gentle king coming in peace
A chariot, as a symbol of royal authority and power
quiz.explanation: Jesus chose a donkey, fulfilling Zechariah 9:9 which said your king comes to you gentle and riding on a donkey. The warhorse was the animal of conquest. The donkey was the animal of peace and service.

quiz.question: What did the crowd do as Jesus entered Jerusalem?
options: They followed Him quietly to the Temple to see what He would do there
They spread cloaks and branches on the road and shouted Hosanna to the Son of David
They stood silently in awe as He passed, not knowing who He was
They asked Him questions about His intentions toward the Roman authorities
quiz.explanation: They spread their cloaks on the road and cut branches from trees, signs of royal welcome, and shouted Hosanna to the Son of David, blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord.

quiz.question: What did the whole city of Jerusalem ask when Jesus entered?
options: Why has this man from Galilee come to Jerusalem now?
Has the Messiah finally come to restore the kingdom of David?
Is this the prophet who will lead us against Rome?
Who is this?
quiz.explanation: When Jesus entered Jerusalem the whole city was stirred and asked who is this? The crowds answered: this is Jesus the prophet from Nazareth in Galilee. They were not wrong, but they were not fully right.

quiz.question: What did Jesus do when He entered the Temple?
options: He drove out the money-changers and sellers, saying they had made God's house a den of robbers
He met with the chief priests to explain who He was and why He had come
He sat down and began teaching the crowds who had followed Him in
He went to the inner courts and prayed alone before doing anything else
quiz.explanation: He overturned the tables of the money-changers and drove out those who were buying and selling. He said: it is written, my house will be called a house of prayer, but you are making it a den of robbers.

quiz.question: Who praised Jesus in the Temple that made the religious leaders angry?
options: The disciples who were singing psalms at the top of their voices
The Pharisees who had followed Him from Galilee and believed in Him
The children who were shouting Hosanna to the Son of David in the Temple courts
The blind and lame people He had healed who were telling everyone what He had done
quiz.explanation: The children were shouting Hosanna to the Son of David. The chief priests and teachers of the law were indignant. Jesus quoted Psalm 8: from the lips of children and infants you have called forth your praise.

stories.title: The Man Who Walked Away
stories.hook: A man ran up to Jesus and asked how to inherit eternal life. He had done everything right his whole life. He walked away sad.
story: As Jesus started on his way, a man ran up to him and fell on his knees before Him. Good teacher, he said, what must I do to inherit eternal life? He was rich. He was young. He was a ruler. He had position, wealth, and religious credibility. He ran to Jesus and knelt in the road. Something genuine was driving him.

Jesus said: why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone. Then He listed the commandments: do not murder, do not commit adultery, do not steal, do not give false testimony, do not defraud, honor your father and mother. The man said: teacher, all these I have kept since I was a boy.

Jesus looked at him and loved him. The text says that. Jesus looked at this man, saw everything about him, and loved him. Then He said: one thing you lack. Go, sell everything you have and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me.
At this, the man's face fell. He went away sad, because he had great wealth. He had run to Jesus. He knelt before Jesus. And he walked away from Jesus because the cost was one thing too many.

Jesus looked around and said to His disciples: how hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom of God! The disciples were amazed. Who then can be saved? In their world, wealth was a sign of God's blessing. If the blessed cannot enter, who can? Jesus said: with man this is impossible, but not with God. All things are possible with God.

The man had asked the right question. He had the right posture. He had the right track record. He had kept the commandments since he was a boy. And he had one thing that was more precious to him than the answer to his own question. He was not willing to let it go.

Jesus did not chase him. He did not revise the terms. He did not say come back when you are ready. He watched him walk away and He grieved it. This is one of the most heartbreaking moments in the Gospels: a man who came close, who was loved by Jesus, who asked the right question, and chose his wealth over the answer.

devotional.bigIdea: Jesus looked at the rich young ruler and loved him. Then He told him the one thing he needed to do. The man walked away sad. Jesus did not lower the bar. Love does not always mean telling people what they want to hear. Sometimes it means telling them the one true thing they are not ready to receive.
questions: Jesus looked at the man and loved him before He said the hard thing. What does it mean that love was the motivation for a requirement the man could not meet?
The man had kept all the commandments since he was a boy and it still was not enough. Why do you think Jesus asked for the one thing that was hardest? What was He really asking for?
The man went away sad. Jesus did not run after him and change the terms. Why do you think Jesus let him walk away? What does that tell you about how Jesus treats human freedom?

connections.title: The Wisest Request
connections.why: Solomon asked for the one thing that would put everything else in right order: wisdom to serve God's people. The rich young ruler could not give up the one thing that was out of order. Both stories are about what we treasure most.

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Jesus said in the Sermon on the Mount that you cannot serve both God and money. The rich young ruler chose. The Sermon on the Mount named the choice. This story shows what it looks like when someone makes the wrong one.

connections.title: The Boy Who Came Home
connections.why: The prodigal son ran to his father when he came to himself. The rich young ruler ran to Jesus kneeling in the road. One found his way home. One walked away. Both were loved. Both were free to choose.

quiz.question: What did the rich young man do when he approached Jesus that showed his earnestness?
options: He climbed a tree to see above the crowd as Zacchaeus had done
He waited at the edge of the crowd for hours hoping Jesus would notice him
He sent a servant ahead to ask if Jesus would be willing to meet with him
He ran up to Jesus and fell on his knees before Him in the road
quiz.explanation: He ran up to Jesus and knelt in the road. He was wealthy, young, and powerful, and he ran and knelt. Something genuine was driving him toward this question.

quiz.question: What did the man say when Jesus listed the commandments?
options: He said he had kept all these since he was a boy
He said he knew the commandments well but was not sure he had kept them perfectly
He asked Jesus which commandment was the greatest so he could focus on that one
He admitted he had broken several of them and asked how he could make it right
quiz.explanation: The man said teacher, all these I have kept since I was a boy. He was not boasting. He was genuinely describing his life. And Jesus did not contradict him.

quiz.question: What does Mark say Jesus felt when He looked at the man before giving the hard requirement?
options: He felt frustration that someone with so much could not see what was missing
He felt compassion because He could see how hard the man had tried his whole life
He loved him
He felt sadness knowing the man would not be able to meet the condition
quiz.explanation: Jesus looked at him and loved him. The text says that plainly. The hard requirement that followed came from love, not from rejection or a desire to see the man fail.

quiz.question: What did Jesus ask the man to do that he could not bring himself to do?
options: Give one tenth of his wealth to the Temple and use the rest to serve the poor
Sell everything he had, give to the poor, and come follow Jesus
Fast for forty days as a sign of his commitment before Jesus would accept him
Leave his family and follow Jesus on the road with the other disciples
quiz.explanation: Jesus said go, sell everything you have and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me. The man's face fell. He went away sad because he had great wealth.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say when the disciples asked who then can be saved?
options: He said those who love God more than their possessions will always find a way
He said salvation is for all who keep the commandments faithfully from their youth
He said the poor have an advantage because they have less to give up
He said with man this is impossible but not with God, all things are possible with God
quiz.explanation: Jesus said with man this is impossible but not with God. All things are possible with God. Salvation is not a human achievement. It requires divine intervention, which is exactly why the young man's rule-keeping was not enough.

stories.title: The Boy Who Came Home
stories.hook: He demanded his inheritance early, spent every coin of it, and ended up starving in a foreign country. Then he came to himself, and started walking home.
story: Jesus told this story to a specific audience on a specific day. The Pharisees and teachers of the law were grumbling because Jesus was eating with tax collectors and sinners, welcoming them. So He told three stories in a row about things that were lost and then found. The third one has never left the world alone.

A man had two sons. The younger one came to his father and said: give me my share of the estate. In the ancient world, this was roughly equivalent to saying I wish you were dead. The inheritance was not released until the father died. Asking for it early was a declaration that the relationship was over, that the son wanted what the father had without the father himself. The father divided his property between them and gave it.

The younger son gathered everything and left for a distant country. He wasted it all in wild living. Then a severe famine hit, and he was broke and starving, feeding pigs for a Gentile farmer, the most degraded position a Jewish boy could imagine. He was so hungry he wanted to eat the pigs' food. No one gave him anything.

When he came to his senses, he said: how many of my father's hired servants have food to spare, and here I am starving to death. I will set out and go back to my father and say to him: Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son. Make me like one of your hired servants. He rehearsed the speech on the road home. He was not expecting restoration. He was asking for employment.

But while he was still a long way off, his father saw him. He had been watching the road. He recognized his son at a distance and he ran, an undignified thing for a man of honor in that culture, robes gathered up, running down the road. He threw his arms around him and kissed him. The son started his rehearsed speech. Father, I have sinned against heaven and against you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son.

The father did not let him finish. He called to the servants: quick, bring the best robe and put it on him. Put a ring on his finger and sandals on his feet. Bring the fattened calf and kill it. Let us have a feast and celebrate. For this son of mine was dead and is alive again. He was lost and is found.

The older son was in the field. When he came home and heard the music and dancing, he refused to go in. He had been here the whole time. He had worked hard. He had never disobeyed. And his father had never thrown him a party. The father came out to him, the same father who had run to the younger son, and walked out to the older one. My son, he said, you are always with me, and everything I have is yours. But we had to celebrate and be glad, because this brother of yours was dead and is alive again. He was lost and is found.

The story ends there, without telling us whether the older son went in or stayed outside. Jesus left it open. Because the Pharisees listening were the older brother. And the question was still hanging in the air for them, as it hangs in the air for everyone who has ever worked hard and felt overlooked. Will you come to the party? The father is at the door. He came out to find you too.

devotional.bigIdea: The father ran. That is the whole sermon. Long before the son finished his apology, before any conditions were named, before any debt was settled, the father ran. That is what God is like.
questions: The father saw his son while he was still a long way off, he had been watching the road. What does it mean to you that God is watching the road for you?

The older son stayed outside the party, angry. Have you ever felt like the older brother, faithful, responsible, and somehow still feeling like you've missed something? What do you think the father's words meant for him?

The son rehearsed a speech asking to be a servant. The father gave him a robe, a ring, and a feast. What is the difference between what the son expected and what the father gave, and what does that tell you about how God responds to coming home?

connections.title: The First Bad Choice
connections.why: When the prodigal son came to his senses he was in a far country, just like Adam and Eve hiding in the garden. Both stories are about people who ran from the Father and found that coming back is always possible.

connections.title: Wherever You Go
connections.why: The father ran to his son, the same extravagant, undignified love that Boaz showed Ruth. Both stories are about someone of higher standing running toward someone who expected rejection.

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Jesus told this parable because the Pharisees were grumbling about him eating with sinners. The Sermon on the Mount said love your enemies. This story showed what that looked like in practice.

quiz.question: Why was the younger son's request for his inheritance so shocking in that culture?
options: The younger son had no legal right to any inheritance at all
It was illegal under Jewish law to divide an inheritance
It was equivalent to saying I wish you were dead, inheritance was not released until the father died
quiz.explanation: In the ancient world, asking for your inheritance before your father died was essentially saying I wish you were dead, I want what you have without you. The father gave it anyway.

quiz.question: What was the son planning to ask his father when he returned home?
options: To be given a second share of the inheritance to start over
To be made like one of the hired servants, he was not expecting to be a son again
To be fully restored as a son with all his rights returned
quiz.explanation: The son rehearsed a speech: I am no longer worthy to be called your son, make me like one of your hired servants. He was asking for employment, not restoration. The father didn't let him finish.

quiz.question: What did the father do when he saw his son coming from a long way off?
options: He waited at the door for his son to approach and apologize first
He sent a servant to bring the son in so he could hear the apology privately
He ran to his son, threw his arms around him, and kissed him before any words were spoken
quiz.explanation: The father saw him while he was still far off, and he ran. An undignified thing for a man of honor in that culture. He didn't wait for the apology. He ran. That is what God is like.

quiz.question: What does the father's response to the returning son tell us about how God sees repentant sinners?
options: That God runs toward us before we finish our apology and restores us fully as His children
That God forgives privately but public shame is part of the consequence
That God is gracious but remembers what we have done wrong
That God forgives but expects a long period of earning back trust
quiz.explanation: The father ran, in that culture a dignified man never ran. He saw the son from far off, ran to him, and threw a party before any apology was finished. That's grace.

quiz.question: What was the older brother's reaction to the party and what does it reveal?
options: He was relieved his brother was alive and forgave him immediately
He was confused but willing to celebrate once he understood what had happened
He said nothing and went quietly to his room
He was angry and refused to go in, believing he deserved more than his brother who had wasted everything
quiz.explanation: The older brother's anger exposes religious pride, he had stayed and worked, but his heart was far from the father's. Jesus told this parable partly for the Pharisees in the crowd.

stories.title: Come Out
stories.hook: His friend had been dead for four days. Jesus wept. Then He told a dead man to walk out of his tomb.
story: Lazarus was sick. His sisters Mary and Martha sent a message to Jesus, who was across the Jordan. Lord, the one you love is sick. It was a simple message. No request, no demand. Just a statement of fact, as if knowing that Jesus knew was enough. And Jesus loved this family. The gospel says so plainly. But when He heard the message, He stayed where He was for two more days.

By the time Jesus arrived in Bethany, Lazarus had been in the tomb for four days. Martha heard He was coming and went to meet Him on the road. Mary stayed home. Martha said what she had probably been saying to herself for four days. Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died. Then quietly, a flicker of something still alive underneath the grief. But I know that even now, God will give you whatever you ask.

Jesus said: your brother will rise again. Martha answered theologically. I know he will rise again in the resurrection at the last day. Jesus said: I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me will live, even though they die. And whoever lives by believing in me will never die. Do you believe this? She said yes. And went to get Mary.

When Mary came to where Jesus was, she fell at His feet and said the same thing Martha had said. Lord, if you had been here, my brother would not have died. And then she wept. The people who had come with her were weeping. And Jesus, God in human flesh, the one who already knew what He was about to do, was deeply moved in His spirit and troubled. Where have you laid him? They showed Him. And Jesus wept.

The shortest verse in the Bible. Two words in English. And it matters enormously, because Jesus was about to raise Lazarus, and He still wept. He did not stand above the grief and say He would fix it in a moment. He entered it. He stood inside the loss with the people He loved before He resolved it. God weeps with us before He works in us.

A cave with a stone across the entrance. Jesus said: take away the stone. Martha objected. It had been four days and there would be an odor. Jesus said: did I not tell you that if you believe, you will see the glory of God? They rolled the stone away. Jesus looked up and prayed out loud, not because He needed to but for the people standing there, so they would understand where the power was coming from. Then He called out in a loud voice: Lazarus, come out!
And he came out. Grave clothes and all, shuffling out of the tomb, still wrapped in linen with a cloth around his face. Jesus said: unwrap him and let him go.

Many who saw it believed. Some went to the Pharisees. The chief priests and Pharisees called a meeting and said: what are we going to do? If we let him go on like this, everyone will believe in him. And the high priest said: it is better for one man to die for the people than for the whole nation to perish. He did not know what he was saying. But he was right. One man would die for the people. And death would not be able to hold Him either.

devotional.bigIdea: Jesus wept at Lazarus's tomb, even though He was about to raise him. He did not stand above the grief. He stepped into it first. God is not distant from your pain. He is inside it with you.
questions: Jesus waited two more days before going to Bethany after hearing Lazarus was sick. He was not too late, He was on a different schedule. Is there something in your life where you have been wondering if God is too late?
Jesus wept even though He knew He was about to raise Lazarus. What does that tell you about how God relates to human suffering?
Martha made a theological statement, I know he will rise at the last day. Jesus said I am the resurrection. What is the difference between knowing a fact about Jesus and knowing Jesus himself?

connections.title: The Empty Tomb
connections.why: Jesus said I am the resurrection and the life at Lazarus's tomb. Three chapters later He proved it, not just by raising someone else, but by rising Himself.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: The raising of Lazarus was the event that pushed the Pharisees to plot Jesus's death. The miracle that gave life to one man sealed the decision that would take the life of another.

connections.title: Impossible News
connections.why: Jesus told Martha did I not tell you that if you believe you will see the glory of God, the same God whose angel told Mary nothing is impossible with God. Both moments required believing before seeing.

quiz.question: What did Mary and Martha's message to Jesus say about Lazarus?
options: Lord, the one you love is sick, no request, just a statement of fact
We need you here immediately or our brother will not survive
Please come quickly, Lazarus is near death and we are afraid
quiz.explanation: The message was simple: Lord, the one you love is sick. No demand, no urgency, no instruction, just a statement of fact, as if knowing that Jesus knew was enough.

quiz.question: Why did Jesus weep at the tomb even though He was about to raise Lazarus?
options: He wept because He knew Lazarus would have to die again one day
He was overcome with grief for Lazarus and had forgotten His plan
He entered the grief of those He loved before He resolved it, God weeps with us
quiz.explanation: Jesus wept even knowing what He was about to do. He did not stand above the grief. He stepped into it first. God does not just fix our pain from a distance, He enters it with us.

quiz.question: What did the high priest Caiaphas accidentally prophesy at the meeting after Lazarus was raised?
options: That a false prophet had arisen and must be silenced
That Jesus would destroy the Temple and rebuild it in three days
That it was better for one man to die for the people than for the whole nation to perish
quiz.explanation: Caiaphas said it is better for one man to die for the people. He was trying to justify killing Jesus politically, but he was accidentally stating the deepest truth of the gospel. One man would die for the people. And death would not hold Him.

quiz.question: Why did Jesus wait two days before going to Lazarus when He heard he was sick?
options: Because the illness was not serious enough to require His immediate attention
Because He said this illness would not end in death but was for God's glory
Because He was waiting for Lazarus's family to send a second message
Because He was afraid of the religious leaders in Jerusalem nearby
quiz.explanation: Jesus deliberately waited, not from indifference but from purpose. He said this was for the glory of God, so that the Son of God might be glorified through it.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say about Himself just before raising Lazarus?
options: I am the resurrection and the life, whoever believes in me will never die
I am the light of the world, whoever follows me will not walk in darkness
I am the good shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep
I am the way the truth and the life
quiz.explanation: "I am the resurrection and the life", one of the great I AM statements. Jesus did not say He would bring resurrection. He said He IS resurrection. Then He proved it.

stories.title: The Night Before
stories.hook: The night before He died, Jesus took bread and broke it and said this is my body. Then He knelt down and washed His disciples' feet.
story: On the first day of the Festival of Unleavened Bread, the disciples asked Jesus where He wanted them to prepare the Passover meal. He gave them precise instructions: go into the city, find a certain man, tell him the Teacher says my appointed time is near, I will celebrate the Passover with my disciples at your house. They did as Jesus directed and prepared the Passover.

When evening came, Jesus was at the table with the twelve. Before the meal, He got up, took off His outer robe, wrapped a towel around His waist, and poured water into a basin. Then He began to wash His disciples' feet and dry them with the towel. This was a servant's job, the task assigned to the lowest member of a household. The disciples were stunned.

When He came to Peter, Peter said: Lord, are you going to wash my feet? Jesus answered: you do not realize now what I am doing, but later you will understand. Peter said: you shall never wash my feet. Jesus said: unless I wash you, you have no part with me. Peter said: then Lord, not just my feet but my hands and my head as well!

After washing their feet, Jesus put on His robe and returned to His place. He said: do you understand what I have done for you? You call me Teacher and Lord, and rightly so, for that is what I am. Now that I, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you also should wash one another's feet. I have set you an example that you should do as I have done for you.

While they were eating, Jesus took bread and when He had given thanks, He broke it and gave it to His disciples, saying: take and eat, this is my body. Then He took a cup and when He had given thanks, He gave it to them, saying: drink from it, all of you. This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.

He told them He would not drink from the fruit of the vine again until the day He drank it new with them in His Father's kingdom. Then He told them one of them would betray Him. And He told Peter that before the rooster crowed, Peter would disown Him three times. Peter said he would never disown Jesus. And meant it completely.

When they had sung a hymn, they went out to the Mount of Olives. Jesus had given them a meal to remember Him by. He had knelt on the floor and washed their feet. He had called them friends. He had told them everything was about to change. And then He led them out into the night.

devotional.bigIdea: The night before He died, Jesus did not sit at the head of the table and give orders. He got on His knees and washed feet. The greatest act of service in history was preceded by the humblest act of service in that room. Leadership in Jesus's kingdom has always looked like kneeling.
questions: Jesus washed the disciples' feet even though He knew Judas was about to betray Him and Peter was about to deny Him. What does it mean to serve people faithfully even when you know they are going to let you down?
Peter refused to let Jesus wash his feet until Jesus said unless I wash you, you have no part with me. Why do you think it is sometimes hard to let someone serve you or care for you?
Jesus said I have set you an example. The Lord of the universe knelt on the floor with a towel. What would it look like in your life this week to do something that looks like washing someone's feet?

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Jesus said this is my blood of the covenant poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins. The next day at Golgotha it was no longer bread and wine. The meal pointed to what was coming and gave the disciples a way to remember it forever.

connections.title: The Scarlet Thread
connections.why: The Passover meal Jesus was sharing with His disciples was the same meal Israel had eaten the night before leaving Egypt, the night the firstborn died. Jesus was the true Passover lamb, and He was eating the meal that pointed to Him.

connections.title: The Day Everything Changed
connections.why: After Pentecost, the early church devoted themselves to the breaking of bread. They were doing what Jesus had told them to do. The meal at the last supper became the meal at the center of every gathering of the church from that night forward.

quiz.question: What did Jesus do before the Passover meal that shocked the disciples?
options: He prayed a long prayer over the meal that revealed everything that was about to happen
He got up, wrapped a towel around His waist, and washed each of their feet
He broke the bread before the meal even began and explained what it meant
He told them He would be betrayed that night and asked them to guess who would do it
quiz.explanation: He got up, took off His outer robe, poured water into a basin, and began washing His disciples' feet. It was a servant's task, the lowest job in any household, and He did it for all twelve of them.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say when Peter refused to let Him wash his feet?
options: Unless I wash you, you have no part with me
You will understand later why this matters. For now just trust me.
Peter, your pride is going to be your greatest struggle. Let me show you humility.
I am not asking for your permission. This is what it means to be Lord.
quiz.explanation: Jesus said unless I wash you, you have no part with me. Peter immediately went the other direction and asked for his hands and head too. Jesus said someone who has had a bath only needs their feet washed.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say the bread represented when He broke it at the meal?
options: The covenant God made with Abraham that would now be fulfilled through them
The bread of the Passover that had kept their ancestors alive in Egypt
His body, which He gave to them saying take and eat
The firstfruits of a new kingdom that would feed the whole world
quiz.explanation: Jesus took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and gave it to them saying take and eat, this is my body. Then He took the cup and said this is my blood of the covenant poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say He would not do again until the day He did it new in His Father's kingdom?
options: Celebrate the Passover with His disciples
Teach in the synagogue or the Temple courts
Walk through the streets of Jerusalem
Drink from the fruit of the vine
quiz.explanation: Jesus said He would not drink again from the fruit of the vine until the day He drank it new with them in His Father's kingdom. He was looking forward to a reunion beyond death.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say after washing their feet about why He had done it?
options: I have done this so that you will remember me every time you wash someone else's feet
I have set you an example that you should do as I have done for you
I did this because none of you offered to do it, and someone had to
The greatest among you is the one who serves, and I wanted to show you what that looks like
quiz.explanation: Jesus said I have set you an example that you should do as I have done for you. The Lord and Teacher had knelt on the floor with a towel. Now they knew what leadership in His kingdom looked like.

stories.title: The Darkest Friday
stories.hook: They arrested Him in a garden. By midmorning He was nailed to a cross. By afternoon He was gone. And the world was never the same again.
story: The night before He died, Jesus walked from Jerusalem across the Kidron Valley to a garden on the Mount of Olives called Gethsemane. He took Peter, James, and John a little further in, told them to keep watch, and went on alone. He fell with His face to the ground and prayed: Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass from me. Yet not as I will, but as you will. He came back and found the disciples asleep. He prayed again. He came back and found them sleeping again. A third time He prayed the same prayer. He was in such anguish that Luke says His sweat was like drops of blood falling to the ground.

He woke them. The hour had come. Out of the darkness, Judas arrived with a crowd carrying swords and clubs, sent by the chief priests and elders. Judas had arranged a signal. Whoever I kiss, that is the man. He walked up to Jesus and said: greetings, Rabbi, and kissed him. Jesus said: do what you came to do. Peter drew a sword and cut off the ear of the high priest's servant. Jesus touched the man's ear and healed him. Then He let them take Him.

His disciples fled. He was taken to the high priest, then to Pilate the Roman governor, then to Herod, then back to Pilate. At each stop the verdict was the same from those who examined Him. I find no basis for a charge against this man. But the crowd demanded crucifixion, and Pilate, afraid of a riot, handed Him over.

The soldiers mocked Him. A crown of thorns pressed into His head, a purple robe thrown over His shoulders, a reed put in His hand as a scepter. They knelt in front of Him in mock worship and said: hail, king of the Jews! Then they beat Him. Then they led Him out.

He carried His cross toward a place called Golgotha, the Place of the Skull. He was too weak from the beatings to carry it the whole way, and a man named Simon of Cyrene was pulled from the crowd and made to carry it the rest of the distance. At nine in the morning, they nailed Him to the cross. Above His head Pilate had posted a sign: Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews.

Two criminals were crucified alongside Him, one on each side. The people walking by hurled insults. The chief priests and teachers of the law mocked Him. One of the criminals joined in. The other said: don't you fear God? We are getting what we deserve, but this man has done nothing wrong. Then he turned to Jesus and said: remember me when you come into your kingdom. Jesus said: truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise.

At noon, darkness covered the whole land for three hours. At three in the afternoon, Jesus cried out in a loud voice: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? Then He said: it is finished. He bowed His head and gave up His spirit.

At that moment the curtain of the Temple was torn in two from top to bottom. The curtain that had separated the Holy of Holies, the presence of God, from the rest of the world for a thousand years. Torn from top to bottom, not the way a human hand tears, but from the top down. The way God tears things. The way of access had been opened.

A Roman centurion who had watched the whole thing said: surely this man was the Son of God. They buried Him before sunset in a borrowed tomb, with a large stone rolled across the entrance and a guard posted outside. It looked, by every measure, like an ending.

devotional.bigIdea: It is finished. Three words in English. One word in Greek, tetelestai. The same word written across a paid debt in the ancient world. Paid in full. Everything that stood between us and God was finished at the cross.
questions: In the garden, Jesus prayed not as I will but as you will, and then submitted to the worst night of His life. What does that kind of surrender look like, and what did it cost Him?
The curtain in the Temple was torn from top to bottom when Jesus died. What do you think that meant, and what does it mean for you today?
The criminal on the cross next to Jesus had done nothing right in his life. Jesus told him today you will be with me in paradise. What does that tell you about what it takes to be with Jesus?

connections.title: A Promise as Big as the Stars
connections.why: Jesus carried wood up a hill, just as Isaac carried wood up Mount Moriah. Abraham said God himself will provide the lamb. At Golgotha, He did.

connections.title: The Scarlet Thread
connections.why: The scarlet cord in Rahab's window pointed to this, without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness. The blood of Jesus is the fulfillment of every scarlet thread in the Old Testament.

connections.title: The Empty Tomb
connections.why: It is finished, tetelestai, was not a cry of defeat. It was a declaration of completion. The resurrection three days later proved it. What Jesus finished at the cross, the empty tomb confirmed.

quiz.question: What did Jesus pray in the Garden of Gethsemane before He was arrested?
options: Father, I am ready, let your will be done quickly
Father, give me strength to face what is coming
Father, if it is possible, let this cup pass, yet not as I will, but as you will
quiz.explanation: Jesus prayed if it is possible let this cup pass from me, then submitted: yet not as I will but as you will. He prayed this three times, in such anguish that His sweat was like drops of blood. Then He went willingly.

quiz.question: What happened to the Temple curtain when Jesus died?
options: It fell from its frame and collapsed to the ground
It was torn in two from top to bottom, the way God tears things, not human hands
It caught fire and burned completely
quiz.explanation: The curtain that had separated the Holy of Holies from the world for a thousand years was torn from top to bottom, not from the bottom up the way a human would tear it. From the top down. God opened the way.

quiz.question: What did the criminal crucified next to Jesus ask of Him?
options: Remember me when you come into your kingdom
Ask God to forgive me for all my sins so I can go to heaven
Take me down from this cross and I will follow you forever
quiz.explanation: The criminal said simply: remember me when you come into your kingdom. He had nothing to offer, no time to change, no good works to point to. Jesus said today you will be with me in paradise. That is all it takes.

quiz.question: What did the sign above Jesus's cross say, and in how many languages?
options: King of the Jews, in Latin only, the language of Rome
This is Jesus, in Greek and Aramaic
The Messiah, in Hebrew and Latin
Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews, in Hebrew, Latin, and Greek
quiz.explanation: The sign was written in Hebrew, Latin, and Greek, the three great languages of the ancient world. The whole world could read the proclamation: this is the King.

quiz.question: What happened to Jesus's clothes at the crucifixion that fulfilled an ancient prophecy?
options: His clothes were returned to His mother Mary
A Roman officer kept them as a trophy of the execution
The soldiers divided his garments and cast lots for his tunic
His robe was burned as part of the execution ritual
quiz.explanation: The soldiers divided His garments and cast lots for His seamless tunic, fulfilling Psalm 22:18 written a thousand years before the cross.

stories.title: The Empty Tomb
stories.hook: They buried Him on Friday. Sunday morning, the tomb was empty and the stone was moved, and then He appeared.
story: He was buried before sunset on Friday because the Jewish Sabbath began at sundown and no work could be done. A man named Joseph of Arimathea, a member of the council who had not consented to their decision, had gone to Pilate and asked for the body. He wrapped it in linen and placed it in his own new tomb, cut from rock, and rolled a large stone across the entrance. The women who had come from Galilee followed and watched where He was laid. They went home and rested on the Sabbath.

On Sunday morning, the first day of the week, before dawn, while it was still dark, Mary Magdalene came to the tomb. She saw the stone had been moved. She ran immediately, without looking inside, to find Peter and John. She said: they have taken the Lord out of the tomb, and we do not know where they have put Him! Peter and John ran. John outran Peter and arrived first. He bent down and looked at the linen cloths lying there but did not go in. Peter arrived and went straight in. The linen cloths were lying there, and the cloth that had been wrapped around Jesus's head was folded up by itself, separate from the linen.
Then John went in. He saw, and he believed. They still did not fully understand the Scripture that He had to rise from the dead. But John believed. They went back to their homes.

Mary stood outside the tomb weeping. She bent over to look inside and saw two angels in white, seated where Jesus's body had been, one at the head, one at the foot. Why are you weeping? they asked. She turned around and saw Jesus standing there but did not recognize Him. She thought He was the gardener. Sir, she said, if you have carried Him away, tell me where you have put Him and I will get Him. Jesus said: Mary. One word. Her name. And she turned and said to Him in Aramaic: Rabboni, which means Teacher.

That same evening, the disciples were hiding behind locked doors. Jesus came and stood among them and said: peace be with you. He showed them His hands and His side. The disciples were overjoyed when they saw the Lord. Then He said again: peace be with you. As the Father has sent me, I am sending you. He breathed on them and said: receive the Holy Spirit.

Thomas was not there. When the other disciples told him they had seen the Lord, he said: unless I see the nail marks in His hands and put my finger where the nails were and put my hand into His side, I will not believe. Eight days later Jesus appeared again. He said to Thomas: put your finger here. See my hands. Reach out your hand and put it into my side. Stop doubting and believe. Thomas said: my Lord and my God. Jesus said: blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.

Later, at dawn, on the shore of the Sea of Galilee, Jesus made breakfast on the beach for seven of the disciples who had been fishing all night and caught nothing. After they ate, He spoke to Peter, the one who had denied Him three times the night of the arrest. Three times Jesus asked: do you love me? Three times Peter said yes. Three times Jesus said: feed my sheep. The denial that had broken Peter was met with three questions, not three accusations. Restoration, not condemnation.

He appeared to them over forty days, speaking about the kingdom of God. Paul would later write that He appeared to more than five hundred people at one time, most of whom were still alive when Paul wrote those words, still available to be questioned, still testifying to what they had seen. Then He led them out to Bethany, lifted His hands, and blessed them. And while He was blessing them, He was taken up into heaven. They worshipped Him and returned to Jerusalem with great joy. Every word He had said was true.

devotional.bigIdea: The resurrection is not a metaphor, a feeling, or a legend. It is the most documented event of the ancient world, witnessed by hundreds of people who went to their deaths insisting it was true. Everything Jesus claimed rests on whether He walked out of that tomb.
questions: Mary didn't recognize Jesus until He said her name. Have you ever had a moment where you suddenly recognized God in something you had been looking at all along?
Thomas said he wouldn't believe without seeing. Jesus showed up eight days later and gave Thomas exactly what he had asked for. What does that tell you about how Jesus responds to honest doubt?
Peter had denied Jesus three times. Jesus asked him three times do you love me, one for each denial. What does that pattern of restoration tell you about how Jesus handles our failures?

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Jesus showed the disciples His hands and His side, the wounds from the cross. The resurrection did not erase the cross. It vindicated it. The risen Jesus still bears the marks.

connections.title: Come Out
connections.why: Lazarus was raised to die again. Jesus was raised never to die again, the firstfruits of a new creation. The raising of Lazarus was a preview. The resurrection of Jesus is the main event.

connections.title: The Day Everything Changed
connections.why: Jesus appeared over forty days after the resurrection, teaching about the kingdom of God, preparing the disciples for Pentecost, when everything He had promised about the Holy Spirit would be poured out.

quiz.question: Who was the first person to see Jesus after the resurrection?
options: Peter, who ran to the tomb first
Mary Magdalene, who recognized Him when He said her name
John, who believed when he saw the empty grave cloths
quiz.explanation: Mary Magdalene was the first. She had gone to the tomb before dawn, found it empty, and stayed weeping after Peter and John left. She thought Jesus was the gardener, until He said her name. Then she knew.

quiz.question: What did Thomas say he would need before he would believe the others had seen Jesus?
options: To see the nail marks and put his hand in Jesus's side
To speak with at least three other witnesses himself
To hear Jesus's voice speaking directly to him
quiz.explanation: Thomas said unless I see the nail marks and put my finger where the nails were and my hand into His side, I will not believe. Eight days later Jesus appeared and offered Thomas exactly what he had asked for.

quiz.question: How many times did Jesus ask Peter do you love me on the beach?
options: Once, because Peter had confessed his denial already
Seven times, because that is the number of completeness
Three times, once for each time Peter had denied Him
quiz.explanation: Jesus asked three times, one question for each of Peter's three denials. It was not accusation. It was restoration. Three denials met with three questions and three commissions: feed my sheep.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say to the disciples when He appeared to them behind locked doors?
options: Do not be afraid, it is truly me, I am not a ghost
Why did you abandon me when I needed you most?
Peace be with you, and then showed them His hands and side
Go and tell everyone what you have seen
quiz.explanation: Jesus's first words to His frightened, hiding disciples were "Peace be with you." Not accusation. Not rebuke. Peace. Then He showed them His wounds so they could believe.

quiz.question: What did Jesus do on the beach before speaking to the disciples about Peter's calling?
options: He walked on the water to show He had truly risen
He cooked breakfast for them, bread and fish on a charcoal fire
He washed their feet again as He had at the Last Supper
He prayed with them as the sun rose
quiz.explanation: Jesus made breakfast, bread and fish on a charcoal fire. The same smell as the night Peter denied Him. Jesus met Peter in the ordinary before He restored him.

stories.title: The Stranger on the Road
stories.hook: Two disciples walked seven miles home after the crucifixion, certain everything was over. A stranger walked with them the whole way.
story: On the same day the tomb was found empty, two of Jesus's followers were walking to a village called Emmaus, about seven miles from Jerusalem. They were talking with each other about everything that had happened. Their faces were downcast. Three days ago they had watched Jesus die. That morning, some women had come back from the tomb saying it was empty and that angels had told them He was alive. The disciples did not know what to make of it.

As they walked and discussed, Jesus Himself came up and walked along with them. But they were kept from recognizing Him. He asked: what are you discussing together as you walk along? They stopped, their faces full of grief. One of them, named Cleopas, said: are you the only visitor to Jerusalem who does not know the things that have happened there in these days?

Jesus said: what things? They told Him about Jesus of Nazareth, a prophet powerful in word and deed. They told Him how the chief priests and rulers handed Him over to be sentenced to death and how He had been crucified. They said: we had hoped that He was the one who was going to redeem Israel. And it is now the third day since all this took place.

Then Jesus said: how foolish you are, and how slow to believe all that the prophets have spoken! Did not the Messiah have to suffer these things and then enter His glory? And beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, He explained to them what was said in all the Scriptures concerning Himself. For seven miles they walked and He opened the whole Bible to them, showing them how every part pointed to this moment.

As they approached the village, Jesus acted as if He were going farther. But they urged Him strongly: stay with us, for it is nearly evening and the day is almost over. So He went in to stay with them. When He was at the table with them, He took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and began to give it to them. And their eyes were opened and they recognized Him. And He disappeared from their sight.

They said to each other: were not our hearts burning within us while He talked with us on the road and opened the Scriptures to us? They got up and returned at once to Jerusalem, seven miles back in the dark, to tell the eleven what had happened. They found the eleven gathered together saying: the Lord has risen and has appeared to Simon! Then the two told them what had happened on the road and how Jesus was recognized in the breaking of the bread.

They had been kept from seeing Him until the moment He broke the bread. The same gesture He had made the night before. Take, give thanks, break, give. The hands that broke the bread were the same hands with the nail scars. And then He was gone, and they were on their feet running seven miles back into the dark to tell the others.

devotional.bigIdea: Two disciples walked seven miles without recognizing Jesus walking right next to them. He was there the whole time. They recognized Him in the breaking of the bread, the same thing He had done the night before He died. Jesus shows up in familiar things when we are looking somewhere else.
questions: They said we had hoped He was the one who would redeem Israel. Their hope was gone. Have you ever felt like something you trusted God for had fallen apart completely? What did that feel like?

They said were not our hearts burning within us while He talked with us on the road? They felt it before they understood it. Have you ever had a moment where you sensed God was near before you could explain why?
They recognized Jesus in the breaking of the bread, the same gesture from the night before. Why do you think Jesus chose that moment? What does it tell you about where He meets us?

connections.title: The Night Before
connections.why: Jesus took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and gave it to them. The exact same words, the exact same gesture as the last supper. Their eyes were opened in the repetition of something He had already done. He showed up in the familiar.

connections.title: The Empty Tomb
connections.why: The Emmaus road happened the same day the tomb was found empty. These two disciples were walking away from Jerusalem not yet knowing what the eleven had already seen. The risen Jesus met them on the way out and turned them back around.

connections.title: The Servant Who Suffers
connections.why: Beginning with Moses and all the Prophets He explained what was said in all the Scriptures concerning Himself. Isaiah 53 was in that conversation. The suffering servant was the key to understanding everything that had happened.

quiz.question: Where were the two disciples going when Jesus joined them on the road?
options: To the high priest's house to demand an explanation for the crucifixion
To the tomb to see for themselves whether it was really empty
Back to Galilee where they had first met Jesus and felt safe
To a village called Emmaus about seven miles from Jerusalem
quiz.explanation: They were walking to Emmaus, about seven miles from Jerusalem. They were leaving. They had hoped Jesus was the one to redeem Israel, and now He was dead.

quiz.question: Why did the disciples not recognize Jesus when He joined them?
options: Because they were looking at the ground and never looked at His face
Because His resurrection body looked different from His earthly body
Because they were kept from recognizing Him
Because it was getting dark and they could not see Him clearly
quiz.explanation: Luke says they were kept from recognizing Him. This was not accidental. Jesus chose to reveal Himself in a specific moment, not at the beginning of the conversation.

quiz.question: What did the disciples say they had hoped about Jesus before He was crucified?
options: They had hoped He was the one who was going to redeem Israel
They had hoped He would gather an army and drive out the Roman occupation
They had hoped He would become the high priest and reform the Temple system
They had hoped He would perform a miracle that would make the whole nation believe in Him
quiz.explanation: They said we had hoped He was the one who was going to redeem Israel. Their hope was past tense. It was gone. They were walking away carrying grief.

quiz.question: What did Jesus do that caused their eyes to be opened and recognize Him?
options: He spoke their names the way He always had, with a tone nobody else had
He took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and gave it to them
He showed them the scars on His hands from the nails
He quoted the exact words He had said to them before the crucifixion
quiz.explanation: When He took bread, gave thanks, broke it, and gave it to them, their eyes were opened. The same gesture from the last supper. Then He disappeared. They recognized the risen Jesus in the breaking of the bread.

quiz.question: What did the disciples do immediately after recognizing Jesus?
options: They stayed in Emmaus until morning because it was too dangerous to travel at night
They went to find the women who had first reported the empty tomb
They sat quietly at the table and prayed together, overwhelmed by what had happened
They got up at once and walked seven miles back to Jerusalem in the dark to tell the others
quiz.explanation: They got up at once and returned to Jerusalem, seven miles in the dark. They could not wait until morning. They had seen the risen Lord and the eleven needed to know.

stories.title: The Day Everything Changed
stories.hook: Fifty days after Passover, the disciples were together in a room when wind filled the house and fire appeared above their heads.
story: Jesus had told His disciples to wait in Jerusalem. He had promised them something. You will receive power when the Holy Spirit comes on you, and you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the ends of the earth. Then He was taken up, and a cloud hid Him from their sight, and He was gone. They went back to Jerusalem and waited. There were about a hundred and twenty of them.

Ten days later, on the day of Pentecost, a Jewish festival that fell fifty days after Passover, they were all together in one place. Suddenly a sound like a violent rushing wind came from heaven and filled the whole house. What looked like tongues of fire appeared and separated, resting on each of them. And they were all filled with the Holy Spirit and began to speak in other languages as the Spirit enabled them.

Jerusalem was full of Jewish pilgrims who had come for the festival from every nation under heaven. The sound brought a crowd together, bewildered, because each one heard them speaking in his own native language. They were utterly amazed. How is it that each of us hears them in our own language? They were declaring the wonders of God, and every person in the crowd was hearing it in the language of their homeland.

Peter stood up. The man who had denied Jesus three times around a charcoal fire six weeks earlier now stood before thousands of people and preached. He quoted the prophet Joel. In the last days, God says, I will pour out my Spirit on all people. Your sons and daughters will prophesy. Then he preached Jesus. His life, His death, His resurrection. He said: God has made this Jesus, whom you crucified, both Lord and Messiah.

When the people heard this, they were cut to the heart and said to Peter and the other apostles: brothers, what shall we do? Peter said: repent and be baptized, every one of you, in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins. And you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit. The promise is for you and your children and for all who are far off.

That day, about three thousand people were added to their number. And they devoted themselves to the apostles' teaching and to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer. They sold possessions to give to anyone who had need. They met together in the Temple courts every day, broke bread in their homes, and ate together with glad and sincere hearts. And the Lord added to their number daily those who were being saved.

What Babel had broken apart, Pentecost put back together. At Babel, God confused the languages and scattered humanity. At Pentecost, the Spirit enabled every language to hear the same message, the wonders of God, and three thousand people from every nation became one family. The reversal was complete. The promise to Abraham, through you all nations will be blessed, had arrived.

devotional.bigIdea: Pentecost is Babel in reverse. What human pride tore apart, the Spirit of God put back together. God has always been working toward one family, from every nation, gathered around one Name.
questions: The disciples waited ten days between the ascension and Pentecost. They didn't know when it was coming, they just kept waiting together. What does faithful waiting look like in your own life?
Peter, the man who had denied Jesus three times, preached to thousands six weeks later. What changed him? What do you think made that kind of transformation possible?
Pentecost reversed Babel, every language heard the same message. What does it mean to you that the gospel was designed from the beginning to reach every nation, every language, every people?

connections.title: The Tower of Babel
connections.why: At Babel God confused languages and scattered people. At Pentecost the Spirit enabled every language to hear the same message. Pentecost is the direct reversal of Babel, what pride broke, grace put back together.

connections.title: The Empty Tomb
connections.why: Peter says Jesus has been exalted to the right hand of God and has received from the Father the promised Holy Spirit and has poured out what you now see and hear. Pentecost is the direct fruit of the resurrection.

connections.title: A Promise as Big as the Stars
connections.why: Peter says the promise is for you and your children and for all who are far off. The promise God made to Abraham, all nations will be blessed through you, is being fulfilled in real time at Pentecost.

quiz.question: What happened when the Holy Spirit fell on the disciples at Pentecost?
options: They fell to the ground and could not speak for three days
They were transported to the Temple and began healing the sick
A sound like rushing wind filled the house, tongues of fire appeared, and they spoke in other languages
quiz.explanation: A violent rushing wind filled the house. Tongues of fire rested on each of them. They were filled with the Holy Spirit and spoke in other languages, and every person in the crowd heard them in their own native tongue.

quiz.question: What Old Testament event does Pentecost directly reverse?
options: The destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah
The golden calf where Israel worshipped an idol in the wilderness
The Tower of Babel where God confused human language and scattered people
quiz.explanation: At Babel, God confused languages to scatter people who were building for their own glory. At Pentecost, the Spirit enabled every language to hear the same message. What pride broke apart, grace put back together.

quiz.question: How many people were added to the church on the day of Pentecost?
options: About three hundred people
About three thousand people
About thirty thousand people
quiz.explanation: About three thousand people were added that day, from every nation under heaven, all hearing the message in their own language. The church went from 120 people to over 3,000 in one afternoon.

quiz.question: What had Jesus told His disciples to do before the Holy Spirit came?
options: Wait in Jerusalem until they received power from on high
Go immediately to all the nations and preach the gospel
Choose a replacement for Judas and then begin their mission
Baptize as many people as possible before He returned
quiz.explanation: Jesus commanded them to wait in Jerusalem until they were clothed with power from on high. The church's first act was not action but waiting on God.

quiz.question: What was the feast of Pentecost, and why was it significant that the Spirit came on that day?
options: It was the feast of Tabernacles, remembering when Israel lived in the wilderness
It was the feast of Passover, celebrating Israel's freedom from Egypt
It was a new moon festival with no harvest significance
It was a harvest festival celebrating the first fruits of the grain harvest, now the first fruits of the church were gathered in
quiz.explanation: Pentecost was a harvest festival of first fruits, and on that day the first great harvest of souls was brought in. Three thousand people. God's timing is never accidental.

stories.title: The First Martyr
stories.hook: Stephen stood before the council that had killed Jesus and told them the whole story of Israel. It was the last thing he ever said.
story: The early church was growing rapidly in Jerusalem, and a man named Stephen was full of faith and of the Holy Spirit. He was chosen to serve the practical needs of the community so the apostles could focus on prayer and teaching. But Stephen also spoke with such wisdom and power that those who argued with him could not stand against him.

His opponents stirred up opposition against him, bringing false witnesses who claimed he had spoken against the Temple and against Moses. They seized him and brought him before the Sanhedrin, the same council that had condemned Jesus. As Stephen stood before them, all who sat in the council saw that his face was like the face of an angel.

The high priest asked: are these charges true? Stephen answered with the longest speech in the book of Acts. He told the whole story of Israel: Abraham leaving his homeland, Joseph sold into slavery and rising to save his family, Moses and the burning bush, the exodus, the wilderness, the tabernacle, Solomon's Temple. He told it all, and at every turn he showed that Israel had always resisted the messengers God sent to them.

He said: you stiff-necked people, you always resist the Holy Spirit. Was there ever a prophet your ancestors did not persecute? They even killed those who predicted the coming of the Righteous One. And now you have betrayed and murdered him.

When they heard this, they were furious and gnashed their teeth at him. But Stephen, full of the Holy Spirit, looked up to heaven and said: look, I see heaven open and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God. They covered their ears. They rushed at him, dragged him out of the city, and began to stone him. The witnesses laid their cloaks at the feet of a young man named Saul, who approved of what was happening.

While they were stoning him, Stephen prayed: Lord Jesus, receive my spirit. Then he fell on his knees and cried out: Lord, do not hold this sin against them. And when he had said this, he fell asleep.

Stephen died saying the same things Jesus had said from the cross: receive my spirit. Forgive them. He had not just preached about Jesus. He had died like Him. And the young man named Saul who stood watching over the cloaks would later say that the witness of Stephen had never left him. The blood of the martyr became the seed of the missionary.

devotional.bigIdea: Stephen stood in front of the people who had killed Jesus and told them the truth. Then he prayed for them while they were killing him. He did not die angry. He died the way Jesus died, forgiving. The way we face the worst moments reveals what we actually believe.
questions: Stephen asked God not to hold his killers' sin against them while they were still throwing stones at him. What does that kind of prayer require? Where does that kind of love come from?
Stephen saw heaven open while he was being stoned. What do you think it meant for him to see that in that moment? How do you think it changed what he was experiencing?
Saul was standing there watching and later said Stephen's witness never left him. Have you ever seen someone else's faith or courage change the way you thought about God? What happened?

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Jesus said from the cross: Father, into your hands I commit my spirit. Father, forgive them. Stephen said: Lord Jesus, receive my spirit. Lord, do not hold this sin against them. He did not just preach about the cross. He reproduced it.

connections.title: The Man Who Hunted Christians
connections.why: Saul stood watching the cloaks of those who stoned Stephen. Years later Paul said Stephen's death had never left him. The first Christian martyr planted something in the heart of the man who would become the greatest missionary.

connections.title: Singing at Midnight
connections.why: Stephen saw heaven open while being stoned. Paul sang hymns while chained in a Roman prison. Both men found a peace that made no sense given their circumstances because both had encountered the same risen Jesus.

quiz.question: What was Stephen chosen to do in the early church before he began preaching?
options: To serve the practical needs of the community so the apostles could focus on prayer and teaching
To lead the apostles in daily prayer and fasting
To manage the funds donated by new believers and distribute them fairly
To teach in the synagogues throughout Jerusalem every Sabbath
quiz.explanation: Stephen was chosen to serve practical needs, to make sure the community was cared for. But he was also full of faith and the Holy Spirit and began speaking with such power that people could not argue against him.

quiz.question: What did all who sat in the council see when Stephen stood before them?
options: Anger that made them understand why he had been causing trouble
That his face was like the face of an angel
Fear in his eyes that he was trying to hide with confident words
Tears running down his face because he knew what was about to happen
quiz.explanation: As Stephen stood before the Sanhedrin, all who sat in the council saw that his face was like the face of an angel. Whatever he was about to say, he was not afraid.

quiz.question: What did Stephen tell the council when they asked if the charges against him were true?
options: He asked permission to pray before answering and then spoke about the resurrection
He said nothing and let his face speak for itself
He denied all the charges and asked for witnesses to come forward to defend him
He told the whole story of Israel from Abraham to Jesus, showing that Israel had always rejected God's messengers
quiz.explanation: Stephen told the whole story of Israel from Abraham through Moses through the Temple to Jesus, showing at every point how Israel had resisted and rejected the messengers God sent, ending with you betrayed and murdered the Righteous One.

quiz.question: What did Stephen see just before he was dragged out to be stoned?
options: A vision of the disciples running toward the council to rescue him
A light from heaven like the one that had appeared on the day of Pentecost
Heaven open and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God
An angel standing in the room who told him not to be afraid
quiz.explanation: Stephen looked up to heaven and said: look, I see heaven open and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God. The council covered their ears and rushed at him.

quiz.question: What were Stephen's last words before he died?
options: Lord Jesus receive my spirit, and Lord do not hold this sin against them
I go to be with the Lord and I will pray for you from His presence
I forgive you all and I pray that God will open your eyes to the truth
The Righteous One whom you killed is alive and I am going to see Him now
quiz.explanation: Stephen prayed: Lord Jesus, receive my spirit. Then he fell on his knees and cried out: Lord, do not hold this sin against them. Then he fell asleep. He died forgiving, just as Jesus had.

stories.title: The Man Who Hunted Christians
stories.hook: He was on his way to arrest Christians when a blinding light knocked him down and God spoke his name.
story: Saul of Tarsus was a zealot in the truest sense. A Pharisee trained under Gamaliel, the greatest rabbi of the generation, he was by his own description advancing in Judaism beyond many of his peers, extremely zealous for the traditions of his fathers. When the followers of Jesus began spreading through Jerusalem after Pentecost, Saul considered them a threat to everything he held sacred. He did not just disagree with them. He hunted them.

He was there when Stephen, the first martyr, was stoned to death outside Jerusalem. He did not throw the stones himself, but he stood and watched and gave his approval, holding the cloaks of those who did. After Stephen's death, a great persecution broke out against the church in Jerusalem. Saul went house to house, dragging off men and women and putting them in prison. Believers scattered throughout Judea and Samaria, which was, without anyone intending it, exactly how the gospel began spreading to the regions Jesus had named at the ascension.

Saul was not finished. He went to the high priest and asked for letters to the synagogues in Damascus, about 150 miles north of Jerusalem, authorizing him to find any followers of Jesus there and bring them as prisoners to Jerusalem. He set out with a company of men. He was on his way to arrest more Christians.

As he traveled and approached Damascus, suddenly a light from heaven flashed around him. He fell to the ground and heard a voice: Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me? He said: who are you, Lord? And the voice said: I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting. Now get up and go into the city, and you will be told what you must do. The men with Saul stood speechless. They heard the sound but saw no one.

Saul got up from the ground. When he opened his eyes he could see nothing. They led him by the hand into Damascus. For three days he was blind and did not eat or drink. He had been on his way to arrest Christians. He arrived in Damascus unable to see, depending on others to lead him, waiting for instructions from the Jesus he had been persecuting.

A disciple in Damascus named Ananias received a vision. Go to the street called Straight and ask for Saul of Tarsus. Ananias answered with understandable concern. Lord, I have heard about this man and all the harm he has done to your people in Jerusalem. He has come here with authority to arrest all who call on your name. The Lord said: go. This man is my chosen instrument to carry my name before the Gentiles and their kings and before the people of Israel.

Ananias went. He placed his hands on Saul and said: Brother Saul, the Lord Jesus who appeared to you on the road has sent me so that you may see again and be filled with the Holy Spirit. Immediately something like scales fell from Saul's eyes and he could see again. He got up and was baptized. He ate and his strength returned.

The man who had been the greatest enemy of the church became its greatest missionary. He took a new name, Paul, and spent the rest of his life traveling across the Roman Empire, planting churches, writing letters that would become Scripture, and suffering everything he had once inflicted on others. He was beaten, shipwrecked, imprisoned, and finally executed in Rome. He said later: I am the worst of sinners. But for that very reason I was shown mercy, so that in me Christ might display His immense patience as an example for those who would believe.

devotional.bigIdea: Paul said he was shown mercy so that in him Christ might display His immense patience as an example. The worst sinner Paul knew was himself, and God chose him on purpose, to show what grace can do with a person who has done everything wrong.
questions: Saul was absolutely certain he was doing the right thing when he was hunting Christians. What does that tell you about the danger of being sincerely wrong?
Ananias was asked to go help the man who had come to arrest him. He went anyway. Is there someone in your life who is hard to extend grace toward? What would it look like to go to them anyway?
Paul said he was the worst of sinners, and that God chose him on purpose to show what grace can do. What does that mean for anyone who feels like they have gone too far for God to use?

connections.title: The Day Everything Changed
connections.why: The persecution that Saul launched after Stephen's death scattered the believers, and that scattering carried the gospel to Judea, Samaria, and beyond. Saul tried to stop the church and accidentally accelerated it.

connections.title: The Darkest Friday
connections.why: Paul said Jesus came to save sinners, of whom I am the worst. The cross was for exactly this, the worst cases, the most hopeless situations, the people furthest from God. Especially those.

connections.title: Singing at Midnight
connections.why: Jesus told Ananias that Paul would suffer for the name of Jesus. From the moment of his conversion, Paul knew the cost. Singing at midnight in a Philippian prison was not a surprise, it was the life he had been told to expect.

quiz.question: What was Saul doing when the light from heaven knocked him to the ground?
options: Traveling to Damascus to arrest Christians and bring them back as prisoners
Overseeing a debate between Pharisees and followers of Jesus
Preaching in a synagogue in Jerusalem against the followers of Jesus
quiz.explanation: Saul was on his way to Damascus with authorization from the high priest to find followers of Jesus and bring them back as prisoners to Jerusalem. He was on his way to arrest more Christians when Jesus stopped him.

quiz.question: What happened to Saul immediately after he encountered Jesus on the road?
options: He was paralyzed and could not move until Ananias came to him
He was struck deaf and could not hear anything for three days
He was struck blind and had to be led by hand into Damascus
quiz.explanation: Saul was struck blind. They led him by the hand into Damascus, where he stayed for three days without sight, without food, without water, waiting for the instructions Jesus had promised.

quiz.question: What did Jesus say to Ananias about why He had chosen Saul?
options: Because Saul was the most educated man available to carry the gospel
Because Saul's transformation would be the greatest miracle of the early church
Because Saul was His chosen instrument to carry His name before the Gentiles and kings and Israel
quiz.explanation: Jesus told Ananias: this man is my chosen instrument to carry my name before the Gentiles and their kings and before the people of Israel. God had already decided what Saul was for, before Saul knew it himself.
