quiz.question: What did the risen Jesus say to Saul when he demanded to know who was speaking?
options: I am the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the one your ancestors followed
I am Jesus whom you are persecuting
I am the one you have been looking for all your life
I am the resurrection and the life, why do you fight against me?
quiz.explanation: "I am Jesus whom you are persecuting", not "I am God" or "I am the Messiah." Jesus made it personal. To persecute His people was to persecute Him.

quiz.question: Who was the disciple God sent to restore Saul's sight and welcome him into the church?
options: Ananias, an ordinary disciple who was terrified of Saul but obeyed God anyway
Barnabas, who later traveled with Paul on his missions
Stephen's brother, who wanted to see if God had truly changed Saul's heart
Peter, who was the leader of the apostles
quiz.explanation: Ananias, an ordinary disciple, not a famous apostle, was the one God chose. He was afraid of Saul, said so honestly to God, and then obeyed anyway. That's courage.

stories.title: Singing at Midnight
stories.hook: Paul and Silas were beaten, thrown in the deepest part of a prison, and chained to the wall. Then they started singing.
story: Paul and his companion Silas were in Philippi, a major Roman city in Macedonia. Paul had cast out a spirit from a slave girl who had been earning money for her owners by fortune-telling. Her owners, furious that their income was gone, dragged Paul and Silas before the magistrates and accused them of throwing the city into an uproar. The crowd joined the attack. The magistrates ordered them stripped, beaten with rods, and thrown into prison. The jailer was commanded to guard them carefully. He threw them into the inner cell, the most secure part of the prison, and fastened their feet in the stocks.

It was midnight. Paul and Silas had been beaten. Their backs were raw. They were in the darkest cell of a Roman prison with their feet locked. And they were praying and singing hymns to God. The other prisoners were listening.

Suddenly there was a violent earthquake. The foundations of the prison shook. All the prison doors flew open. Everybody's chains came loose. The jailer woke up, saw the prison doors open, and drew his sword to kill himself. A Roman jailer whose prisoners escaped faced execution anyway, and suicide was considered more honorable. Paul shouted from inside the dark: do not harm yourself! We are all here!

The jailer called for lights, rushed in, and fell trembling before Paul and Silas. He brought them out and asked: sirs, what must I do to be saved? They said: believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved, you and your household. They spoke the word of God to him and to everyone in his house. That same night, at that same hour, the jailer washed their wounds. And he and all his household were baptized immediately. He brought them into his house, set a meal before them, and was filled with joy, because he had come to believe in God.

The next morning the magistrates sent officers to release Paul and Silas. Paul said: they beat us publicly without a trial, Roman citizens, and threw us into prison. And now they want to release us quietly? No. Let them come themselves and escort us out. The magistrates were alarmed when they heard they were Roman citizens. Beating a Roman citizen without trial was a serious offense. They came and apologized and escorted them out of the city.

Midnight in a Roman prison with a raw back is not the setting you would choose for a worship service. But that is where Paul and Silas chose to sing. Not because the circumstances were good. Not because they felt like it. Because God was worth singing to in the dark as much as in the light, and more, because the dark is where the song is most needed and most surprising.

The prisoners were listening. The jailer was watching. A family came to faith. It always works this way. Faithfulness in the worst moments reaches people that nothing else can touch. The song in the dark is the one nobody forgets.

devotional.bigIdea: Paul and Silas sang at midnight in a Roman prison with raw backs and locked feet, not because things were good, but because God was worth it in the dark as much as in the light. The song in the darkest moment is the one nobody forgets.
questions: Paul and Silas prayed and sang at midnight, not in the morning when they knew they had survived, but in the actual dark, in the actual pain. What do you think made that possible?
The other prisoners were listening. The jailer was watching. Their faithfulness in suffering reached people that nothing else could have reached. Who is watching how you handle your hard moments?
The jailer asked what must I do to be saved, right in the middle of the worst night of his life. Why do you think people often come to God in the hardest moments rather than the comfortable ones?

connections.title: The Man Who Hunted Christians
connections.why: Jesus told Ananias that Paul would suffer for the name of Jesus. Midnight in a Philippian prison is that promise being kept. Paul knew this was coming, and he sang anyway.

connections.title: The Lion's Den
connections.why: An earthquake opened the prison doors just as an angel shut the mouths of the lions. Both Daniel and Paul were imprisoned for their faith, both were rescued miraculously, and both rescues led directly to their captors turning to God.

connections.title: The Upside-Down Kingdom
connections.why: Jesus said blessed are those who are persecuted for righteousness, rejoice and be glad. Paul and Silas were not just enduring persecution. They were doing exactly what Jesus said: rejoicing in it. Singing it.

quiz.question: Why were Paul and Silas thrown into prison in Philippi?
options: They refused to offer a sacrifice to the Roman gods
They were caught teaching in the synagogue without permission
They cast out a spirit from a slave girl and her owners lost their source of income
quiz.explanation: Paul cast a spirit out of a slave girl who had been earning money for her owners by fortune-telling. Her furious owners dragged Paul and Silas before the magistrates, the crowd attacked, and they were beaten and imprisoned.

quiz.question: What were Paul and Silas doing at midnight in prison?
options: Praying and singing hymns to God while the other prisoners listened
Quietly planning how to escape through the window
Sleeping, they were exhausted from the beatings
quiz.explanation: At midnight, beaten, with feet in stocks, Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns. The other prisoners were listening. The song in the darkest moment is the one nobody forgets.

quiz.question: What did the jailer do immediately after he and his household believed?
options: He went to the magistrates to confess what had happened to Paul and Silas
He released all the prisoners and resigned from his position
He washed their wounds, had his whole household baptized, brought them to his house and fed them
quiz.explanation: The jailer washed Paul and Silas's wounds, cleaning the injuries he had indirectly caused, then had his whole household baptized, brought them into his home, set a meal before them, and was filled with joy. All in one night.

quiz.question: What happened at midnight that shook the entire prison?
options: The prisoners began shouting together and the walls collapsed
A violent earthquake shook the foundations and every door flew open and every chain fell off
An angel appeared in a blinding light that frightened all the guards away
A lightning bolt struck the prison wall and cracked it open
quiz.explanation: A violent earthquake, every foundation shook, every door opened, every chain fell. God's response to His servants singing hymns in a Roman prison was to shake the earth.

quiz.question: Why did the jailer draw his sword to kill himself when he woke up?
options: He had heard Paul preaching and was convicted of his sins
He was ashamed of how he had treated Paul and Silas
He thought the prisoners had escaped and he would be executed for failing his duty
He had been planning to leave his post anyway and saw this as his chance
quiz.explanation: Roman law required a jailer to pay with his own life if prisoners escaped. Seeing the open doors, he assumed the worst, and Paul's shout "We are all here!" stopped him.

stories.title: The Purple Merchant
stories.hook: Paul crossed into Europe looking for a synagogue. He found a group of women by a river. One of them changed history.
story: Paul received a vision in the night: a man from Macedonia standing and begging him, come over to Macedonia and help us. He and his companions immediately set sail. They were crossing from Asia into Europe, and they did not fully know what they were heading toward.

They came to Philippi, a Roman colony and the leading city of that district of Macedonia. On the Sabbath they went outside the city gate down to the river, where they expected to find a place of prayer. There was no synagogue in Philippi, which meant there were fewer than ten Jewish men in the city. But there was a group of women gathered by the river, and Paul sat down and began to speak to them.

One of the women listening was named Lydia, a dealer in purple cloth from the city of Thyatira who was a worshipper of God. Purple dye was one of the most expensive commodities in the ancient world, extracted from a sea snail one drop at a time. Only the wealthy could afford it. Only the connected could trade it. Lydia was a successful businesswoman in one of the most lucrative markets in the empire.

The Lord opened her heart to pay attention to what Paul was saying. It is a small sentence with enormous meaning. She was already a worshipper of God. She was already at the river on the Sabbath. She was already seeking. And God opened her heart to receive what Paul was bringing.

She was baptized, along with her household. Then she invited Paul and his companions to stay at her house. She said: if you consider me a believer in the Lord, come and stay at my home. And she persuaded them. The first church in Europe gathered in Lydia's house, the home of a businesswoman who traded in the color of royalty.

Paul's vision had been of a man from Macedonia. The person he found at the river was a woman. The place of prayer he expected was a synagogue. What he found was a group meeting outside the city. God had been more specific about the continent than about the details. He had simply said come and help. The helping looked different from what Paul had imagined.

Lydia's household became the base of operations for the mission to Europe. The letter Paul later wrote to the church in Philippi, one of the warmest letters he ever wrote, was written to people who had first gathered in her home. The gospel reached an empire through a woman who traded purple.

devotional.bigIdea: Paul looked for a synagogue and found a group of women by a river. Lydia was already seeking God, and God opened her heart to hear the next thing. The first church in Europe started in a businesswoman's house. God rarely works the way we plan, and He often works through people we did not expect to find.
questions: Paul received a vision of a man from Macedonia, but the person he found by the river was a woman. What does that tell you about the difference between following God's direction and controlling exactly how it unfolds?
The Lord opened Lydia's heart to pay attention to what Paul was saying. She was already seeking God. What does that suggest about the relationship between our seeking and God's opening?
The first church in Europe met in a businesswoman's home. What does that tell you about the kinds of people and places God chooses to do significant things through?

connections.title: The Woman at the Well
connections.why: The Lord opened Lydia's heart to pay attention to Paul. Jesus crossed into Samaria specifically to find one woman at a well. In both stories, God is actively seeking the person before the conversation even begins.

connections.title: Singing at Midnight
connections.why: Lydia's house became Paul's base in Philippi. The singing at midnight in the Philippian jail happened in the same city. Paul's letter to Philippi was written to the church that began in Lydia's living room.

connections.title: The Day Everything Changed
connections.why: At Pentecost the gospel went from a room in Jerusalem to three thousand people from every nation. Through Lydia the gospel crossed into Europe. The Spirit kept pushing outward, continent by continent, exactly as Jesus had said: to the ends of the earth.

quiz.question: What vision caused Paul to sail to Macedonia?
options: He saw a vision of a great harvest field that he understood to be Europe
Jesus appeared to him at night and told him to take the gospel westward
A man from Macedonia stood and begged him come over to Macedonia and help us
An angel appeared to him telling him there were many people ready to hear the gospel there
quiz.explanation: Paul saw a man from Macedonia standing and begging him to come over and help. He concluded that God was calling them to preach the gospel there, and they set sail immediately.

quiz.question: Why did Paul go to the river on the Sabbath instead of a synagogue?
options: He had been told the synagogue leaders were hostile to him and wanted to avoid them
The synagogue was on the other side of the city and too far to reach before Sabbath ended
He had received specific instructions in a dream to go to the river instead
There was no synagogue in Philippi because there were fewer than ten Jewish men in the city
quiz.explanation: A synagogue required ten Jewish men to be established. Philippi had none. So Paul went outside the city gate to the river, where he expected to find a place of prayer, and found women gathered there.

quiz.question: What was Lydia's occupation?
options: A keeper of an inn on the main road through Philippi
A dealer in purple cloth from the city of Thyatira
A merchant who traded in spices brought from the eastern trade routes
A weaver of linen cloth who supplied the local Roman garrison
quiz.explanation: Lydia was a dealer in purple cloth from Thyatira. Purple dye was one of the most expensive commodities in the ancient world. She was a wealthy, connected businesswoman in one of the most lucrative trades in the empire.

quiz.question: What did Luke say God did while Lydia was listening to Paul?
options: He opened her heart to pay attention to what Paul was saying
He sent an angel to stand beside Paul as a sign that his message was true
He gave her a vision confirming that Paul was a true apostle sent by God
He brought to her mind all the prophecies she had read that pointed to Jesus
quiz.explanation: The Lord opened her heart to pay attention to what Paul was saying. She was already a worshipper of God. God opened what was already seeking. Then she was baptized along with her household.

quiz.question: What did Lydia do after she was baptized?
options: She returned to Thyatira to tell her family and bring them back to Philippi
She donated her entire warehouse of purple cloth to support Paul's missionary journeys
She invited Paul and his companions to stay at her house, and the church met there
She went immediately into the city to tell the magistrates about her conversion
quiz.explanation: Lydia said if you consider me a believer in the Lord, come and stay at my home. She persuaded them. The first church in Europe gathered in her house. Paul's warmest letter was later written to that church.
